    author = "Fang, Wenji and Wang, Jing and Lu, Yao and Liu, Shang and Wu, Yuchao and Ma, Yuzhe and Xie, Zhiyao",
    title = "A Survey of Circuit Foundation Model: Foundation AI Models for VLSI Circuit Design and EDA",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2504.03711",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711"
}


Abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI)-driven electronic design automation (EDA) techniques have been extensively explored for VLSI circuit design applications. Most recently, foundation AI models for circuits have emerged as a new technology trend. Unlike traditional task-specific AI solutions, these new AI models are developed through two stages: 1) self-supervised pre-training on a large amount of unlabeled data to learn intrinsic circuit properties; and 2) efficient fine-tuning for specific downstream applications, such as early-stage design quality evaluation, circuit-related context generation, and functional verification. This new paradigm brings many advantages: model generalization, less reliance on labeled circuit data, efficient adaptation to new tasks, and unprecedented generative capability. In this paper, we propose referring to AI models developed with this new paradigm as circuit foundation models (CFMs). This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the latest progress in circuit foundation models, unprecedentedly covering over 130 relevant works. Over 90% of our introduced works were published in or after 2022, indicating that this emerging research trend has attracted wide attention in a short period. In this survey, we propose to categorize all existing circuit foundation models into two primary types: 1) encoder-based methods performing general circuit representation learning for predictive tasks; and 2) decoder-based methods leveraging large language models (LLMs) for generative tasks. For our introduced works, we cover their input modalities, model architecture, pre-training strategies, domain adaptation techniques, and downstream design applications. In addition, this paper discussed the unique properties of circuits from the data perspective. These circuit properties have motivated many works in this domain and differentiated them from general AI techniques.

## 9. AICircuit: A Multi-Level Dataset and Benchmark for AI-Driven Analog Integrated Circuit Design
Valid Text Names:
'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 2-4', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 4-6', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 7-8', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 8-10', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 10-13', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 13-15', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 15-17', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 17-18', 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 18-19'

    author = "Mehradfar, Asal and Zhao, Xuzhe and Niu, Yue and Babakniya, Sara and Alesheikh, Mahdi and Aghasi, Hamidreza and Avestimehr, Salman",
    title = "AICircuit: A Multi-Level Dataset and Benchmark for AI-Driven Analog Integrated Circuit Design",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2407.18272",
    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Analog and radio-frequency circuit design requires extensive exploration of both circuit topology and parameters to meet specific design criteria like power consumption and bandwidth. Designers must review state-of-the-art topology configurations in the literature and sweep various circuit parameters within each configuration. This design process is highly specialized and time-intensive, particularly as the number of circuit parameters increases and the circuit becomes more complex. Prior research has explored the potential of machine learning to enhance circuit design procedures. However, these studies primarily focus on simple circuits, overlooking the more practical and complex analog and radio-frequency systems. A major obstacle for bearing the power of machine learning in circuit design is the availability of a generic and diverse dataset, along with robust metrics, which are essential for thoroughly evaluating and improving machine learning algorithms in the analog and radio-frequency circuit domain. We present AICircuit, a comprehensive multi-level dataset and benchmark for developing and evaluating ML algorithms in analog and radio-frequency circuit design. AICircuit comprises seven commonly used basic circuits and two complex wireless transceiver systems composed of multiple circuit blocks, encompassing a wide array of design scenarios encountered in real-world applications. We extensively evaluate various ML algorithms on the dataset, revealing the potential of ML algorithms in learning the mapping from the design specifications to the desired circuit parameters.

## 10. AnalogFed: Federated Discovery of Analog Circuit Topologies with Generative AI
Valid Text Names:
'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 1-2', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 2-4', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 2-2', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 4-5', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 4-4', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 5-6', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 6-7', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 7-8', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 8-9', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 9-9'

    author = "Li, Qiufeng and Hong, Shu and Gao, Jian and Zhang, Xuan and Lan, Tian and Cao, Weidong",

    year = "2025",

    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.15104",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.15104"
}


Abstract:
Recent breakthroughs in AI/ML offer exciting opportunities to revolutionize analog design automation through data-driven approaches. In particular, researchers are increasingly fascinated by harnessing the power of generative AI to automate the discovery of novel analog circuit topologies. Unlocking the full potential of generative AI in these data-driven discoveries requires access to large and diverse datasets.Yet, there is a significant barrier in the analog domain--Analog circuit design is inherently proprietary, involving not only confidential circuit structures but also the underlying commercial semiconductor processes. As a result, current generative AI research is largely confined to individual researchers who construct small, narrowly focused private datasets. This fragmentation severely limits collaborative innovation and impedes progress across the research community. To address these challenges, we propose AnalogFed. AnalogFed enables collaborative topology discovery across decentralized clients (e.g., individual researchers or institutions) without requiring the sharing of raw private data. To make this vision practical, we introduce a suite of techniques tailored to the unique challenges of applying FedL in analog design--from generative model development and data heterogeneity handling to privacy-preserving strategies that ensure both flexibility and security for circuit designers and semiconductor manufacturers. Extensive experiments across varying client counts and dataset sizes demonstrate that AnalogFed achieves performance comparable to centralized baselines--while maintaining strict data privacy. Specifically, the generative AI model within AnalogFed achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and scalability in the design of analog circuit topologies.

## 12. AnalogXpert: Automating Analog Topology Synthesis by Incorporating Circuit Design Expertise into Large Language Models
Valid Text Names:
'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 1-2', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 4-5', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 5-6', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 6-7', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 7-9', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 10-13', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 13-17', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 17-21', 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 21-25'

}


Abstract:
Analog topology synthesis is one of the major challenges in analog design automation since the topology of analog circuits has a large design space and contains a lot of human expertise. Traditional methods suffer in generating high-quality topology due to the diversity of topologies and the lack of ability to understand human experience. Therefore, LLM has been adopted in recent studies to generate such topologies. However, most of the existing work utilizes ideal model-based generation or ambiguous design requirements, both of which are not in line with industrial practice and require additional effort. In this work, we propose AnalogXpert, an LLM-based agent formulating topology synthesis as subcircuit-level SPICE code generation which is more practical. AnalogXpert incorporates circuit design expertise by introducing a proofreading strategy that allows LLMs to incrementally correct the errors in the initial design. Finally, we construct a high-quality benchmark validated by both real data (30) and synthetic data (2k). AnalogXpert achieves 40% and 23% success rates on the synthetic dataset and real dataset respectively, which is markedly better than those of GPT-4o (3%,3%) and AnalogCoder (8%,6%).

Abstract:
Recent advances in machine learning (ML) for automating analog circuit synthesis have been significant, yet challenges remain. A critical gap is the lack of a standardized evaluation framework, compounded by various process design kits (PDKs), simulation tools, and a limited variety of circuit topologies. These factors hinder direct comparisons and the validation of algorithms. To address these shortcomings, we introduced AnalogGym, an open-source testing suite designed to provide fair and comprehensive evaluations. AnalogGym includes 30 circuit topologies in five categories: sensing front ends, voltage references, low dropout regulators, amplifiers, and phase-locked loops. It supports several technology nodes for academic and commercial applications and is compatible with commercial simulators such as Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, and the open-source simulator Ngspice. AnalogGym standardizes the assessment of ML algorithms in analog circuit synthesis and promotes reproducibility with its open datasets and detailed benchmark specifications. AnalogGym's user-friendly design allows researchers to easily adapt it for robust, transparent comparisons of state-of-the-art methods, while also exposing them to real-world industrial design challenges, enhancing the practical relevance of their work. Additionally, we have conducted a comprehensive comparison study of various analog sizing methods on AnalogGym, highlighting the capabilities and advantages of different approaches. AnalogGym is available in the GitHub repository1. The documentations are also available at2.

## 6. TRouter: Thermal-Driven PCB Routing via Nonlocal Crisscross Attention Networks

}


Abstract:
In this article, we propose TRouter, a thermal-driven printed circuit board (PCB) routing framework via a machine-learning model. The model is designed to capture the long-range spatial information from the PCB layout and predict thermal distribution. The information contains pads, vias, components and wire segments. A gradient in each grid cell obtained from the backpropagation is integrated into a full-board routing algorithm to guide thermal-aware wire detour and via punching. To achieve a significant speedup, we construct a conflict graph according to whether overlapping among convex hulls of nets. A greedy-based method is adopted to remove nonroot nodes from all nodes. Then, a task graph is constructed to improve the parallelism. We conduct experiments on open-source benchmarks to illustrate our TRouter can achieve significant speedup and lower-temperature designs, compared with a state-of-the-art PCB routing algorithm.

## 7. New Interaction Paradigm for Complex EDA Software Leveraging GPT
Valid Text Names:
'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 1-2', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 2-3', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 3-4', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 4-6', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 6-8', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 8-9'

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2307.14740",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.14740",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
In the rapidly growing field of electronic design automation (EDA), professional software such as KiCad, Cadence , and Altium Designer provide increasingly extensive design functionalities. However, the intricate command structure and high learning curve create a barrier, particularly for novice printed circuit board (PCB) designers. This results in difficulties in selecting appropriate functions or plugins for varying design purposes, compounded by the lack of intuitive learning methods beyond traditional documentation, videos, and online forums. To address this challenge, an artificial intelligence (AI) interaction assist plugin for EDA software named SmartonAl is developed here, also KiCad is taken as the first example. SmartonAI is inspired by the HuggingGPT framework and employs large language models, such as GPT and BERT, to facilitate task planning and execution. On receiving a designer request, SmartonAI conducts a task breakdown and efficiently executes relevant subtasks, such as analysis of help documentation paragraphs and execution of different plugins, along with leveraging the built-in schematic and PCB manipulation functions in both SmartonAl itself and software. Our preliminary results demonstrate that SmartonAI can significantly streamline the PCB design process by simplifying complex commands into intuitive language-based interactions. By harnessing the powerful language capabilities of ChatGPT and the rich design functions of KiCad, the plugin effectively bridges the gap between complex EDA software and user-friendly interaction. Meanwhile, the new paradigm behind SmartonAI can also extend to other complex software systems, illustrating the immense potential of AI-assisted user interfaces in advancing digital interactions across various domains.

## 1. Advancing Placement

    doi = "10.1145/3439706.3446884",

    publisher = "ACM"

Abstract:
Placement is central to IC physical design: it determines spatial embedding, and hence parasitics and performance. From coarse-to fine-grain, placement is conjointly optimized with logic, performance, clock and power distribution, routability and manufacturability. This paper gives some personal thoughts on futures for placement research in IC physical design. Revisiting placement as optimization prompts a new look at placement requirements, optimization quality, and scalability with resources. Placement must also evolve to meet a growing need for co-optimizations and for co-operation with other design steps. "New" challenges will naturally arise from scaling, both at the end of the 2D scaling roadmap and in the context of future 2.5D/3D/4D integrations. And, the nexus of machine learning and placement optimization will continue to be an area of intense focus for research and practice. In general, placement research is likely to see more flow-scale optimization contexts, open source, benchmarking of progress toward optimality, and attention to translations into real-world practice.

## 4. The 2023 MLCAD FPGA Macro Placement Benchmark Design Suite and Contest Results
Valid Text Names:
'bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 1-2', 'bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 2-3', 'bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 3-4', 'bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 4-5', 'bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 5-6', 'bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 6-6'

    author = "Bustany, Ismail and Gasparyan, Grigor and Gupta, Amit and Kahng, Andrew B. and Kalase, Meghraj and Li, Wuxi and Pramanik, Bodhisatta",
    title = "The 2023 MLCAD FPGA Macro Placement Benchmark Design Suite and Contest Results",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "2023 ACM/IEEE 5th Workshop on Machine Learning for CAD (MLCAD)",
    pages = "1-6",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.1109/mlcad58807.2023.10299868",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1109/mlcad58807.2023.10299868",
    publisher = "IEEE"
}


Abstract:
The 2023 IEEE/ACM MLCAD Workshop program included the inaugural MLCAD Contest, a multi-month research and development competition intended to focus attention on pressing challenges at the nexus of electronic design automation (EDA) and machine learning. The 2023 contest aimed to spur research into machine learning-driven solutions that can supplant state-of-the-art algorithms for FPGA macro placement. Several factors make this problem more challenging than its ASIC counterpart. These include: (i) discrete and SITE-typed columnated nature of the FPGA device layout; (ii) cascaded macros or carry chains that can greatly impact routability and timing closure because of their high pin count and net connectivities, (iii) prevalence of resource-bound multi-clock domains; (iv) larger number of macros (typically 100s–1000s) than in ASICs; and (v) fragmentation of placement across multiple dies. Two benchmark suites containing 140 public and 198 hidden designs with varying complexities (based on Rent exponent, LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP utilization, carry chains, cascaded macro shapes and number of clock domains) were created for this competition. 19 teams globally participated in the contest. We share the results from the 8 finalists, 6 of which had competitive solutions. Our goal was for the contest to draw international participation and catalyze industry-academic collaborations, leading to contributions in premier conferences and journals. Several contestants used electrostatic-based frameworks (e.g., [13], DreamPlaceFPGA [29] and OpenPARF [24]) for global placement solution evaluations, alongside core macro placement algorithms based on classical optimization approaches (e.g., min-cost flow, bipartite graph matching, and simulated annealing). One team used an RL-parameterized simulated annealing algorithm for their macro placement solution. The contributed solutions are insightful, and we remain optimistic about the contest’s potential lasting influence for developing better algorithms for FPGA macro placement.

## 6. Benchmarking End-To-End Performance of AI-Based Chip Placement Algorithms
Valid Text Names:
'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 1-2', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 2-4', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 4-6', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 6-8', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 8-9', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 9-12', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 12-14', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 14-17', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 17-19', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 19-21', 'wang2024benchmarkingendtoendperformance pages 21-21'

    title = "Benchmarking End-To-End Performance of AI-Based Chip Placement Algorithms",
    year = "2026",

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2407.15026",

    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
The increasing complexity of modern very-large-scale integration (VLSI) design highlights the significance of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) technologies. Chip placement is a critical step in the EDA workflow, which positions chip modules on the canvas with the goal of optimizing performance, power, and area (PPA) metrics of final chip designs. Recent advances have demonstrated the great potential of AI-based algorithms in enhancing chip placement. However, due to the lengthy workflow of chip design, the evaluations of these algorithms often focus on intermediate surrogate metrics, which are easy to compute but frequently reveal a substantial misalignment with the end-to-end performance (i.e., the final design PPA). To address this challenge, we introduce ChiPBench, which can effectively facilitate research in chip placement within the AI community. ChiPBench is a comprehensive benchmark specifically designed to evaluate the effectiveness of existing AI-based chip placement algorithms in improving final design PPA metrics. Specifically, we have gathered 20 circuits from various domains (e.g., CPU, GPU, and microcontrollers). These designs are compiled by executing the workflow from the verilog source code, which preserves necessary physical implementation kits, enabling evaluations for the placement algorithms on their impacts on the final design PPA. We executed six state-of-the-art AI-based chip placement algorithms on these designs and plugged the results of each single-point algorithm into the physical implementation workflow to obtain the final PPA results. Experimental results show that even if intermediate metric of a single-point algorithm is dominant, while the final PPA results are unsatisfactory. We believe that our benchmark will serve as an effective evaluation framework to bridge the gap between academia and industry.

## 7. OpenPARF: An Open-Source Placement and Routing Framework for Large-Scale Heterogeneous FPGAs with Deep Learning Toolkit

    url = "https://doi.org/10.1109/asicon58565.2023.10396248",
    publisher = "IEEE"

Abstract:
This paper proposes OpenPARF, an open-source placement and routing framework for large-scale FPGA designs1. OpenPARF is implemented with the deep learning toolkit PyTorch and supports massive parallelization on GPU. The framework proposes a novel asymmetric multi-electrostatic field system to solve FPGA placement. It considers fine-grained routing resources inside configurable logic blocks (CLBs) for FPGA routing and supports large-scale irregular routing resource graphs. Experimental results on ISPD 2016 and ISPD 2017 FPGA contest benchmarks and industrial benchmarks demonstrate that OpenPARF can achieve 0.4-12.7% improvement in routed wirelength and more than 2× speedup in placement. We believe that OpenPARF can pave the road for developing FPGA physical design engines and stimulate further research on related topics.

## 1. Challenges and Limitations in the Synthetic Generation of mHealth Sensor Data
Valid Text Names:
'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 1-2', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 2-4', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 4-6', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 6-8', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 8-9', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 9-11', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 11-13', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 13-15', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 15-17', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 17-19', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 19-20', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 20-22', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 22-24', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 24-25', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 25-27', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 27-28', 'martino2505challengesandlimitations pages 28-29'

BibTex:
@article{martino2505challengesandlimitations,
    author = "Martino, Flavio Di and Delmastro, Franca",
    title = "Challenges and Limitations in the Synthetic Generation of mHealth Sensor Data",
    year = "2505",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.14206",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.14206",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.14206"
}


Abstract:
The widespread adoption of mobile sensors has the potential to provide massive and heterogeneous time series data, driving Artificial Intelligence applications in mHealth. However, data collection remains limited due to stringent ethical regulations, privacy concerns, and other constraints, hindering progress in the field. Synthetic data generation, particularly through Generative Adversarial Networks and Diffusion Models, has emerged as a promising solution to address both data scarcity and privacy issues. Yet, these models are often limited to short-term, unimodal signal patterns. This paper presents a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art generative models for time series synthesis, with a focus on their ability to jointly handle multi-modality, long-range dependencies, and conditional generation-key challenges in the mHealth domain. To ensure a fair comparison, we introduce a novel evaluation framework designed to measure both the intrinsic quality of synthetic data and its utility in downstream predictive tasks. Our findings reveal critical limitations in the existing approaches, particularly in maintaining cross-modal consistency, preserving temporal coherence, and ensuring robust performance in train-on-synthetic, test-on-real, and data augmentation scenarios. Finally, we present our future research directions to enhance synthetic time series generation and improve the applicability of generative models in mHealth.

Abstract:
Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming industries by producing high-quality text, images, music, and code. Its applications extend to natural language processing, computer vision, and creative arts. However, assessing these systems' performance and impact remains challenging due to their complexity, subjectivity, and open-ended outputs.

This paper comprehensively reviews evaluation methods for generative AI, beginning with its evolution and major applications, including advanced models like GPT, DALL·E, and AlphaCode. It categorizes evaluation approaches into quantitative metrics (such as BLEU and FID) and qualitative methods (human assessment and user-centered testing).

Key challenges, such as subjectivity, bias, and scalability, are explored alongside emerging trends like automated evaluation tools, ethical impact assessments, and multimodal techniques. Through real-world case studies, this paper highlights practical evaluation strategies and their limitations. By integrating current best practices and identifying future research opportunities, this study aims to guide the development of reliable, fair, and comprehensive evaluation frameworks for generative AI systems.

Abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study presents a comparative performance evaluation of state-of-the-art generative artificial intelligence models in the context of data analysis. Eight large language models (Claude, Gemini, ChatGPT, Qwen, Grok, DeepSeek, LLaMA, and Mistral) were tested on 13 distinct analytical tasks derived from the Titanic dataset. Performance was assessed using a multidimensional scoring rubric consisting of five main categories—technical accuracy, analytical depth, machine learning application, presentation and communication, and originality—with a total of 14 sub-criteria. Each model’s output was rated on a five-point scale by independent evaluators. Results indicate that Claude and Gemini outperformed others, particularly in tasks requiring reasoning and transparency, while LLaMA and Mistral showed weaknesses in higher-order cognitive tasks. Overall, the findings provide theoretical insight into the cognitive capacities of generative artificial intelligence models in data-driven contexts and offer practical guidance for model selection in applied analytics.</jats:p>

Abstract:
<jats:p>Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a quickly evolving sphere that has allowed generating real data and making progress in the area of producing high-quality images, text-to-image translation, and predicting time series. Nevertheless, the growing variety of model architectures has turned the choice of suitable generative methods, the tailoring of these approaches, and their assessment into a significant concern of the researchers. The presented literature review provides a task-based and systematic taxonomy of generative modeling, which provides a comparative summary of the most common paradigms such as the Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), the Normalizing Flows, and the Diffusion Models. The survey is structured in the context of the main research activities: high-fidelity data synthesis, controllable and conditional generation, sequential and structured data modeling, representation learning and disentanglement, data augmentation and simulation, and probabilistic density estimation. In every task, we discuss technical goals, essential practices, measures of evaluation, and best practices. The paper also includes cross-cutting techniques such as utilizing foundation models, data curation, large-scale data processing, computational scaling, reproducibility and responsible AI practices in addition to model selection. Lastly, we present the modern technical issues and areas of future research pointing to outstanding questions concerning controllability, evaluation, efficiency, and ethical applications.This paper serves as a comprehensive resource for researchers and practitioners in the field of generative modeling.</jats:p>

Abstract:
<jats:p>This review uniquely integrates open access dataset taxonomy with methodological trends in stress analysis, outlining the experimental framework and highlighting key gaps in reproducibility and FAIR compliance. In this context, stress induction methods, ground truth labeling approaches, open access datasets, computational advances, and current challenges and limitations are reported. A systematic review over the last decade (2014–2024) identified thirty-two open access affective datasets eligible for stress-related research, encompassing multimodal physiological signals, including electroencephalography (EEG), electrocardiography (ECG), electrodermal activity (EDA), and respiration (Resp), as well as behavioral measures, such as motion, audiovisual, and eye tracking data. Recent developments in signal analysis methods (2023–2025) highlight the predominance of multimodal fusion, advances in deep and self-supervised learning, personalized/adaptive models, and the growing adoption of explainable Artificial Intelligence, while machine learning approaches continue to hold a fundamental role. Despite these advances, several limitations and challenges remain, including heterogeneous experimental designs, sensor variability, limited demographic representation, data synchronization and labeling, and class imbalance. An effective experimental framework for stress research should integrate individual demographics and traits, reliable stressors, and high-quality physiological recordings within a well-defined and bias-controlled protocol, thereby producing reliable data to support and validate computational stress modeling. Continued progress in sensing, experimental standardization, and interpretable modeling is essential to produce reproducible, interpretable, and generalizable models of stress and emotions.</jats:p>

Abstract:
<jats:p>Emotional stress plays a critical role in mental health conditions such as anxiety, depression, and cognitive decline, yet its assessment remains challenging due to the subjective and episodic nature of conventional self-report methods. Multimodal physiological approaches, integrating signals such as electrocardiogram (ECG), electrodermal activity (EDA), and electromyography (EMG), offer a promising alternative by enabling objective, continuous, and complementary characterization of autonomic stress responses. Recent advances in machine learning and artificial intelligence (ML/AI) have become central to this paradigm, as they provide the capacity to model nonlinear dynamics, inter-modality dependencies, and individual variability that cannot be effectively captured by rule-based or single-modality methods. This paper reviews multimodal physiological stress recognition with an emphasis on ECG-centered systems and their integration with EDA and EMG. We summarize stress-related physiological mechanisms, catalog public and self-collected databases, and analyze their ecological validity, synchronization, and annotation practices. We then examine preprocessing pipelines, feature extraction methods, and multimodal fusion strategies across different stages of model design, highlighting how ML/AI techniques address modality heterogeneity and temporal misalignment. Comparative analysis shows that while deep learning models often improve within-dataset performance, their generalization across subjects and datasets remains limited. Finally, we discuss open challenges and future directions, including self-supervised learning, domain adaptation, and standardized evaluation protocols. This review provides practical insights for developing robust, generalizable, and scalable multimodal stress recognition systems for mental health monitoring.</jats:p>

Within the rapidly evolving domain of Electronic Design Automation (EDA), Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as transformative technologies, offering unprecedented capabilities for optimizing and automating various aspects of electronic design. This survey provides a comprehensive exploration of LLM applications in EDA, focusing on advancements in model architectures, the implications of varying model sizes, and innovative customization techniques that enable tailored analytical insights. By examining the intersection of LLM capabilities and EDA requirements, the article highlights the significant impact these models have on extracting nuanced understandings from complex datasets. Furthermore, it addresses the challenges and opportunities in integrating LLMs into EDA workflows, paving the way for future research and application in this dynamic field. Through this detailed analysis, the survey aims to offer valuable insights to professionals in the EDA industry, AI researchers, and anyone interested in the convergence of advanced AI technologies and electronic design.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 1-3'.

Nathaniel Pinckney, Christopher Batten, Mingjie Liu, Haoxing Ren, and Brucek Khailany. Revisiting verilogeval: a year of improvements in large-language models for hardware code generation. ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3718088, doi:10.1145/3718088. This article has 52 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The original VerilogEval benchmark (VerilogEval v1) contains 156 problems sourced from HDLBits and includes two problem-description types: VerilogEval-machine and VerilogEval-human. Performance is reported as a pass rate using the pass@k metric: the LLM is sampled multiple times and a problem is considered passed if at least one of the k samples passes the functional check. The paper explains the pass@k formulation and notes the original results used k = 1, 5, 10. Functional correctness is determined by counting trials that "pass the functional check" (c) out of trials (n). VerilogEval v2 extends the benchmark to support specification-to-RTL tasks (v1 only supported code completion), in-context learning examples, fine-grained failure classification, and a Makefile-based evaluation environment for easier scaling and inspection. The improved benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval. The excerpt does not state the project's license, so licensing details are not provided in the provided text.

## pqac-00000007

From text 'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3'.

Yao Lu, Shang Liu, Qijun Zhang, and Zhiyao Xie. Rtllm: an open-source benchmark for design rtl generation with large language model. 2024 29th Asia and South Pacific Design Automation Conference (ASP-DAC), pages 722-727, Aug 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.05345, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.05345. This article has 353 citations.

RTLLM is an open benchmark for RTL generation that "collects 30 common designs with various design scales and complexities." It defines three progressive evaluation goals: the syntax goal (generated RTL must be syntactically correct and "can be verified by checking whether the design can be correctly synthesized into netlist by synthesis tools"), the functionality goal (the RTL should match designers' expectations and "can be verified by checking whether the generated design passes all test cases in a comprehensive testbench"; the benchmark samples a reasonable number of test cases and notes that passing them does not guarantee exhaustive correctness), and the quality goal (practical design qualities: "including performance, power, area (PPA)" evaluated by measuring PPA "after the synthesis and layout of generated V"). For each of the 30 designs the benchmark supplies three files for automated evaluation: a natural-language Description, a Testbench (testbench.v) with multiple test cases, and a Correct Design (designer RTL.v) reference. The excerpt states availability: "It will be open-sourced in https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM." The provided excerpt does not state the licensing terms.

The excerpt describes the RTLLM/AssertEval benchmark and dataset details. AssertEval (part of RTLLM-2.0) includes 18 open-source designs spanning cryptographic units, processor cores, arithmetic units, communication protocols, and memory controllers. For each design RTLLM-2.0 provides three files: design_description.txt (natural-language specification and I/O), testbench.v (multiple test cases with inputs and expected outputs), and designer_RTL.v (human reference Verilog). Evaluation uses three progressive goals: (1) Syntax goal — the generated RTL should be syntactically correct and synthesizable (verifiable by synthesis into a netlist); (2) Functionality goal — the RTL should pass the provided testbench test cases; (3) Design-quality goal — practical qualities (PPA: performance, power, area) verified by measuring PPA after synthesis and layout. For assertion-generation evaluation the metrics include Syntax checks, FPV pass/fail (formal property verification), and COI (cone of influence) coverage. The authors limited specification documents to "fewer than 60 pages and contain fewer than 60" (signal/architectural items). Prior dataset work (RTLCoder) produced 27K samples and a verified high-quality subset of 7K “mostly-correct” samples. ## pqac-00000011

From text 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4'.

Dario Garcia-Gasulla, Gokcen Kestor, Emanuele Parisi, Miquel Albert'i-Binimelis, Cristian Gutierrez, Razine Moundir Ghorab, Orlando Montenegro, Bernat Homs, and Miquel Moretó. Turtle: a unified evaluation of llms for rtl generation. ArXiv, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.01986, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2504.01986. This article has 12 citations.

The excerpt describes TURTLE's evaluation pipeline and metrics relevant to RTL generation benchmarks (including mentions of RTLLM plots). Syntax, functionality and synthesis checks are binary stages named STX, FNC, and SYN, respectively, and are aggregated using PASS@k; TURTLE sets k = 1 and reports PASS@1 for LCA, STX, FNC, and SYN. Designs that pass SYN are assessed for design quality using a post-synthesis PPA-score derived from OpenLANE reports; PPA components are power, performance, and area. The designs in TURTLE are synthesized with the open-source SKY130A PDK and constrained to a 10ns delay. The PPA-score maps per-generation PPA metrics ˆpi,j to discrete interpretations (ˆpi,j=0: fails or ≥2× worse than reference; ˆpi,j=1: equal to human reference; ˆpi,j=2: idealized zero area/time/energy) and averages these across generations and across PPA dimensions (area, power, performance). The framework uses a cascade scoring (STX ≥ FNC ≥ SYN) and aggregates results across benchmarks via a weighted average. The excerpt does not provide RTLLM-specific counts (number of designs), license, or availability details; those items must be obtained from other sections or the paper's repository/metadata.

The excerpt describes CircuitNet as the "first open-source dataset called CircuitNet" for ML in VLSI CAD. The dataset uses RISC-V designs: "RISC-V designs were mapped from register transfer level (RTL) designs to gate-level netlists in the 28 nm technology node with Synopsys Design Compiler." Physical design used Cadence Innovus to produce layouts. The corpus includes multiple design variants: "Each design has 2160 settings, and all the designs have 12,960 runs of the back-end design flow." Available prediction targets and features mentioned include congestion, DRC violations, and IR drop, along with other layout and power/timing related features (e.g., cell density, macro regions, pin configuration, instance power, utilizations, #Macro, #Power Mesh, filler insertion, clock tree, signal arrival). Feature extraction spans synthesis through placement and routing stages. Evaluation metrics cited include pixel-level accuracy for some spatial tasks (an FCN baseline produced "0.040 and 0.80, respectively"), and for DRC violation prediction they used "the area under the curve (AUC) of the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and that of the precision-recall (PR) curve." ## pqac-00000016

From text 'jiang2024circuitnet2.0an pages 3-4'.

X Jiang, Y Zhao, Y Lin, and R Wang. Circuitnet 2.0: an advanced dataset for promoting machine learning innovations in realistic chip design environment. Unknown journal, 2024.

CircuitNet 2.0 is a cross-stage, multi-modal dataset generated with commercial EDA tools. It uses an advanced 14nm FinFET PDK (the original CircuitNet used 28nm planar CMOS) and includes a wider variety of design types (CPU, GPU, AI chip). The dataset targets three core objectives: routability, IR-drop, and timing. Typical evaluation metrics and labels discussed include routing congestion ("the overflow of routing demand over routing resources"), distributions of design rule violations (DRV hotspots), and IR-drop ("the deviation of supply power due to large local current demand"). CircuitNet 2.0 provides both geometric (cell locations, suitable for image-based models/image-to-image tasks) and topological data (cell/net connectivity, suitable for graph-based modeling), enabling image- and graph-based ML approaches. Table 1 lists multiple designs spanning scales (examples: RISCY, RISCY-FPU, zero-riscy, OpenC910-1, Vortex-small/large, NVDLA-small/large) with per-design cell/net/macro/pin/IO counts and sample counts. Sample generation time varies widely (from ~2 hours for zero-riscy to ~1 week for NVDLA-large). The excerpt does not state dataset license or public availability details.

The excerpt documents the long history of ISPD/ICCAD placement contests and their shifting emphases. From ISPD05/06 “Placement” through a series of specialized contests, 14 placement contests have been run at major conferences. Several contest themes are explicit in the table: routability-driven placement (ISPD11, DAC12, ICCAD12), detailed placement (ICCAD13), detailed routing-driven placement (ISPD14), timing-driven placement (ICCAD14/ICCAD15), and blockage-aware placement (ISPD15). The contest suites have also been used as part of broader flow contexts that include stages such as synthesis, placement, CTS, post-place sizing/optimization, and routing; contest frameworks expose tool interfaces like Timer API and Parasitics API. The excerpt therefore indicates that contest metrics and objectives have focused on routability, timing, detailed-routing objectives and placement robustness (e.g., blockage-awareness), and that contests often evaluate flow-level outcomes across multiple stages. The excerpt does not list explicit numeric metrics (e.g., wirelength or congestion scores) or their exact scoring formulas.

The excerpt critiques existing placement/routing benchmark suites and contest testcases as episodic, obfuscated, incomplete, and often outdated (e.g., MCNC-era suites). It calls for updated, long-lived core formulations, solution criteria, and benchmark generators to better represent modern designs (Challenge 7). Recent open-hardware initiatives (RISC-V, POSH, FOSSi, CHIPS Alliance, OpenHW) are noted as promising sources for more complete and modern benchmarks. The text emphasizes that placement optimizers co-optimize many metrics — explicitly listing wirelength, timing, density, congestion, non-overlap, and pin accessibility — and that importance of these objectives can shift across iterations/stages. It highlights routability as especially hard to capture because a placement’s routability is only known after routing and depends on a specific router’s heuristics and fixed-die context. The excerpt also calls for improved congestion formulations for analytic placers (Challenge 11) and implies that contest suites and benchmarking culture should be refreshed to drive research and properly measure tradeoffs (quality vs runtime).

The excerpt describes the 2023 MLCAD FPGA macro-placement contest benchmark format and evaluation methodology. Benchmarks were provided in an extended Bookshelf format (similar to ISPD contest formats), with files such as design.nodes and design.nets; the public suite has 140 designs and a hidden suite of 198 designs. Contestants used a Vivado place-and-route (P&R) flow that ingested placements in the extended Bookshelf format, validated legality, and performed standard-cell placement and routing. The P&R flow was explicitly non-timing-driven: the contest emphasized routability and runtime rather than timing optimization. Solutions were evaluated on column/SITE legality, total routing congestion, macro placement runtime, and total Vivado P&R runtime (5-hour limit). The scoring function combined runtime and routability: scorej = (timplj + timeP&Rj) * routabilityj, with timemplj = 1 + max(0, timemplj − 10) (no penalty under 10 minutes) and routability as the sum of initial and final routing scores. Routing congestion metrics were derived from Vivado’s router and, for AMD FPGAs, assessed on the interconnect tile grid, categorized by wire-length (short/long) and direction. The excerpt references ISPD-style Bookshelf formats but does not detail ICCAD suites or explicit wirelength/timing metrics beyond noting that timing was not used for this contest.

The excerpt describes AnalogGym’s open testbench: simulations use the open-source Ngspice simulator with the sky130 PDK for AMP and LDO circuits. Some analog blocks (voltage references, temperature sensing front ends) are not supported with open PDKs because precise subthreshold modeling is needed; PLLs likewise require detailed analog/noise models typically absent from open PDKs. The benchmark frames analog circuit synthesis as a multi-objective optimization problem F(x,P,V,T) over design parameters x (transistor dims, resistances, capacitances, bias currents, ...), optimizing metrics such as gain, bandwidth, power consumption, noise, and area while satisfying constraints across PVT corners. The paper gives an active-area computation that sums transistor W×L×multiplicity×parallel plus capacitor and resistor contributions (capacitors scaled by 1089 per pF, resistors by 5 per kΩ) and then takes the square root. Process corners (SS, FF, SF, FS, TT), voltage points (max 1.32V, min 1.08V, nominal 1.2V), and temperatures (125°C, -40°C, 25°C) are explicitly considered. The excerpt does not state the benchmark license, availability location, or the total number of circuits included.

## pqac-00000031

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The authors built a custom benchmark and component library for PCB schematic generation using real-world IC components and datasheets. The benchmark contains 23 unique tasks spanning analog, digital, power, and mixed circuits; task difficulty is measured by circuit nodes and net counts, ranging from 4 component pins to over 100. They injected lookalike components (same package and similar pin counts but different functionality, e.g., voltage regulator vs. op-amp) to test LLMs' ability to choose correct parts. Verification and metrics emphasize topology- and rule-based checks: subgraph isomorphism (VF2) for topology verification, semantic constraint checks, and error reporting. They provide three feedback/error-reporting modalities—detailed and specific error messages (syntax, ERC, subgraph verification with precise pin/net/value information), concise error-only messages, and binary pass/fail—to evaluate LLM performance and guide refinement. The framework's correctness was validated via human expert evaluation, and ablation studies show subcircuit retrieval improves success rates. Full benchmark details and component-library construction are deferred to the Appendix.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 26-28'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen’s authored KiCad component library and evaluation approach. The provided library includes a small, curated dataset of components: 18 symbols, 21 footprints, and 14 reference datasheets (organized for KiCad 9 / SKiDL). PCBSchemaGen emits executable SKiDL (Python) artifacts (netlists, SVGs, initialized PCB files) enabling downstream EDA integration. In benchmarking, the paper performs a qualitative comparison across dimensions such as core task, output executability, scale, error correction, and constraints — claiming end-to-end system generation capability for system-scale PCBs (>100 pins) with closed-loop verification and knowledge-graph-driven physical constraints and subgraph-isomorphism checks. It contrasts against CircuitLM (which outputs CircuitJSON, is prone to open-loop hallucination and lacks datasheet-grounded verification) and GNN baselines (which degrade beyond ~50 nodes and lack pin-role semantics). The excerpt references Pass@k metrics but the detailed metric definitions and dataset split information are truncated. Major dataset gaps implied: lack of large, standardized, datasheet-linked PCB schematic corpora for system-scale (>100-pin) designs, inconsistent intermediate formats (CircuitJSON vs executable SKiDL), and missing ground-truth pin-to-datasheet mappings needed for reliable physical verification.

## pqac-00000033

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The PCBSchemaGen benchmark comprises an open-sourced KG and benchmark (available at the project's GitHub) and evaluates LLM-driven schematic generation across 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains. The framework is training-free: an LLM proposes SKiDL-style Python code and a datasheet-driven knowledge graph plus a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) verifier enforces pin-role and topological constraints. Reported evaluation focuses on design accuracy and computational efficiency, with results claiming significant improvements in those metrics. The paper stresses gaps in PCB datasets and verification: PCB schematic designs are scarce for large scale training, schematic capture is vendor-specific, and there is no established verification tool for PCB schematics. Prior analog verification via SPICE is noted as inadequate because "SPICE is designed for idealized functional simulation, not for validating PCB schematic correctness under real IC package and pin-level constraints." These limitations motivate the KG+SI verifier and the choice to release the benchmark and KG as open-source to help address dataset scarcity and evaluation reproducibility.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen introduces a first benchmark for evaluating LLM-driven PCB schematic generation consisting of 23 unique circuits covering digital, analog, and power domains; each task is tied to real-world IC components and packages. The system demonstrates end-to-end capability: from natural-language specification to KiCAD PCB-layout–ready output, and the benchmark is presented as reproducible and practical for future work. The authors also build a domain knowledge graph from open-source IC datasheets encoding pin-role semantics, constraint types, and topological constraints to drive verification and provide interpretable feedback. The excerpt does not enumerate explicit numeric metrics or detailed evaluation protocols; it implies evaluation focuses on end-to-end generation success (e.g., KiCAD-ready outputs) and verifier-driven code-synthesis success but gives no formal metric list. Important dataset gaps are highlighted: PCB schematic data are scarce, schematic capture formats are vendor-specific (hindering large-scale training), and prior work largely omits board-level packaging and pin-constraint modeling—limiting opportunities for fine-tuning LLMs on PCB schematic tasks.

## pqac-00000035

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The PCBSchemaGen benchmark is a 23-task PCB schematic-generation benchmark (Table 1 lists tasks ID 1–23) covering sensing, auxiliary power, power stages, drivers, DC-DC and DC-AC designs (examples include resistor dividers for HV-to-LV ADC sensing, LDO-based low-voltage supplies, and synchronous buck converters). The evaluation focuses on LLM capabilities for pin-function understanding, knowledge-graph and topology constraints, topology generation, and mixed analog/digital/power synthesis. Evaluation metrics: Pass@k (from Chen et al. 2021) is used with k = 1 and 5, n = 15 trials, and each trial allows up to three self-correction retry attempts; the paper gives the Pass@k formula and sets model temperature = 0.5. Operational cost/runtime reported: ~23k tokens per task, ~$0.07, ~2.4 minutes; human expert ~1.5 hours. Models evaluated include multiple open- and closed-source LLMs and fine-tuned variants. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss broader PCB dataset sources or availability; it does note that open-source models underperform on complex designs (e.g., >20 devices and >70 pins), implying challenges on large/complex PCB tasks but not detailing dataset gaps or public dataset availability.

## pqac-00000036

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes methodological elements of PCBSchemaGen relevant to benchmarking LLM-generated PCB schematics, though it does not list specific dataset names or standard numeric metrics. The authors build a domain-specific Knowledge Graph (KG) from real-world IC datasheets to encode pin roles, electrical properties, topological constraints, and isolation boundaries, explicitly to avoid uploading full datasheets to LLMs. This KG reduces per-component token footprint from ~16k tokens (full datasheet) to ~300 tokens, a 30–70× reduction. The KG is formally defined as KG = (C, R, A, Φ, I) and is used both for constrained generation and verification. For evaluation, they employ a multi-stage verification pipeline: Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in Electrical Rule Checking (ERC), inter- and intra-connection checks, and subgraph isomorphism/topology verification (Algorithm 1: BuildBipartiteGraph, BuildTopologyGraph, rule evaluation). Prompt engineering techniques (ICL, Chain-of-Thought) and SKiDL as the code medium are emphasized to improve LLM understanding and executability. The excerpt implies gaps in raw PCB dataset availability (motivating the KG and token-reduction approach) but does not enumerate benchmark datasets, quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, BLEU, or F1), or dataset scale—those details are absent from this excerpt.

Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.", "text_name_focus_list": ["wang2025vericontaminatedassessingllmdriven pages 1-2", "chen2026reportfornsf pages 3-4", "chen2026reportfornsf pages 5-6", "fang2025asurveyof pages 58-59", "pan2025asurveyof pages 18-19", "chen2024thedawnof pages 11-12"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000037

From text 'chen2026reportfornsf pages 3-4'.

Deming Chen, Vijay Ganesh, Weikai Li, Yingyan Lin, Yong Liu, Subhasish Mitra, David Z. Pan, Ruchir Puri, Jason Cong, and Yizhou Sun. Report for nsf workshop on ai for electronic design automation [nsf workshop report]. IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine, 26:67-81, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2601.14541, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2601.14541. This article has 1 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several gaps and challenges directly relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. Data availability and openness are limited: "EDA and design data are limited and often not in the open domain," which constrains creation of standardized PCB datasets. Data quality issues are called out explicitly: "Garbage-in, garbage-out issues in AI training data often limit the effectiveness of predictive models," implying risks of contaminated or low-quality corpora undermining evaluation. Model generalization is a showstopper: "AI models often fail to adapt to new designs or nodes," complicating benchmark validity across domains. The text also notes practical barriers to reproducible evaluation: lack of standardized datasets/benchmarks and tool integration, urging "Open-source initiatives for developing shared datasets, tools, and benchmarks" and to "Develop public benchmarks and datasets ... to enable reproducibility and innovation." Additional constraints impacting evaluation include "DRC complexity" (many negotiable rules), "Computational resource demands" limiting who can run evaluations, and "Integration challenges" that prevent end-to-end, comparable pipelines. Together these points identify dataset scarcity/openness, data quality/contamination risks, reproducibility and benchmarking needs, and resource/integration limits as core evaluation gaps.


---

## pqac-00000038

From text 'jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3'.

X Jiang, Y Zhao, Y Lin, and R Wang. Circuitnet 2.0: an advanced dataset for promoting machine learning innovations in realistic chip design environment. Unknown journal, 2024.

The excerpt highlights several gaps and challenges relevant to evaluating generative circuit design datasets. Public datasets are scarce: "there are very few public datasets available to implement and evaluate ML algorithms." Generating ML-ready EDA data is costly and slow because it "needs to be generated by numerous runs of EDA flows ... each run can take weeks to months." Legal and commercial restrictions further impede sharing: "the Non-disclosure Agreement (NDA) in manufacturing technology and EDA tools also prohibits the release of the original data." As a result, "most studies can only generate small internal datasets," which makes "benchmarking and reproducing results extremely challenging." Existing benchmarks and suites often provide only isolated data for narrow tasks (e.g., placement or routing) and "cannot be directly used for ML tasks" or cross-stage innovation; they also lack intermediate flow dumps. Even efforts to expand public resources (e.g., CircuitNet) still leave gaps in representing realistic design diversity: "the design types, design scales, manufacturing technology, and supported ML tasks are still far from representing the realistic chip design environment."


---

## pqac-00000039

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt identifies several key gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. First, there is a scarcity of open-source PCB schematic data and a near absence of suitable simulation-based verification, which limits training and evaluation: "Automated PCB schematic design remains unexplored due to the scarcity of open-source data and the absence of simulation-based verification." Second, PCB schematic work remains largely manual and iterative, demanding long validation cycles and expert knowledge: "the traditional PCB schematic design workflow is heavily dependent on manual effort, often requiring months or even years of iteration to validate a usable design." Third, common analog simulators like SPICE are insufficient for PCB-level correctness because they model idealized components and do not validate real IC package and pin-level constraints: "SPICE is designed for idealized functional simulation, not for vali- dating PCB schematic correctness under real IC package and pin-level constraints." Additional hurdles include vendor-specific schematic capture formats that impede large-scale dataset creation and generalization: "Since PCB schematic capture is vendor specific, this makes large scale training difficult." The paper also emphasizes the need for interpretable, rule-based verification to improve reproducibility and reliability in evaluation workflows.


---

## pqac-00000040

From text 'wang2025vericontaminatedassessingllmdriven pages 1-2'.

Zeng Wang, Minghao Shao, Jitendra Bhandari, Likhitha Mankali, Ramesh Karri, Ozgur Sinanoglu, Muhammad Shafique, and J. Knechtel. Vericontaminated: assessing llm-driven verilog coding for data contamination. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 117-123, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.13572, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.13572. This article has 15 citations.

The excerpt analyzes evaluation challenges for LLM-driven hardware (Verilog) code generation, focusing on data contamination where benchmark examples leak into pre-training/fine-tuning corpora. It highlights the undisclosed nature of pre-training datasets (even for open-source models) and the relative sparsity of domain-specific hardware data, which raises the likelihood that benchmarks overlap with training data and thereby skew results. The authors detect near-100% contamination rates for some models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o) across VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks and use detection methods (e.g., CCD, Min-K% Prob, Dolos) to assess similarity. They frame contamination as undermining fair ranking, benchmarking validity, and industrial adoption, and they explore mitigation trade-offs between code quality and fairness, aiming for contamination-free evaluation. The work is specific to Verilog/hardware LLMs and does not address PCB datasets explicitly, but the problems identified—undisclosed pretraining data, dataset sparsity, benchmark overlap/contamination, need for standardized/clean benchmarks, and trade-offs when mitigating contamination—are directly relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design systems.


---

## pqac-00000041

From text 'shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 1-3'.

EDA-Schema-V2: A Multimodal Schema, Open Datasets, and Benchmarks for Machine Learning in Digital Physical Design This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt highlights several gaps relevant to evaluating generative circuit (and by extension PCB) design. Key issues include a limited availability of open datasets and an absence of standardized data representations and schemas, which impede interoperability, comparability, and reproducibility across ML-based EDA research. Closed-source workflows and proprietary PDKs further restrict reproducibility and public accessibility. Existing efforts are limited in scope: METRICS2.1 provides only aggregate RTL-to-GDSII metrics rather than stage-wise or structural data, CircuitNet relies on closed tools, and CircuitOps—though open—omits pre-route representations and image modalities. Additional challenges cited are variations across technology nodes, time-consuming data preparation, inconsistent preprocessing, and the lack of a unified benchmarking framework. The paper argues for a standardized, stage-aware, multimodal schema and openly shared datasets to enable reproducible, comparable ML benchmarks across stages (from synthesis through routing) and modalities (graphs and images). The excerpt does not explicitly discuss data contamination or benchmark saturation, focusing instead on dataset availability, standardization, and reproducibility challenges.


---

## pqac-00000042

From text 'jiang2024circuitnet2.0an pages 1-3'.

X Jiang, Y Zhao, Y Lin, and R Wang. Circuitnet 2.0: an advanced dataset for promoting machine learning innovations in realistic chip design environment. Unknown journal, 2024.

The excerpt highlights key gaps and challenges relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. Public datasets for EDA are scarce because producing them requires many full EDA flow runs that can take weeks to months and deep domain knowledge, making data generation extremely expensive. NDAs in manufacturing technology and EDA tools further prevent releasing original data, forcing most studies to rely on small internal datasets and hindering benchmarking and reproducibility. Existing benchmarks are often isolated and task-specific (e.g., placement or routing) and lack intermediate EDA-flow data needed for cross-stage ML tasks. Current public datasets fall short in representing realistic variation in design types, scales, manufacturing technology nodes, and ML task diversity, which can bias model evaluation and limit generalization. The paper positions CircuitNet 2.0 as an attempt to mitigate these issues by providing a larger, multi-modal dataset covering million-gate designs, an advanced 14nm FinFET node, and additional tasks (e.g., timing) to support broader, more realistic evaluation and transfer learning across PDKs and designs.


---

## pqac-00000043

From text 'chen2026reportfornsf pages 5-6'.

Deming Chen, Vijay Ganesh, Weikai Li, Yingyan Lin, Yong Liu, Subhasish Mitra, David Z. Pan, Ruchir Puri, Jason Cong, and Yizhou Sun. Report for nsf workshop on ai for electronic design automation [nsf workshop report]. IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine, 26:67-81, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2601.14541, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2601.14541. This article has 1 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several gaps relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. Data scarcity and access barriers are primary issues: high-quality labeled datasets are scarce, proprietary, sensitive, and costly to produce, limiting training and evaluation. Existing datasets and efforts (HLsyn: 80,000 HLS programs; Chrysalis: ~1,500 annotated designs; ICCAD 2024 benchmark contest) help but are still far from "ImageNet-scale", so standardized, large open PCB/circuit datasets remain lacking. Toolchain drift and domain shift are critical evaluation challenges: models can fail when the target toolchain or design domain changes, requiring transfer-learning or adaptation methods. Trust, reliability, and verification are also major gaps — establishing comprehensive validation/verification processes is resource-intensive but necessary for industry adoption. Together these factors impede reproducibility and robust benchmarking: proprietary data and varying toolchains make experiments hard to reproduce, and limited benchmark scale risks overfitting or saturation of metrics. The report implies more coordinated dataset collection, standardized benchmarks, and methods for toolchain- and domain-robust evaluation are needed.


---

## pqac-00000044

From text 'pan2025asurveyof pages 18-19'.

Jingyu Pan, Guanglei Zhou, Chen-Chia Chang, Isaac Jacobson, Jiang Hu, and Yiran Chen. A survey of research in large language models for electronic design automation. Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3715324, doi:10.1145/3715324. This article has 109 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several evaluation-relevant gaps for applying LLMs to EDA that relate to generative circuit/PCB design. Key concerns include data privacy and proprietary data leakage: models trained on large datasets may inadvertently expose confidential designs, and the authors explicitly note the need for techniques that let models learn from proprietary data without risk of leakage. There is a lack of EDA-specific research addressing these issues (“no public work directly relating such concerns to using LLMs for EDA”), indicating a gap in domain-tailored evaluation protocols. Interpretability is another major gap: the “black box” nature of LLMs hampers understanding of design rationales, which is critical for debugging, verification, validation, and regulatory compliance. Practical evaluation is also affected by computational and energy costs; iterative design workflows with LLMs incur nontrivial inference energy, motivating hardware and model-efficiency solutions that impact reproducibility and the feasibility of large-scale benchmarks. The authors suggest work to demonstrate/exploit extraction risks and adapt defenses. The excerpt does not explicitly mention standardized open PCB datasets, benchmark saturation, or granular reproducibility protocols, so those topics are not directly supported here.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the details of AICircuit dataset for analog circuit design? How many circuits, what topologies, what evaluation metrics, what license?", "text_name_focus_list": ["mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2", "mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 2-4", "mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000045

From text 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 2-4'.

Asal Mehradfar, Xuzhe Zhao, Yue Niu, Sara Babakniya, Mahdi Alesheikh, Hamidreza Aghasi, and Salman Avestimehr. Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272. This article has 20 citations.

The excerpt describes AICircuit, a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ML-assisted analog and RF circuit design. The dataset includes seven core homogeneous circuit blocks: common-source voltage amplifier (CSVA), two-stage voltage amplifier (TSVA), cascode voltage amplifier (CVA), low-noise amplifier (LNA), power amplifier (PA), voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO), and mixer. In addition, AICircuit contains complex heterogeneous systems composed of multiple cascaded circuit components. Data were generated by creating Cadence schematics, identifying key parameters, setting [beg, end] ranges, sweeping each parameter with small step sizes, running an accurate commercial simulator to obtain performance metrics, and saving each parameter set and its performance as a row. The dataset is split into train and test sets. The collection contains circuit parameters and simulated performance metrics (including advanced metrics) and lists typical performance targets such as DC power consumption, bandwidth, and voltage gain. The work evaluates a range of ML models—from conventional methods like random forest and decision trees to neural networks such as MLPs and transformers—on the benchmark. The excerpt does not state the dataset license or exact metric definitions/formats.


---

## pqac-00000046

From text 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7'.

Asal Mehradfar, Xuzhe Zhao, Yue Niu, Sara Babakniya, Mahdi Alesheikh, Hamidreza Aghasi, and Salman Avestimehr. Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272. This article has 20 citations.

The AICircuit dataset (Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design) is presented as a multi-level benchmark for AI-driven analog IC design. The excerpt describes evaluations on homogeneous circuit blocks including two-stage voltage amplifier (TSVA), Mixer, voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO), and a power amplifier (PA); it also references noise figure in a low-noise amplifier (LNA) and phase noise in a VCO as advanced metrics included. Reported evaluation metrics and targets include DC power consumption, bandwidth, voltage gain, conversion gain, IF voltage swing, noise figure, oscillation frequency, output power, phase noise, and tuning range. Data preprocessing normalizes features/metrics to [−1, 1]. The dataset split used in experiments is 90% training / 10% test. Models trained include transformer and MLP (also SVR and RF compared); neural nets were trained for 100 epochs with the Adam optimizer at learning rate 0.001. The excerpt does not state the total number of circuits/topologies included in the full dataset nor the dataset license; it notes additional circuit results are deferred to Appendix D.


---

## pqac-00000047

From text 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 8-10'.

Asal Mehradfar, Xuzhe Zhao, Yue Niu, Sara Babakniya, Mahdi Alesheikh, Hamidreza Aghasi, and Salman Avestimehr. Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272. This article has 20 citations.

AICircuit is presented as a multi-level benchmark dataset for analog and radio-frequency (RF) circuit design that includes both homogeneous circuits (one or multiple circuits with identical functions) and heterogeneous circuits (circuits with different functions). The paper evaluates ML models (MLPs, Transformers, SVRs) on example heterogeneous systems: a transmitter (noted components: VCO and PA) and a receiver (LNA, mixer, cascode voltage amplifier). Evaluations report both system-level and component-level metrics; example metrics shown include Power Consumption, Voltage Gain, Noise Figure for the receiver and Large Signal Power Gain, Drain Efficiency, PAE for the PA in the transmitter. The authors note that receiver circuits are less complex and easier to predict, while transmitter-level relationships become more non-linear and harder to model. They also show that models trained on an isolated circuit (e.g., PA alone) generalize poorly inside a larger heterogeneous system due to load effects. The excerpt does not state how many circuits are in the dataset, the full list of topologies covered, dataset size, evaluation protocol details, or the dataset license.


---

## pqac-00000048

From text 'mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2'.

Asal Mehradfar, Xuzhe Zhao, Yue Niu, Sara Babakniya, Mahdi Alesheikh, Hamidreza Aghasi, and Salman Avestimehr. Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18272. This article has 20 citations.

The excerpt describes AICircuit as a multi-level dataset and benchmark for AI-driven analog and radio-frequency circuit design. The dataset “comprises seven commonly used basic circuits and two complex wireless transceiver systems composed of multiple circuit blocks,” covering both simple building blocks and heterogeneous system-level topologies. It is intended to support ML workflows that learn the mapping from design specifications (examples given: power consumption, bandwidth) to circuit parameters and to facilitate circuit sizing and topology decisions. The authors report that they “extensively evaluate various ML algorithms on the dataset,” showing ML’s potential for predicting parameters from specifications. The data and codebase are open-sourced with a GitHub link provided. The excerpt does not list the specific basic-circuit types/topology names, the concrete evaluation metrics or protocols used for benchmarking, nor does it state the dataset license — those details are not present in the provided pages and would need to be retrieved from the paper’s later sections or the linked GitHub repository.
