function.arguments: {"question": "What are the details of the survey on circuit foundation models, large circuit models, and EDA-Schema datasets for ML in physical design? What specific open datasets and benchmark suites are discussed?", "text_name_focus_list": ["fang2025asurveyof pages 56-58", "fang2025asurveyof pages 58-59", "chhabria2024strengtheningthefoundations pages 2-3", "chhabria2024strengtheningthefoundations pages 5-7"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000049

From text 'fang2025asurveyof pages 56-58'.

Wenji Fang, Jing Wang, Yao Lu, Shang Liu, Yuchao Wu, Yuzhe Ma, and Zhiyao Xie. A survey of circuit foundation model: foundation ai models for vlsi circuit design and eda. ArXiv, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711. This article has 40 citations.

The excerpt from the survey catalogs recent circuit foundation models and the open datasets / benchmark suites commonly used in ML for EDA and physical design. It lists a range of foundation-model-style works (e.g., DeepGate, DeepGate2, DeepGate4, Nettag) and points to benchmark sources and public corpora used by the community: classical netlist/RTL collections (RT-level ITC’99), open IP/core repositories (OpenCores), research platforms and SoC frameworks (Chipyard, VexRiscv), and dedicated ML datasets (Openabc-d). It also enumerates standard benchmark suites for logic synthesis and physical design: IWLS 2005, the EPFL combinational benchmark suite, LGSynth93, and contest-driven benchmark suites for placement/routability (ISPD-2011, DAC-2012), as well as ICCAD contest datasets. The excerpt additionally references work on HLS-targeted benchmarks (“Towards a comprehensive benchmark for high-level synthesis targeted to fpgas”). Together these items represent the open datasets and contests the survey identifies as relevant resources for training and evaluating large circuit models and ML methods in physical design.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'chhabria2024strengtheningthefoundations pages 5-7'.

Vidya A. Chhabria, Vikram Gopalakrishnan, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Zhiang Wang, Bing-Yue Wu, and Dooseok Yoon. Strengthening the foundations for ic physical design and ml eda research. Proceedings of the 43rd IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer-Aided Design, pages 1-9, Oct 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3676536.3697136, doi:10.1145/3676536.3697136. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt describes datasets, formats, APIs, and benchmark suites created to enable ML research in physical design. Key infrastructure includes CircuitOps (an AI-friendly data format) and numerous OpenROAD Python APIs that speed dataset generation and enable back-annotation and ML-in-the-loop workflows. DATC has produced IR tables (CircuitOps IR Table format) from post-route DEF for multiple open PDK/design sets: eight designs in NanGate45, five in SKY130HD, and four in ASAP7, published in the OpenROAD-flow-scripts and CircuitOps repositories. The 2024 ICCAD Contest Problem C (gate sizing) benchmarks include eight MacroPlacement GitHub designs and two OpenCores designs implemented in ASAP7; these benchmarks are distributed both as standard EDA files (SDC, LIB, LEF, DEF, gate-level Verilog) and as NVIDIA CircuitOps IR Tables. For LLM-based EDA work, the EDA Corpus is introduced as the first open-source LLM-supporting dataset: 943 OpenROAD prompt-script pairs (including 373 prompt→script pairs for flow stages and 570 prompt→script database-query pairs) plus question-answer pairs; OpenROAD-Assistant is an open-source LLM built on this corpus. Repositories and tutorial materials (ASP-DAC24, CircuitOps GitHub, OpenROAD-flow-scripts) are provided to seed community research, though challenges such as fragmented repos and reproducibility remain.


---

## pqac-00000051

From text 'fang2025asurveyof pages 58-59'.

Wenji Fang, Jing Wang, Yao Lu, Shang Liu, Yuchao Wu, Yuzhe Ma, and Zhiyao Xie. A survey of circuit foundation model: foundation ai models for vlsi circuit design and eda. ArXiv, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711. This article has 40 citations.

The excerpt from the Fang et al. survey catalogs many recent open datasets and benchmark suites used for ML and large-model work in RTL/Verilog/VHDL code generation and evaluation. Notable open resources called out include RTL-Repo ("A benchmark for evaluating llms on large-scale rtl design projects"), OpenLLM-RTL ("Open dataset and benchmark for llm-aided design rtl generation"), and MG-Verilog ("Multi-grained dataset towards enhanced llm-assisted verilog generation"). The survey also references evaluation frameworks and benchmark efforts such as VHDL-Eval (a framework for evaluating LLMs in VHDL), Revisiting VerilogEval (updated VerilogEval evaluations), and a multimodal Verilog benchmark ("Natural language is not enough: Benchmarking multi-modal generative ai for verilog generation"). In addition, works focused on synthetic data and representation — e.g., CraftRTL (high-quality synthetic data generation for Verilog models) and DeepRTL (a unified representation model bridging Verilog understanding and generation) — are listed. The survey thus aggregates datasets, benchmarks, and tooling efforts that support LLM-assisted hardware code generation, evaluation, and data augmentation for RTL/Verilog/VHDL tasks.


---

## pqac-00000052

From text 'shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 1-3'.

EDA-Schema-V2: A Multimodal Schema, Open Datasets, and Benchmarks for Machine Learning in Digital Physical Design This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes EDA-Schema-V2, an open, multimodal schema and dataset for ML in digital physical design. Datasets are generated with the OpenROAD tool flow and open PDKs (SkyWater 130 nm, Nangate 45 nm, IHP SG13G2 130 nm, ASAP 7 nm) and are based on the IWLS’05 benchmark suite. The release contains approximately 7,800 design instances across 18 benchmark circuits with stage-resolved representations from synthesis through detailed routing, providing multimodal data (circuit graphs, spatial layout images, and QoR metrics). The dataset scale is large: over 275 million gates, 75 million nets, and more than 36 million extracted timing paths. The work identifies twelve representative prediction tasks spanning timing, power, area, and routing, and provides baseline analyses and standardized metrics; the formatted dataset and schema code are released on GitHub. Related prior efforts noted include METRICS2.1, CircuitNet, and CircuitOps, with discussion of their scope and limitations (e.g., closed-source PDKs or limited stage/modal coverage). The excerpt does not, however, include a survey or detailed information specifically on "circuit foundation models" or "large circuit models."


---

## pqac-00000053

From text 'shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 28-29'.

EDA-Schema-V2: A Multimodal Schema, Open Datasets, and Benchmarks for Machine Learning in Digital Physical Design This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt documents the EDA-Schema-V2 multimodal schema and the accompanying large-scale dataset and benchmarks for ML in digital physical design. The dataset was generated with the OpenROAD tool chain using four open PDKs (SkyWater 130 nm, Nangate 45 nm, ASAP7 7 nm, IHP SG13G2 130 nm) and is derived from IWLS’05 benchmark circuits via systematic parameter sweeps. It comprises roughly 7,800 physical design instances from 18 benchmark circuits, covering >275 million gates, 75 million nets, and >36 million extracted timing paths. The schema captures stage-resolved RTL-to-GDSII artifacts in an entity–relationship structure; representations include graph models (logical/physical connectivity), spatial layout images, and heatmaps of analyzed properties. The paper provides baseline analyses and metrics (referenced in Table 8) to quantify early-stage QoR prediction accuracy across stages and PDKs, positioning Table 8 as a reference leaderboard. The specification, dataset, and tooling are publicly available at the cited GitHub repository. The excerpt does not include a survey or details on "circuit foundation models" or broader "large circuit models" beyond this EDA-Schema-V2 dataset and its benchmark information.


---

## pqac-00000054

From text 'jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3'.

X Jiang, Y Zhao, Y Lin, and R Wang. Circuitnet 2.0: an advanced dataset for promoting machine learning innovations in realistic chip design environment. Unknown journal, 2024.

The excerpt surveys several public datasets and benchmark suites relevant to ML for physical design. It notes a scarcity of public data due to NDA and generation cost, and highlights prior open resources: CircuitNet (over 10,000 samples) supporting routability and IR-drop prediction, and the new CircuitNet 2.0 which extends CircuitNet by including million-gate designs (CPU/GPU/AI chips), using a 14nm FinFET PDK, and adding timing prediction alongside routability and IR-drop to support multi-model tasks. Standard EDA contest benchmarks from ISPD and ICCAD are mentioned as providers of pre-processed task-specific data. Other open benchmarks discussed include OPDB (a scalable, extensible design benchmark), the EPFL combinational benchmark suite (logic optimization benchmarks), and BeGAN (GAN-based synthetic PDN benchmark generation focused on IR-drop). The excerpt also references ML research directions enabled by datasets (e.g., transfer learning across PDKs, handling imbalanced samples) and emphasizes that many existing benchmarks are task-specific and often lack intermediate EDA flow dumps needed for cross-stage ML innovations.

## pqac-00000055

From text 'fang2025asurveyof pages 2-4'.

Wenji Fang, Jing Wang, Yao Lu, Shang Liu, Yuchao Wu, Yuzhe Ma, and Zhiyao Xie. A survey of circuit foundation model: foundation ai models for vlsi circuit design and eda. ArXiv, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711. This article has 40 citations.

The excerpt introduces a survey of foundation AI models for VLSI circuit design and EDA, motivating the trend by noting that circuit-focused AI lags behind NLP/CV and that recent foundation models for EDA show strong generalization, few-shot learning, and generative abilities. It states that these models commonly follow a two-stage paradigm: pre-training on large-scale datasets followed by fine-tuning for specific tasks. The authors highlight a lack of systematic definitions and analyses in the community and present an evolutionary tree (Fig. 2) that categorizes recent works into encoder-based (covered in Section 4) and decoder-based (covered in Section 5) circuit foundation models. Numerous representative model names are shown across 2021–2025 (examples visible in the figure include Design2Vec, FGNN, NetTAG, DeepGate3, LLM-HD, DeepRTL, RTLSquad, ChipGPTV, VerilogEval). The excerpt, however, does not provide specific details about "large circuit models" definitions, EDA-Schema datasets, or explicit open datasets and benchmark suites for ML in physical design — those details are not present on these pages.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive table of datasets and benchmarks for ML/generative EDA as of 2025. Include the following columns: Dataset/Benchmark Name, Citation (author-year), Domain (HDL Generation / Placement-Routing / Analog / PCB), Scale (number of problems/designs/samples), Key Metrics, Availability (URL/license), and Relevance to Open-Hardware PCB Pipeline. Include these entries:

1. VerilogEval - Liu et al. 2023 - 156 problems from HDLBits - pass@k functional correctness - GitHub NVlabs/verilog-eval
2. RTLLM / RTLLM 2.0 - Lu et al. 2024 / Liu et al. 2024 - 30/50 designs - syntax/functionality/quality goals - GitHub hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM
3. CVDP - Pinckney et al. 2025 - 783 problems, 13 categories - pass@1, BLEU - open-source
4. VeriContaminated - Wang et al. 2025 - contamination analysis on VerilogEval/RTLLM - contamination rate metrics
5. CircuitNet / CircuitNet 2.0 - Chai et al. 2022 / Jiang et al. 2024 - 10K+ samples, 28nm/14nm - congestion/DRC/IR-drop - circuitnet.github.io
6. ISPD/ICCAD Contest Suites - Various 2005-2025 - wirelength/congestion/timing - conference archives
7. EDALearn - Pan et al. 2024 - RTL-to-signoff, FreePDK45/ASAP7 - routability/IR-drop prediction - open-source
8. EDA-Schema-V2 - Shrestha et al. 2025 - ~7800 instances, 18 circuits, 4 PDKs - timing/power/area/routing - GitHub
9. AnalogGym - Li et al. 2024 - 30 topologies, 5 categories - PPA/PVT metrics - GitHub CODA-Team/AnalogGym
10. AICircuit - Mehradfar et al. 2024 - 7 basic circuits + 2 systems - power/bandwidth/gain - GitHub open-source
11. PCBSchemaGen - Zou et al. 2025 - 23 PCB schematic tasks - Pass@k, topology verification - GitHub open-source
12. TuRTLe - Garcia-Gasulla et al. 2025 - unified framework integrating VerilogEval+RTLLM - STX/FNC/SYN/PPA-score - open-source
13. ChiPBench - Wang et al. 2024 - 20 circuits from CPU/GPU/MCU - end-to-end PPA - open-source"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Dataset/Benchmark Name | Citation (author-year) | Domain | Scale | Key Metrics | Availability (URL/license) | Relevance to Open-Hardware PCB Pipeline |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| VerilogEval | Liu et al. 2023 | HDL Generation | 156 HDLBits problems; machine/human subsets | Functional correctness via pass@k (commonly pass@1/5/10); simulation against golden solutions | GitHub: NVlabs/verilog-eval; open-source benchmark, license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000005) | Useful for controller/FPGA/MCU-support logic generation that may accompany open control PCBs, but evaluates RTL only—not schematics, BOMs, footprints, or manufacturable PCB constraints. |
| RTLLM / RTLLM 2.0 | Lu et al. 2024; Liu et al. 2024 | HDL Generation | 30 designs in RTLLM; 50 designs in RTLLM 2.0 | Syntax goal, functionality goal, design-quality goal (PPA after synthesis/layout); testbench pass/fail | GitHub: hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM and related OpenLLM-RTL repos; open-source intent, license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013) | More relevant than VerilogEval when an open-hardware PCB pipeline includes synthesizable digital peripherals or glue logic; still misses board-level connectivity, ERC/DRC, connectors, and component/package realism. |
| CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems) | Pinckney et al. 2025 | HDL Generation | 783 problems across 13 task categories | pass@1 for code-generation tasks; BLEU and LLM-judge scoring for comprehension-style tasks | Open-source benchmark/infrastructure reported by authors; repository/open tooling stated, license not stated in retrieved evidence (from paper search results) | Valuable for evaluating agentic HDL workflows beyond one-shot generation, especially verification/debug loops relevant to mixed HW/SW control systems on open boards. |
| VeriContaminated | Wang et al. 2025 | HDL Generation / Evaluation Methodology | Contamination study over VerilogEval and RTLLM across many LLMs | Contamination-rate metrics using CCD, Min-K% Prob, Dolos-style analyses; fairness vs quality tradeoff | Open-sourced code on GitHub per paper; DOI/arXiv available (pqac-00000040) | Critical meta-benchmark for any open-hardware generative pipeline: reminds evaluators that apparent HDL success may be inflated by benchmark leakage, undermining reproducibility and trust. |
| CircuitNet / CircuitNet 2.0 | Chai et al. 2022; Jiang et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing | Original CircuitNet: 10,242 post-flow layouts from 12,960 backend runs, 28nm; CircuitNet 2.0: 10K+ samples, 14nm FinFET, CPU/GPU/AI-class designs | Congestion: NRMSE, SSIM; DRC/IR-drop: ROC-AUC and PR-AUC; labels include congestion, DRC/DRV hotspots, IR-drop, timing/routability | https://circuitnet.github.io/ ; open-source dataset, explicit license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000019) | Strongest open chip-physical-design dataset family for ML baselines. Indirectly relevant to open control PCBs by informing placement/routing evaluation thinking, but not directly transferable to PCB manufacturing files or board-level constraints. |
| ISPD / ICCAD Contest Suites | Various, 2005–2025 | Placement-Routing | Multi-year contest benchmark families across placement, routability, detailed placement, timing-driven placement, FPGA macro placement, global routing | HPWL/wirelength, congestion/routability, timing slack (WNS/TNS), legality, runtime; contest-specific objective functions | Conference archives / contest sites; generally public benchmark files, licenses vary by contest (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025) | Essential baselines for chip-floorplanning and routing evaluation culture. Helpful as methodological precedent for PCB benchmark design, but not a substitute for open PCB corpora. |
| EDALearn | Pan et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing / End-to-End EDA | Holistic RTL-to-signoff suite across FreePDK45 and ASAP7; diverse public designs from IWLS, Chipyard/BOOM, OpenCores, ISCAS, ITC’99, etc. | Standardized evaluation for routability prediction, IR-drop prediction, cross-stage analysis; image-like and vector features | Open-source benchmark suite reported by authors; exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027) | Highly relevant as a template for reproducible end-to-end benchmarking. For open-hardware PCBs, it suggests how a board benchmark should connect source design intent to final manufacturable artifacts. |
| EDA-Schema-V2 | Shrestha et al. 2025 | Placement-Routing / Digital Physical Design | ~7,800 instances from 18 IWLS’05 circuits across 4 open PDKs (Sky130, Nangate45, IHP SG13G2, ASAP7) | Timing, power, area, routing/QoR prediction tasks; stage-aware benchmark metrics and baseline leaderboards | GitHub release reported; open schema/dataset, exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053) | Particularly relevant methodologically: multimodal, stage-aware, reproducible, and open. A strong model for how an open PCB benchmark could combine schematic, layout, nets, images, and fabrication outputs. |
| AnalogGym | Li et al. 2024 | Analog | 30 circuit topologies in 5 categories: amplifiers, LDOs, sensing front ends, voltage references, PLLs | Multi-objective analog metrics over PVT: gain, bandwidth, power, noise, area; DC/AC/PZ/transient analyses | GitHub: CODA-Team/AnalogGym; open-source suite/documentation reported, exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030) | Very relevant for analog portions of control PCBs (power rails, sensing front ends). However, it evaluates IC-level analog synthesis, not discrete-component PCB schematics or board manufacturability. |
| AICircuit | Mehradfar et al. 2024 | Analog | 7 basic circuits + 2 heterogeneous wireless transceiver systems | Power consumption, bandwidth, gain, conversion gain, IF swing, noise figure, oscillation frequency, output power, phase noise, tuning range | Open-sourced data/code on GitHub per paper; exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000048) | Relevant for learning analog performance mappings and inverse design ideas that could inform board-level analog block selection, but not directly a PCB schematic/layout benchmark. |
| PCBSchemaGen | Zou et al. 2025 | PCB | 23 PCB schematic-generation tasks; curated library with 18 symbols, 21 footprints, 14 datasheets | Pass@k (k=1,5), topology verification via subgraph isomorphism, ERC/syntax checks, semantic constraint checks, executable SKiDL/KiCad-ready outputs | Open-sourced KG/benchmark on GitHub per paper; exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000035) | The most directly relevant benchmark for an open-hardware control-PCB generative pipeline: board-level, datasheet-grounded, KiCad/SKiDL-compatible, and checks topology rather than text similarity alone. |
| TuRTLe | Garcia-Gasulla et al. 2025 | HDL Generation / Unified Evaluation | Unified framework integrating benchmarks such as VerilogEval and RTLLM | STX (syntax), FNC (functional), SYN (synthesis) as PASS@1 stages; post-synthesis PPA-score from OpenLANE/SKY130A | Open-source framework reported in paper; exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (pqac-00000011) | Useful because it adds synthesis-aware and PPA-aware evaluation, closer to practical deployment. Analogous board-level frameworks should similarly add ERC/DRC/BOM/manufacturability checks. |
| ChiPBench | Wang et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing / End-to-End Chip Placement | 20 circuits from CPU, GPU, and microcontroller domains | End-to-end final PPA rather than only surrogate placement metrics; compares intermediate vs final outcomes | Open benchmark reported by authors; paper states benchmark availability, exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (from paper search results) | Important cautionary benchmark: surrogate placement metrics can misalign with final chip QoR. The same lesson likely applies to PCB generation, where schematic correctness alone may mispredict routability or manufacturability. |


*Table: This table summarizes major open datasets, benchmark suites, and evaluation frameworks used in machine-learning and generative EDA through 2025. function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table summarizing the key evaluation metrics used across different EDA domains. Columns: Domain, Key Metrics, Description, Relevance to PCB Generation. Include rows for:
1. HDL/RTL Generation - pass@k, synthesizability, PPA - functional correctness via simulation, synthesis feasibility, area/power/delay
2. Placement - HPWL, congestion, density, timing slack (WNS/TNS) - half-perimeter wirelength, routing demand vs capacity, cell overlap
3. Routing - wirelength, DRC violations, congestion overflow - routed wirelength, design rule check violations
4. Analog Circuit Synthesis - gain, bandwidth, power, noise figure, PVT compliance - circuit performance specifications across corners
5. PCB Schematic Generation - Pass@k, topology verification, ERC, DRC - topological correctness, electrical rule compliance, design rule compliance
6. End-to-End Chip Design - final PPA, timing closure, DRC-clean GDSII - holistic post-route metrics"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Domain | Key Metrics | Description | Relevance to PCB Generation |
|---|---|---|---|
| HDL/RTL Generation | pass@k; functional pass rate; synthesizability; PPA | Measures whether any of *k* samples pass simulation/testbench checks, whether generated RTL compiles and synthesizes, and optionally whether post-synthesis area/power/delay are acceptable rather than merely syntactically valid (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000011). | Relevant for open-hardware control boards that include FPGA/CPLD logic, soft peripherals, or digital glue logic; less directly useful for PCB-only pipelines because it does not assess connectors, footprints, ERC, or manufacturability. |
| Placement | HPWL; congestion; density; timing slack (WNS/TNS) | HPWL approximates interconnect cost before detailed routing; congestion compares routing demand to routing capacity; density/legalization captures overlap and spread; WNS/TNS quantify timing closure risk after placement (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000025). | Provides a strong methodological template for evaluating board component placement quality, especially compactness and routability, but chip placement metrics do not map cleanly to board-level assembly, creepage, or connector accessibility. |
| Routing | routed wirelength; DRC violations; congestion overflow; runtime | Evaluates the quality of routed solutions by total route length, whether design-rule violations remain, how much routing demand exceeds available resources, and often solve/runtime cost in contest settings (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000024). | Highly relevant to PCB generation because board autorouters also need short routes, low overflow, and DRC-clean outputs; however PCB routing additionally needs layer assignment, EMI, thermal, return-current, and manufacturability considerations not captured in chip-routing benchmarks. |
| Analog Circuit Synthesis | gain; bandwidth; power; noise figure; area; PVT compliance | Assesses whether synthesized analog circuits meet electrical specifications across process, voltage, and temperature corners using DC/AC/PZ/transient analyses and multi-objective optimization targets (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000046). | Relevant for control-PCB subcircuits such as sensor front ends, regulators, references, and filters; still mostly IC-centric, so discrete-component tolerances, package parasitics, and board parasitics remain underrepresented. |
| PCB Schematic Generation | Pass@k; topology verification; ERC; DRC; semantic constraint checks | Measures whether generated schematics are executable and correct at the graph/topology level, pass electrical-rule checks, satisfy pin-role and datasheet constraints, and can be iteratively repaired through verifier feedback (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000035). | This is the most directly relevant metric family for open-hardware control-PCB generation because it checks whether a produced schematic is actually electrically and structurally valid, not just textually plausible. |
| End-to-End Chip Design | final PPA; timing closure; DRC-clean GDSII; signoff success | Evaluates the final post-route outcome instead of surrogate intermediate metrics, asking whether the generated design closes timing, satisfies physical verification, and delivers acceptable final QoR/signoff artifacts (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000043). | Extremely important as a lesson for PCB evaluation: schematic-level or placement-level success is insufficient unless the full pipeline yields a manufacturable, rule-clean, and functionally testable board release package. |


*Table: This table summarizes the main evaluation metrics used across HDL generation, physical design, analog synthesis, PCB schematic generation, and end-to-end chip design. It is useful for mapping chip-level evaluation practice onto the still immature evaluation landscape for generative open-hardware PCB pipelines.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00, artifact-01]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038, pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000047, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000049, pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055]

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

query.title: Query

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Survey the datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation methodologies used in machine-learning and generative electronic design automation (EDA) as of 2025, spanning both PCB-level and chip-level design. Cover: (a) circuit / netlist / schematic datasets and benchmark suites (e.g., VerilogEval, RTLLM, MCNC/ISPD/ICCAD placement and routing benchmarks, CircuitNet, the Open Circuit Benchmark / analog topology datasets, any open KiCad/PCB corpora), (b) metrics for placement, routing, schematic generation, and HDL generation (wirelength, congestion, DRC violations, functional pass rate, synthesizability), and (c) the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design (lack of standardized, manufacturable, open PCB datasets; reproducibility concerns). For each dataset/benchmark give a full citation, a 3-5 sentence description, license/availability, and its relevance to evaluating a generative pipeline for open-hardware control PCBs. Aim for ~10-15 references.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

function.name: paper_search

messages.content:
Retrieved Papers:
## 1. VerilogEval: Evaluating Large Language Models for Verilog Code Generation
Valid Text Names:
'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 1-2', 'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 2-3', 'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4', 'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 4-5', 'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 5-7', 'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 7-8', 'liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 8-8'

BibTex:
@article{liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge,
    author = "Liu, Mingjie and Pinckney, Nathaniel and Khailany, Brucek and Ren, Haoxing",
    title = "VerilogEval: Evaluating Large Language Models for Verilog Code Generation",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2309.07544",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2309.07544",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.07544",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
The increasing popularity of large language models (LLMs) has paved the way for their application in diverse domains. This paper proposes a benchmarking framework tailored specifically for evaluating LLM performance in the context of Verilog code generation for hardware design and verification. We present a comprehensive evaluation dataset consisting of 156 problems from the Verilog instructional website HDLBits. The evaluation set consists of a diverse set of Verilog code generation tasks, ranging from simple combinational circuits to complex finite state machines. The Verilog code completions can be automatically tested for functional correctness by comparing the transient simulation outputs of the generated design with a golden solution. We also demonstrate that the Verilog code generation capability of pretrained language models could be improved with supervised fine-tuning by bootstrapping with LLM generated synthetic problem-code pairs.

Relevant Snippet:
… a benchmarking framework tailored specifically for evaluating LLM performance in the context of Verilog code generation … and reproducible evaluation of LLM Verilog code generation. …


---

## 2. Revisiting VerilogEval: A Year of Improvements in Large-Language Models for Hardware Code Generation
Valid Text Names:
'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 1-3', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 3-5', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 5-8', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 8-11', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 11-12', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 12-15', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 15-17', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 17-19', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 19-21', 'pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 21-21'

BibTex:
@article{pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala,
    author = "Pinckney, Nathaniel and Batten, Christopher and Liu, Mingjie and Ren, Haoxing and Khailany, Brucek",
    title = "Revisiting VerilogEval: A Year of Improvements in Large-Language Models for Hardware Code Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.1145/3718088",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1145/3718088",
    publisher = "Association for Computing Machinery (ACM)",
    issn = "1084-4309"
}


Abstract:
The application of large-language models (LLMs) to digital hardware code generation is an emerging field, with most LLMs primarily trained on natural language and software code. Hardware code like Verilog constitutes a small portion of training data, and few hardware benchmarks exist. The open-source VerilogEval benchmark, released in November 2023, provided a consistent evaluation framework for LLMs on code completion tasks. Since then, both commercial and open models have seen significant development. In this work, we evaluate new commercial and open models since VerilogEval’s original release—including GPT-4o, GPT-4 Turbo, Llama3.1 (8B/70B/405B), Llama3 70B, Mistral Large, DeepSeek Coder (33B and 6.7B), CodeGemma 7B, and RTL-Coder—against an improved VerilogEval benchmark suite. We find measurable improvements in state-of-the-art models: GPT-4o achieves a 63% pass rate on specification-to-RTL tasks. The recently released and open Llama3.1 405B achieves a 58% pass rate, almost matching GPT-4o, while the smaller domain-specific RTL-Coder 6.7B models achieve an impressive 34% pass rate. Additionally, we enhance VerilogEval’s infrastructure by automatically classifying failures, introducing in-context learning support, and extending the tasks to specification-to-RTL translation. We find that prompt engineering remains crucial for achieving good pass rates and varies widely with model and task. A benchmark infrastructure that allows for prompt engineering and failure analysis is essential for continued model development and deployment.

Abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized code generation, achieving exceptional results on various established benchmarking frameworks. However, concerns about data contamination—where benchmark data inadvertently leaks into pre-training or fine-tuning datasets—raise questions about the validity of these evaluations. While this issue is known, limiting the industrial adoption of LLM-driven software engineering, hardware coding has received little to no attention regarding these risks. For the first time, we analyze state-of-the-art (SOTA) evaluation frameworks for Verilog code generation (VerilogEval and RTLLM), using established methods for contamination detection (CCD and Min-K% Prob). We cover SOTA commercial and open-source LLMs (CodeGen2.5, Minitron 4b, Mistral 7b, phi-4 mini, LLaMA-{1,2,3.1}, GPT- {2,3.5,4o} , Deepseek-Coder, and CodeQwen 1.5), in baseline and fine-tuned models (RTLCoder and Verigen). Our study confirms that data contamination is a critical concern. We explore mitigations and the resulting trade-offs for code quality vs fairness (i.e., reducing contamination toward unbiased benchmarking). Codes are open-sourced at https://github.com/DfX-NYUAD/VeriContaminated.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Large language models (LLMs) are emerging as powerful tools for hardware design, with recent work exploring their ability to generate register-transfer level (RTL) code directly from natural-language specifications. This paper provides a survey and evaluation of LLM-based RTL generation. We review twenty-six published efforts, covering techniques such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, retrieval-augmented prompting, and multi-agent orchestration, and we analyze their contributions across eight methodological dimensions including debugging support, post-RTL metrics, and benchmark development. Building on this review, we experimentally evaluate frontier commercial models---GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1-mini, and Claude Sonnet 4---on the VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks under both single-shot and lightweight agentic settings. Results show that these models achieve up to 89.74% on VerilogEval and 96.08% on RTLLM, matching or exceeding prior domain-specific pipelines without specialized fine-tuning. Detailed failure analysis reveals systematic error modes, including FSM mis-sequencing, handshake drift, blocking vs. non-blocking misuse, and state-space oversimplification. Finally, we outline a forward-looking research roadmap toward natural-language-to-SoC design, emphasizing controlled specification schemas, open benchmarks and flows, PPA-in-the-loop feedback, and modular assurance frameworks. Together, this work provides both a critical synthesis of recent advances and a baseline evaluation of frontier LLMs, highlighting opportunities and challenges in moving toward AI-native electronic design automation.</jats:p>

## 6. VeriMind: Agentic LLM for Automated Verilog Generation with a Novel Evaluation Metric
Valid Text Names:
'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 1-2', 'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 2-3', 'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 3-4', 'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 4-5', 'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 5-6', 'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 6-7', 'nadimi2503verimindagenticllm pages 7-10'

    title = "VeriMind: Agentic LLM for Automated Verilog Generation with a Novel Evaluation Metric",
    year = "2503",

    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2503.16514",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.16514"

Abstract:
Designing Verilog modules requires meticulous attention to correctness, efficiency, and adherence to design specifications. However, manually writing Verilog code remains a complex and time-consuming task that demands both expert knowledge and iterative refinement. Leveraging recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) and their structured text generation capabilities, we propose VeriMind, an agentic LLM framework for Verilog code generation that significantly automates and optimizes the synthesis process. Unlike traditional LLM-based code generators, VeriMind employs a structured reasoning approach: given a user-provided prompt describing design requirements, the system first formulates a detailed train of thought before the final Verilog code is generated. This multi-step methodology enhances interpretability, accuracy, and adaptability in hardware design. In addition, we introduce a novel evaluation metric-pass@ARC-which combines the conventional pass@k measure with Average Refinement Cycles (ARC) to capture both success rate and the efficiency of iterative refinement. Experimental results on diverse hardware design tasks demonstrated that our approach achieved up to $8.3\%$ improvement on pass@k metric and $8.1\%$ on pass@ARC metric. These findings underscore the transformative potential of agentic LLMs in automated hardware design, RTL development, and digital system synthesis.

    year = "2025",

    issn = "1084-4309"
}


Abstract:
Traditionally, digital hardware designs are written in the Verilog hardware description language (HDL) and debugged manually by engineers. This can be time-consuming and error-prone for complex designs. Large Language Models (LLMs) are emerging as a potential tool to help generate fully functioning HDL code, but most works have focused on generation in the single-shot capacity: i.e., run and evaluate, a process that does not leverage debugging and, as such, does not adequately reflect a realistic development process. In this work, we evaluate the ability of LLMs to leverage feedback from electronic design automation (EDA) tools to fix mistakes in their own generated Verilog. To accomplish this, we present an open-source, highly customizable framework, AutoChip, which combines conversational LLMs with the output from Verilog compilers and simulations to iteratively generate and repair Verilog. To determine the success of these LLMs we leverage the VerilogEval benchmark set. We evaluate four state-of-the-art conversational LLMs, focusing on readily accessible commercial models. EDA tool feedback proved to be consistently more effective than zero-shot prompting only with GPT-4o, the most computationally complex model we evaluated. In the best case, we observed a 5.8% increase in the number of successful designs with a 34.2% decrease in cost over the best zero-shot results. Mixing smaller models with this larger model at the end of the feedback iterations resulted in equally as much success as with GPT-4o using feedback, but incurred 41.9% lower cost (corresponding to an overall decrease in cost over zero-shot by 89.6%).

Abstract:
Recently, the use of large language models (LLMs) for software code generation, e.g., C/C++ and Python, has proven a great success. However, LLMs still suffer from low syntactic and functional correctness when it comes to the generation of register-transfer level (RTL) code, such as Verilog. To address this issue, in this paper, we develop AutoVCoder, a systematic open-source framework that significantly improves the LLMs' correctness of generating Verilog code and enhances the quality of its output at the same time. Our framework integrates three novel techniques, including a high-quality hardware dataset generation approach, a two-round LLM fine-tuning method and a domain-specific retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) mechanism. Experimental results demonstrate that AutoVCoder outperforms both industrial and academic LLMs in Verilog code generation. Code and models are available at https://github.com/sjtu-zhao-lab/AutoVCoder.

Abstract:
With the unprecedented advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), their application domains have expanded to include code generation tasks across various programming languages. While significant progress has been made in enhancing LLMs for popular programming languages, there exists a notable gap in comprehensive evaluation frameworks tailored for Hardware Description Languages (HDLs), particularly VHDL. This paper addresses this gap by introducing a comprehensive evaluation framework designed specifically for assessing LLM performance in VHDL code generation task. We construct a dataset for evaluating LLMs on VHDL code generation task. This dataset is constructed by translating a collection of Verilog evaluation problems to VHDL and aggregating publicly available VHDL problems, resulting in a total of 202 problems. To assess the functional correctness of the generated VHDL code, we utilize a curated set of self-verifying testbenches specifically designed for those aggregated VHDL problem set. We conduct an initial evaluation of different LLMs and their variants, including zero-shot code generation, in-context learning (ICL), and Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods. Our findings underscore the considerable challenges faced by existing LLMs in VHDL code generation, revealing significant scope for improvement. This study emphasizes the necessity of supervised fine-tuning code generation models specifically for VHDL, offering potential benefits to VHDL designers seeking efficient code generation solutions.

Abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have been applied to various hardware design tasks, including Verilog code generation, EDA tool scripting, and RTL bug fixing. Despite this extensive exploration, LLMs are yet to be used for the task of post-synthesis metric reasoning and estimation of HDL designs. In this paper, we assess the ability of LLMs to reason about post-synthesis metrics of Verilog designs. We introduce MetRex, a large-scale dataset comprising 25,868 Verilog HDL designs and their corresponding post-synthesis metrics, namely area, delay, and static power. MetRex incorporates a Chain of Thought (CoT) template to enhance LLMs' reasoning about these metrics. Extensive experiments show that Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) boosts the LLM's reasoning capabilities on average by 37.0%, 25.3%, and 25.7% on the area, delay, and static power, respectively. While SFT improves performance on our benchmark, it remains far from achieving optimal results, especially on complex problems. Comparing to state-of-the-art regression models, our approach delivers accurate post-synthesis predictions for 17.4% more designs (within a 5% error margin), in addition to offering a 1.7x speedup by eliminating the need for pre-processing. This work lays the groundwork for advancing LLM-based Verilog code metric reasoning.

## 16. Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems: A Next-Generation Benchmark Dataset for Evaluating Large Language Models and Agents on RTL Design and Verification
Valid Text Names:
'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 1-3', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 3-4', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 4-6', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 6-8', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 8-10', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 10-12', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 12-13', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 13-14', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 14-15', 'pinckney2025comprehensiveverilogdesign pages 15-16'

    title = "Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems: A Next-Generation Benchmark Dataset for Evaluating Large Language Models and Agents on RTL Design and Verification",

}


Abstract:
We present the Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) benchmark, a new dataset and infrastructure to advance LLM and agent research in hardware design and verification. CVDP includes 783 problems across 13 task categories, covering RTL generation, verification, debugging, specification alignment, and technical Q&A authored by experienced hardware engineers. Problems are offered in both non-agentic and agentic formats. The benchmark introduces more realistic and challenging contexts than prior work, with state-of-the-art models achieving no more than 34% pass@1 on code generation. Agentic tasks$\unicode{x2013}$especially those involving RTL reuse and verification$\unicode{x2013}$are particularly difficult. Evaluation uses open-source tools and model scoring infrastructure, with comprehension tasks assessed via BLEU and LLM-based judging. CVDP reveals substantial gaps in current model capabilities, underscoring the need for continued research toward robust, real-world hardware design automation.

## 1. RTLLM: An Open-Source Benchmark for Design RTL Generation with Large Language Model
Valid Text Names:
'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-1', 'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3', 'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 3-4', 'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 4-5', 'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 5-6', 'lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 6-6'

    author = "Lu, Yao and Liu, Shang and Zhang, Qijun and Xie, Zhiyao",
    title = "RTLLM: An Open-Source Benchmark for Design RTL Generation with Large Language Model",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "2024 29th Asia and South Pacific Design Automation Conference (ASP-DAC)",
    pages = "722-727",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2308.05345",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.05345",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Inspired by the recent success of large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, researchers start to explore the adoption of LLMs for agile hardware design, such as generating design RTL based on natural-language instructions. However, in existing works, their target designs are all relatively simple and in a small scale, and proposed by the authors themselves, making a fair comparison among different LLM solutions challenging. In addition, many prior works only focus on the design correctness, without evaluating the design qualities of generated design RTL. In this work, we propose an open-source benchmark named RTLLM, for generating design RTL with natural language instructions. To systematically evaluate the auto-generated design RTL, we summarized three progressive goals, named syntax goal, functionality goal, and design quality goal. This benchmark can automatically provide a quantitative evaluation of any given LLM-based solution. Furthermore, we propose an easy-to-use yet surprisingly effective prompt engineering technique named self-planning, which proves to significantly boost the performance of GPT-3.5 in our proposed benchmark.

## 2. OpenLLM-RTL: Open Dataset and Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design RTL Generation
Valid Text Names:
'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 1-2', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 2-3', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 3-4', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 4-5', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 5-6', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 6-7', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 7-8', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 8-9', 'liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 9-9'

    author = "Liu, Shang and Lu, Yao and Fang, Wenji and Li, Mengming and Xie, Zhiyao",

    year = "2024",
    journal = "Proceedings of the 43rd IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer-Aided Design",
    pages = "1-9",

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2503.15112",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.15112",
    publisher = "ACM"
}


Abstract:
The automated generation of design RTL based on large language model (LLM) and natural language instructions has demonstrated great potential in agile circuit design. However, the lack of datasets and benchmarks in the public domain prevents the development and fair evaluation of LLM solutions. This paper highlights our latest advances in open datasets and benchmarks from three perspectives: (1) RTLLM 2.0, an updated benchmark assessing LLM's capability in design RTL generation. The benchmark is augmented to 50 hand-crafted designs. Each design provides the design description, test cases, and a correct RTL code. (2) AssertEval, an open-source benchmark assessing the LLM's assertion generation capabilities for RTL verification. The benchmark includes 18 designs, each providing specification, signal definition, and correct RTL code. (3) RTLCoder-Data, an extended open-source dataset with 80K instruction-code data samples. Moreover, we propose a new verification-based method to verify the functionality correctness of training data samples. Based on this technique, we further release a dataset with 7K verified high-quality samples. These three studies are integrated into one framework, providing off-the-shelf support for the development and evaluation of LLMs for RTL code generation and verification. Finally, extensive experiments indicate that LLM performance can be boosted by enlarging the training dataset, improving data quality, and improving the training scheme.

## 4. TuRTLe: A Unified Evaluation of LLMs for RTL Generation
Valid Text Names:
'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 1-2', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 2-3', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 4-5', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 4-4', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 5-6', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 6-7', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 7-8', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 7-7', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 8-9', 'garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 9-11'

    title = "TuRTLe: A Unified Evaluation of LLMs for RTL Generation",
    year = "2025",

    volume = "abs/2504.01986",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2504.01986",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.01986"
}


Abstract:
The rapid advancements in LLMs have driven the adoption of generative AI in various domains, including Electronic Design Automation (EDA). Unlike traditional software development, EDA presents unique challenges, as generated RTL code must not only be syntactically correct and functionally accurate but also synthesizable by hardware generators while meeting performance, power, and area constraints. These additional requirements introduce complexities that existing code-generation benchmarks often fail to capture, limiting their effectiveness in evaluating LLMs for RTL generation. To address this gap, we propose TuRTLe, a unified evaluation framework designed to systematically assess LLMs across key RTL generation tasks. TuRTLe integrates multiple existing benchmarks and automates the evaluation process, enabling a comprehensive assessment of LLM performance in syntax correctness, functional correctness, synthesis, PPA optimization, and exact line completion. Using this framework, we benchmark a diverse set of open LLMs and analyze their strengths and weaknesses in EDA-specific tasks. Our results show that reasoning-based models, such as DeepSeek R1, consistently outperform others across multiple evaluation criteria, but at the cost of increased computational overhead and inference latency. Additionally, base models are better suited in module completion tasks, while instruct-tuned models perform better in specification-to-RTL tasks.

## 5. ScaleRTL: Scaling LLMs with Reasoning Data and Test-Time Compute for Accurate RTL Code Generation
Valid Text Names:
'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 1-2', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 2-2', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 2-3', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 3-4', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 3-3', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 4-5', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 5-6', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 6-6', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 6-7', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 7-8', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 8-9', 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 9-9'

    author = "Deng, Chenhui and Tsai, Yun-Da and Liu, Guan-Ting and Yu, Zhongzhi and Ren, Haoxing",
    title = "ScaleRTL: Scaling LLMs with Reasoning Data and Test-Time Compute for Accurate RTL Code Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2506.05566",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2506.05566",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.05566"
}


Abstract:
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled near-human performance on software coding benchmarks, but their effectiveness in RTL code generation remains limited due to the scarcity of high-quality training data. While prior efforts have fine-tuned LLMs for RTL tasks, they do not fundamentally overcome the data bottleneck and lack support for test-time scaling due to their non-reasoning nature. In this work, we introduce ScaleRTL, the first reasoning LLM for RTL coding that scales up both high-quality reasoning data and test-time compute. Specifically, we curate a diverse set of long chain-of-thought reasoning traces averaging 56K tokens each, resulting in a dataset of 3.5B tokens that captures rich RTL knowledge. Fine-tuning a general-purpose reasoning model on this corpus yields ScaleRTL that is capable of deep RTL reasoning. Subsequently, we further enhance the performance of ScaleRTL through a novel test-time scaling strategy that extends the reasoning process via iteratively reflecting on and self-correcting previous reasoning steps. Experimental results show that ScaleRTL achieves state-of-the-art performance on VerilogEval and RTLLM, outperforming 18 competitive baselines by up to 18.4% on VerilogEval and 12.7% on RTLLM.

## 7. RTLCoder: Outperforming GPT-3.5 in Design RTL Generation with Our Open-Source Dataset and Lightweight Solution
Valid Text Names:
