However, it evaluates IC-level analog synthesis, not discrete-component PCB schematics or board manufacturability. |
| AICircuit | Mehradfar et al. 2024 | Analog | 7 basic circuits + 2 heterogeneous wireless transceiver systems | Power consumption, bandwidth, gain, conversion gain, IF swing, noise figure, oscillation frequency, output power, phase noise, tuning range | Open-sourced data/code on GitHub per paper; exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 2-4, mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7, mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2) | Relevant for learning analog performance mappings and inverse design ideas that could inform board-level analog block selection, but not directly a PCB schematic/layout benchmark. |
| PCBSchemaGen | Zou et al. 2025 | PCB | 23 PCB schematic-generation tasks; curated library with 18 symbols, 21 footprints, 14 datasheets | Pass@k (k=1,5), topology verification via subgraph isomorphism, ERC/syntax checks, semantic constraint checks, executable SKiDL/KiCad-ready outputs | Open-sourced KG/benchmark on GitHub per paper; exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7) | The most directly relevant benchmark for an open-hardware control-PCB generative pipeline: board-level, datasheet-grounded, KiCad/SKiDL-compatible, and checks topology rather than text similarity alone. |
| TuRTLe | Garcia-Gasulla et al. 2025 | HDL Generation / Unified Evaluation | Unified framework integrating benchmarks such as VerilogEval and RTLLM | STX (syntax), FNC (functional), SYN (synthesis) as PASS@1 stages; post-synthesis PPA-score from OpenLANE/SKY130A | Open-source framework reported in paper; exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4) | Useful because it adds synthesis-aware and PPA-aware evaluation, closer to practical deployment. Analogous board-level frameworks should similarly add ERC/DRC/BOM/manufacturability checks. |
| ChiPBench | Wang et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing / End-to-End Chip Placement | 20 circuits from CPU, GPU, and microcontroller domains | End-to-end final PPA rather than only surrogate placement metrics; compares intermediate vs final outcomes | Open benchmark reported by authors; paper states benchmark availability, exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (from paper search results) | Important cautionary benchmark: surrogate placement metrics can misalign with final chip QoR. The same lesson likely applies to PCB generation, where schematic correctness alone may mispredict routability or manufacturability. |


*Table: This table summarizes major open datasets, benchmark suites, and evaluation frameworks used in machine-learning and generative EDA through 2025. It highlights scale, metrics, availability, and how each resource does—or does not—support evaluation of an open-hardware control-PCB generation pipeline.*

## 2. HDL / RTL Generation Benchmarks

### 2.1 VerilogEval

**Citation:** Liu, M., Pinckney, N., Khailany, B., & Ren, H. (2023). VerilogEval: Evaluating Large Language Models for Verilog Code Generation. *ArXiv*, abs/2309.07544.

VerilogEval is a benchmark comprising 156 Verilog code-generation problems sourced from the HDLBits instructional website, ranging from simple combinational circuits to complex finite state machines (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 1-2). It provides two evaluation tracks—VerilogEval-machine (143 validated problems with auto-generated descriptions) and VerilogEval-human (156 problems with manually crafted descriptions) (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4). Functional correctness is evaluated using the pass@k metric, where a problem is considered solved if at least one of *k* sampled LLM outputs passes simulation against a golden reference using the open-source Icarus Verilog simulator (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4, liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 5-7). The benchmark also includes 8,502 problem-code training pairs mined from GitHub Verilog repositories for supervised fine-tuning experiments (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 5-7). The updated VerilogEval v2 extends evaluation to specification-to-RTL tasks, adds in-context learning support, and provides automated failure classification (pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 1-3). The benchmark is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 2-3). **Relevance:** Directly applicable for evaluating generation of controller/FPGA logic modules that may accompany open control PCBs, though it does not assess board-level concerns such as component selection, connectivity, or manufacturability.

### 2.2 RTLLM and RTLLM 2.0

**Citation:** Lu, Y., Liu, S., Zhang, Q., & Xie, Z. (2024). RTLLM: An Open-Source Benchmark for Design RTL Generation with Large Language Model. *ASP-DAC 2024*, pp. 722–727. Liu, S., Lu, Y., Fang, W., Li, M., & Xie, Z. (2024). OpenLLM-RTL: Open Dataset and Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design RTL Generation. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–9.

RTLLM collects 30 common digital designs (11 arithmetic and 19 logic) with three progressive evaluation goals: a syntax goal (verifiable via synthesis into a netlist), a functionality goal (verifiable via testbench simulation), and a design quality goal (evaluating post-synthesis PPA against human-designed references) (lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3, lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 3-4). RTLLM 2.0 expands the suite to 50 hand-crafted designs covering a broader range of complexities and is accompanied by AssertEval (18 designs for assertion generation) and RTLCoder-Data (80K training samples, including a 7K verified high-quality subset) (liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 1-2, liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 2-3). Each design provides a natural-language description, testbench, and correct reference RTL. Available at https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM (lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3). **Relevance:** More relevant than VerilogEval for evaluating synthesizable digital peripherals or glue logic that may reside on an open control board, but still misses board-level connectivity, ERC, and component/package constraints.

### 2.3 CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems)

**Citation:** Pinckney, N. et al. (2025). Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems: A Next-Generation Benchmark Dataset for Evaluating Large Language Models and Agents on RTL Design and Verification. *ArXiv*, abs/2506.14074.

CVDP is a next-generation benchmark comprising 783 problems across 13 task categories authored by experienced hardware engineers, covering RTL generation, verification, debugging, specification alignment, and technical Q&A. It supports both non-agentic and agentic task formats, with state-of-the-art models achieving no more than 34% pass@1 on code generation. **Relevance:** Especially valuable for evaluating agentic HDL workflows involving iterative debug and verification loops, analogous to how a PCB design agent would need to iterate on ERC/DRC feedback.

### 2.4 VeriContaminated

**Citation:** Wang, Z. et al. (2025). VeriContaminated: Assessing LLM-Driven Verilog Coding for Data Contamination. *IEEE ICLAD 2025*, pp. 117–123.

VeriContaminated is a meta-evaluation study that analyzes data contamination across VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks for state-of-the-art LLMs, finding near-100% contamination rates for some commercial models (wang2025vericontaminatedassessingllmdriven pages 1-2). It employs detection methods including CCD and Min-K% Prob and explores mitigations and their trade-offs for code quality versus fairness. **Relevance:** A critical cautionary resource for any open-hardware generative pipeline—apparent HDL generation success may be inflated by benchmark data leaking into LLM pre-training corpora, undermining reproducibility and trustworthiness of evaluations.

### 2.5 TuRTLe

**Citation:** Garcia-Gasulla, D. et al. (2025). TuRTLe: A Unified Evaluation of LLMs for RTL Generation. *ArXiv*, abs/2504.01986.

TuRTLe is a unified evaluation framework that integrates multiple existing benchmarks (including VerilogEval and RTLLM) and adds synthesis-aware scoring. It evaluates LLMs across cascaded stages—syntax (STX), functional correctness (FNC), and synthesizability (SYN)—using PASS@1, and adds a post-synthesis PPA-score derived from OpenLANE and the open-source SKY130A PDK (garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4). **Relevance:** By incorporating synthesis and PPA into the evaluation loop, TuRTLe provides a methodological precedent for PCB benchmarks that should similarly go beyond schematic correctness to assess ERC, DRC, BOM validity, and manufacturability.

## 3. Chip-Level Placement and Routing Benchmarks

### 3.1 CircuitNet and CircuitNet 2.0

**Citation:** Chai, Z. et al. (2022). CircuitNet: An Open-Source Dataset for Machine Learning Applications in Electronic Design Automation. *arXiv:2208.01040*. Jiang, X. et al. (2024). CircuitNet 2.0: An Advanced Dataset for Promoting Machine Learning Innovations in Realistic Chip Design Environment.

CircuitNet is the first large-scale open-source dataset for ML in VLSI physical design, built from RISC-V designs mapped to 28nm technology with 10,242 post-flow layouts from 12,960 backend design runs (chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 1-2, chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 2-2). It supports three prediction tasks—congestion, DRC violation, and IR drop—evaluated using NRMSE and SSIM for congestion, and ROC-AUC/PR-AUC for DRC and IR drop (chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 2-2). CircuitNet 2.0 extends coverage to million-gate designs (CPU, GPU, AI chips) at 14nm FinFET and adds timing prediction tasks (jiang2024circuitnet2.0an pages 1-3, jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3). Available at https://circuitnet.github.io/ (jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3). **Relevance:** The strongest open chip-physical-design dataset family; it informs evaluation methodology for congestion/DRC prediction that could be adapted for PCB routing quality assessment, though it does not directly address board-level constraints.

### 3.2 ISPD/ICCAD Contest Benchmark Suites

**Citation:** Various, 2005–2025. See Kahng, A.B. (2021). Advancing Placement. *ISPD 2021*, pp. 15–22; and individual contest proceedings.

The ISPD and ICCAD conferences have hosted 14+ placement and routing contests since 2005, with evolving themes including routability-driven placement (ISPD 2011, DAC 2012), detailed placement (ICCAD 2013), timing-driven placement (ICCAD 2014/2015), and large-scale global routing (ISPD 2025) (kahng2021advancingplacement pages 5-6, jiang2023invitedpaperaccelerating pages 8-9). Standard metrics include half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL), routing congestion/overflow, timing slack (WNS/TNS), legality, and runtime (kahng2021advancingplacement pages 2-3, jiang2023invitedpaperaccelerating pages 8-9). Benchmarks are typically distributed in Bookshelf or LEF/DEF formats and are publicly available through conference archives (bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 3-4). **Relevance:** Essential methodological precedent for chip placement/routing evaluation; the benchmarking culture and progressive metric design serve as a model for establishing equivalent PCB benchmark contests, though chip-level benchmarks do not capture board-level assembly, EMI, or thermal constraints.

### 3.3 EDALearn

**Citation:** Pan, J., Chang, C.-C., Xie, Z., Chen, Y., & Li, H.H. (2024). EDALearn: A Comprehensive RTL-to-Signoff EDA Benchmark for Democratized and Reproducible ML for EDA Research. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–8.

EDALearn is the first holistic, open-source benchmark suite spanning the complete RTL-to-signoff EDA flow, targeting FreePDK 45nm and ASAP 7nm technology nodes (pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 1-2, pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 3-4). It aggregates practical VLSI instances from multiple public sources (IWLS, BOOM/Chipyard, OpenCores, ISCAS, ITC'99) and provides standardized evaluation for routability prediction, IR-drop prediction, and cross-stage analysis with both image-like and vector-based features. **Relevance:** A strong template for how an open PCB benchmark should connect source design intent through intermediate stages to final manufacturable artifacts, supporting reproducibility and cross-technology transfer.

### 3.4 EDA-Schema-V2

**Citation:** Shrestha, P. et al. (2025). EDA-Schema-V2: A Multimodal Schema, Open Datasets, and Benchmarks for Machine Learning in Digital Physical Design.

EDA-Schema-V2 provides approximately 7,800 physical design instances from 18 IWLS'05 benchmark circuits across four open PDKs (SkyWater 130nm, Nangate 45nm, IHP SG13G2 130nm, ASAP 7nm), encompassing over 275 million gates, 75 million nets, and 36 million extracted timing paths (shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 1-3, shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 28-29). It offers stage-resolved representations from synthesis through detailed routing in a multimodal entity-relationship schema covering circuit graphs, spatial layout images, and QoR metrics. The dataset and schema are released on GitHub. **Relevance:** Particularly relevant methodologically as a multimodal, stage-aware, reproducible, and open benchmark—a strong model for how an open PCB benchmark could combine schematic, layout, netlist, images, and fabrication outputs in a unified schema.

## 4. Analog Circuit Datasets and Benchmarks

### 4.1 AnalogGym

**Citation:** Li, J. et al. (2024). AnalogGym: An Open and Practical Testing Suite for Analog Circuit Synthesis. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–9.

AnalogGym provides 30 circuit topologies across five categories: amplifiers (16), low-dropout regulators (6), sensing front ends (6), voltage references (3), and phase-locked loops (1) (li2024analoggymanopen pages 2-3). It supports comprehensive evaluation including DC, AC, pole-zero, and transient analyses across process-voltage-temperature (PVT) corners (SS/FF/SF/FS/TT, 1.08–1.32V, −40 to 125°C) (li2024analoggymanopen pages 3-4). Compatible with Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, and the open-source Ngspice simulator with the sky130 PDK for amplifier and LDO categories (li2024analoggymanopen pages 1-2). Available at https://github.com/CODA-Team/AnalogGym. **Relevance:** Directly relevant for analog portions of control PCBs (power rails, sensor front ends, voltage references), though it evaluates IC-level analog synthesis rather than discrete-component board schematics.

### 4.2 AICircuit

**Citation:** Mehradfar, A. et al. (2024). AICircuit: A Multi-Level Dataset and Benchmark for AI-Driven Analog Integrated Circuit Design. *ArXiv*, abs/2407.18272.

AICircuit comprises seven basic analog/RF circuits (common-source voltage amplifier, two-stage amplifier, cascode amplifier, LNA, power amplifier, VCO, and mixer) plus two complex heterogeneous wireless transceiver systems (mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 2-4, mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2). Data is generated via Cadence schematic-level parameter sweeps with commercial simulation, and performance metrics include DC power, bandwidth, gain, noise figure, phase noise, and output power (mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7). The dataset and code are open-sourced on GitHub (mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2). **Relevance:** Useful for learning analog performance mappings and inverse design concepts that could inform board-level analog block selection, but it is IC-centric rather than a PCB schematic/layout benchmark.

## 5. PCB-Level Datasets and Benchmarks

### 5.1 PCBSchemaGen

**Citation:** Zou, H., Han, P., Nazerian, E., & Huang, A.Q. (2025). PCBSchemaGen: Constraint-Guided Schematic Design via LLM for Printed Circuit Boards (PCB). *ArXiv*, abs/2602.00510.

PCBSchemaGen is the most directly relevant benchmark for evaluating generative PCB design pipelines. It provides 23 unique PCB schematic-generation tasks spanning analog, digital, and power domains, with a curated KiCad-compatible component library containing 18 symbols, 21 footprints, and 14 reference datasheets (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 26-28). Evaluation uses Pass@k (k=1,5 with n=15 trials and up to 3 self-correction retries), topology verification via subgraph isomorphism (VF2), ERC checks, and semantic constraint checking grounded in a datasheet-derived knowledge graph (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2). The framework outputs executable SKiDL Python code that produces KiCad-ready PCB layout files. The benchmark and knowledge graph are open-sourced on GitHub. **Relevance:** The most directly applicable resource for evaluating open-hardware control-PCB generation—it is board-level, datasheet-grounded, KiCad/SKiDL-compatible, and checks topology rather than text similarity alone.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics by Domain

The following table summarizes the key metrics employed across different EDA evaluation domains:

| Domain | Key Metrics | Description | Relevance to PCB Generation |
|---|---|---|---|
| HDL/RTL Generation | pass@k; functional pass rate; synthesizability; PPA | Measures whether any of *k* samples pass simulation/testbench checks, whether generated RTL compiles and synthesizes, and optionally whether post-synthesis area/power/delay are acceptable rather than merely syntactically valid (pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 1-3, lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3, garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4). | Relevant for open-hardware control boards that include FPGA/CPLD logic, soft peripherals, or digital glue logic; less directly useful for PCB-only pipelines because it does not assess connectors, footprints, ERC, or manufacturability. |
| Placement | HPWL; congestion; density; timing slack (WNS/TNS) | HPWL approximates interconnect cost before detailed routing; congestion compares routing demand to routing capacity; density/legalization captures overlap and spread; WNS/TNS quantify timing closure risk after placement (kahng2021advancingplacement pages 2-3, jiang2023invitedpaperaccelerating pages 8-9). | Provides a strong methodological template for evaluating board component placement quality, especially compactness and routability, but chip placement metrics do not map cleanly to board-level assembly, creepage, or connector accessibility. |
| Routing | routed wirelength; DRC violations; congestion overflow; runtime | Evaluates the quality of routed solutions by total route length, whether design-rule violations remain, how much routing demand exceeds available resources, and often solve/runtime cost in contest settings (chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 1-2, bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 3-4). | Highly relevant to PCB generation because board autorouters also need short routes, low overflow, and DRC-clean outputs; however PCB routing additionally needs layer assignment, EMI, thermal, return-current, and manufacturability considerations not captured in chip-routing benchmarks. |
| Analog Circuit Synthesis | gain; bandwidth; power; noise figure; area; PVT compliance | Assesses whether synthesized analog circuits meet electrical specifications across process, voltage, and temperature corners using DC/AC/PZ/transient analyses and multi-objective optimization targets (li2024analoggymanopen pages 2-3, li2024analoggymanopen pages 3-4, mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7). | Relevant for control-PCB subcircuits such as sensor front ends, regulators, references, and filters; still mostly IC-centric, so discrete-component tolerances, package parasitics, and board parasitics remain underrepresented. |
| PCB Schematic Generation | Pass@k; topology verification; ERC; DRC; semantic constraint checks | Measures whether generated schematics are executable and correct at the graph/topology level, pass electrical-rule checks, satisfy pin-role and datasheet constraints, and can be iteratively repaired through verifier feedback (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7). | This is the most directly relevant metric family for open-hardware control-PCB generation because it checks whether a produced schematic is actually electrically and structurally valid, not just textually plausible. |
| End-to-End Chip Design | final PPA; timing closure; DRC-clean GDSII; signoff success | Evaluates the final post-route outcome instead of surrogate intermediate metrics, asking whether the generated design closes timing, satisfies physical verification, and delivers acceptable final QoR/signoff artifacts (pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 1-2, chen2026reportfornsf pages 5-6). | Extremely important as a lesson for PCB evaluation: schematic-level or placement-level success is insufficient unless the full pipeline yields a manufacturable, rule-clean, and functionally testable board release package. |


*Table: This table summarizes the main evaluation metrics used across HDL generation, physical design, analog synthesis, PCB schematic generation, and end-to-end chip design. It is useful for mapping chip-level evaluation practice onto the still immature evaluation landscape for generative open-hardware PCB pipelines.*

## 7. Gaps and Challenges in Evaluating Generative Circuit/PCB Design

### 7.1 Lack of Standardized, Manufacturable, Open PCB Datasets

The most acute gap identified across the literature is the near-complete absence of large-scale, standardized, open PCB schematic and layout datasets suitable for training and evaluating generative models. As noted by multiple sources, "automated PCB schematic design remains unexplored due to the scarcity of open-source data and the absence of simulation-based verification" (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2). PCB schematic capture formats are vendor-specific, making large-scale corpus construction difficult (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3). Even at the chip level, "EDA and design data are limited and often not in the open domain," and generating ML-ready EDA data requires expensive and time-consuming flows that can take "weeks to months" per run, compounded by NDA restrictions that "prohibit the release of the original data" (chen2026reportfornsf pages 3-4, jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3). PCBSchemaGen's 23-task benchmark represents a pioneering but very small-scale effort; no "ImageNet-scale" open PCB dataset yet exists (chen2026reportfornsf pages 5-6).

### 7.2 Data Contamination and Benchmark Validity

Data contamination—where benchmark data inadvertently leaks into LLM pre-training corpora—is a critical concern for HDL generation benchmarks. VeriContaminated demonstrated near-100% contamination rates for some commercial LLMs on both VerilogEval and RTLLM, fundamentally undermining the validity of reported pass rates and limiting industrial adoption (wang2025vericontaminatedassessingllmdriven pages 1-2). The undisclosed nature of pre-training datasets, even for nominally open-source models, makes contamination detection essential but difficult. This concern extends directly to any PCB-level benchmarks that might be developed from publicly available KiCad repositories.

### 7.3 Surrogate vs. End-to-End Metrics

A recurring finding across chip-level work is that intermediate surrogate metrics frequently misalign with final design quality. ChiPBench demonstrated that even if a placement algorithm achieves dominant intermediate metrics, "the final PPA results are unsatisfactory" (from paper search results). At the HDL level, prior work critiques BLEU-like metrics noting they "do not correlate well with functional correctness" (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4). For PCB generation, this implies that evaluating only schematic correctness is insufficient—a complete evaluation must also verify that the generated design can be routed, passes DRC, has a valid BOM, and is actually manufacturable.

### 7.4 Reproducibility Concerns

Multiple sources highlight reproducibility as a systemic challenge. Proprietary PDKs, closed-source EDA tools, and restricted benchmark data limit who can reproduce experimental results (shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 1-3, jiang2024circuitnet2.0an pages 1-3). The NSF Workshop on AI for EDA explicitly calls for "open-source initiatives for developing shared datasets, tools, and benchmarks" to "enable reproducibility and innovation" (chen2026reportfornsf pages 3-4). Open toolchains such as OpenROAD, Ngspice, KiCad, and open PDKs (sky130, Nangate45, ASAP7) are enabling a more reproducible ecosystem, but coverage gaps remain—especially for advanced nodes and board-level design rules.

### 7.5 Missing Dimensions for PCB Evaluation

Board-level design introduces evaluation dimensions absent from chip-level benchmarks: component sourcing and BOM validation, mechanical constraints (connector placement, enclosure fit), thermal and EMI compliance, creepage/clearance requirements, return-current path analysis, and integration with firmware/software. Existing analog simulators like SPICE are insufficient for PCB-level correctness validation because they model idealized components rather than real IC packages with pin-level constraints (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2). No current benchmark comprehensively addresses these board-specific dimensions, representing the largest gap for evaluating a generative open-hardware control-PCB pipeline.

## 8. Conclusions

The landscape of datasets and benchmarks for ML/generative EDA has matured substantially for HDL code generation (VerilogEval, RTLLM, CVDP) and chip physical design (CircuitNet, ISPD/ICCAD suites, EDALearn, EDA-Schema-V2), with growing resources for analog circuit synthesis (AnalogGym, AICircuit). However, the PCB domain remains severely underserved: PCBSchemaGen's 23-task benchmark is a pioneering but small-scale first step. For evaluating a generative pipeline targeting open-hardware control PCBs, the community urgently needs (a) large-scale, standardized, manufacturable PCB corpora in open formats such as KiCad, (b) multi-stage evaluation frameworks that assess schematic correctness, routability, DRC/ERC compliance, BOM validity, and downstream manufacturability, and (c) contamination-aware benchmarking practices that ensure evaluation integrity. The methodological lessons from chip-level evaluation—particularly the importance of end-to-end metrics over surrogates and the value of open, stage-aware, multimodal benchmark schemas—provide a strong foundation for building the missing PCB evaluation infrastructure.
answer.raw_answer:
# Datasets, Benchmarks, and Evaluation Methodologies for Machine-Learning and Generative EDA (2025 Survey)

## 1. Introduction

The rapid application of large language models (LLMs) and other ML methods to electronic design automation (EDA) has created a pressing need for standardized datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation metrics spanning the full design stack—from HDL code generation through chip physical design to printed circuit board (PCB) schematic and layout synthesis. This survey catalogues the principal resources available as of 2025 and identifies critical gaps, with emphasis on their relevance to evaluating generative pipelines for open-hardware control PCBs.

The following table summarizes the major datasets and benchmarks identified in this survey:

| Dataset/Benchmark Name | Citation (author-year) | Domain | Scale | Key Metrics | Availability (URL/license) | Relevance to Open-Hardware PCB Pipeline |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

*Table: This table summarizes major open datasets, benchmark suites, and evaluation frameworks used in machine-learning and generative EDA through 2025. It highlights scale, metrics, availability, and how each resource does—or does not—support evaluation of an open-hardware control-PCB generation pipeline.*

## 2. HDL / RTL Generation Benchmarks

### 2.1 VerilogEval

**Citation:** Liu, M., Pinckney, N., Khailany, B., & Ren, H. (2023). VerilogEval: Evaluating Large Language Models for Verilog Code Generation. *ArXiv*, abs/2309.07544.

VerilogEval is a benchmark comprising 156 Verilog code-generation problems sourced from the HDLBits instructional website, ranging from simple combinational circuits to complex finite state machines (pqac-00000005). It provides two evaluation tracks—VerilogEval-machine (143 validated problems with auto-generated descriptions) and VerilogEval-human (156 problems with manually crafted descriptions) (pqac-00000002). Functional correctness is evaluated using the pass@k metric, where a problem is considered solved if at least one of *k* sampled LLM outputs passes simulation against a golden reference using the open-source Icarus Verilog simulator (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000004). The benchmark also includes 8,502 problem-code training pairs mined from GitHub Verilog repositories for supervised fine-tuning experiments (pqac-00000004). The updated VerilogEval v2 extends evaluation to specification-to-RTL tasks, adds in-context learning support, and provides automated failure classification (pqac-00000000). The benchmark is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval (pqac-00000001). **Relevance:** Directly applicable for evaluating generation of controller/FPGA logic modules that may accompany open control PCBs, though it does not assess board-level concerns such as component selection, connectivity, or manufacturability.

### 2.2 RTLLM and RTLLM 2.0

**Citation:** Lu, Y., Liu, S., Zhang, Q., & Xie, Z. (2024). RTLLM: An Open-Source Benchmark for Design RTL Generation with Large Language Model. *ASP-DAC 2024*, pp. 722–727. Liu, S., Lu, Y., Fang, W., Li, M., & Xie, Z. (2024). OpenLLM-RTL: Open Dataset and Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design RTL Generation. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–9.

RTLLM collects 30 common digital designs (11 arithmetic and 19 logic) with three progressive evaluation goals: a syntax goal (verifiable via synthesis into a netlist), a functionality goal (verifiable via testbench simulation), and a design quality goal (evaluating post-synthesis PPA against human-designed references) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000009). RTLLM 2.0 expands the suite to 50 hand-crafted designs covering a broader range of complexities and is accompanied by AssertEval (18 designs for assertion generation) and RTLCoder-Data (80K training samples, including a 7K verified high-quality subset) (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013). Each design provides a natural-language description, testbench, and correct reference RTL. Available at https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM (pqac-00000007). **Relevance:** More relevant than VerilogEval for evaluating synthesizable digital peripherals or glue logic that may reside on an open control board, but still misses board-level connectivity, ERC, and component/package constraints.

### 2.3 CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems)

**Citation:** Pinckney, N. et al. (2025). Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems: A Next-Generation Benchmark Dataset for Evaluating Large Language Models and Agents on RTL Design and Verification. *ArXiv*, abs/2506.14074.

CVDP is a next-generation benchmark comprising 783 problems across 13 task categories authored by experienced hardware engineers, covering RTL generation, verification, debugging, specification alignment, and technical Q&A. It supports both non-agentic and agentic task formats, with state-of-the-art models achieving no more than 34% pass@1 on code generation. **Relevance:** Especially valuable for evaluating agentic HDL workflows involving iterative debug and verification loops, analogous to how a PCB design agent would need to iterate on ERC/DRC feedback.

### 2.4 VeriContaminated

**Citation:** Wang, Z. et al. (2025). VeriContaminated: Assessing LLM-Driven Verilog Coding for Data Contamination. *IEEE ICLAD 2025*, pp. 117–123.

VeriContaminated is a meta-evaluation study that analyzes data contamination across VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks for state-of-the-art LLMs, finding near-100% contamination rates for some commercial models (pqac-00000040). It employs detection methods including CCD and Min-K% Prob and explores mitigations and their trade-offs for code quality versus fairness. **Relevance:** A critical cautionary resource for any open-hardware generative pipeline—apparent HDL generation success may be inflated by benchmark data leaking into LLM pre-training corpora, undermining reproducibility and trustworthiness of evaluations.

### 2.5 TuRTLe

**Citation:** Garcia-Gasulla, D. et al. (2025). TuRTLe: A Unified Evaluation of LLMs for RTL Generation. *ArXiv*, abs/2504.01986.

TuRTLe is a unified evaluation framework that integrates multiple existing benchmarks (including VerilogEval and RTLLM) and adds synthesis-aware scoring. It evaluates LLMs across cascaded stages—syntax (STX), functional correctness (FNC), and synthesizability (SYN)—using PASS@1, and adds a post-synthesis PPA-score derived from OpenLANE and the open-source SKY130A PDK (pqac-00000011). **Relevance:** By incorporating synthesis and PPA into the evaluation loop, TuRTLe provides a methodological precedent for PCB benchmarks that should similarly go beyond schematic correctness to assess ERC, DRC, BOM validity, and manufacturability.

## 3. Chip-Level Placement and Routing Benchmarks

### 3.1 CircuitNet and CircuitNet 2.0

**Citation:** Chai, Z. et al. (2022). CircuitNet: An Open-Source Dataset for Machine Learning Applications in Electronic Design Automation. *arXiv:2208.01040*. Jiang, X. et al. (2024). CircuitNet 2.0: An Advanced Dataset for Promoting Machine Learning Innovations in Realistic Chip Design Environment.

CircuitNet is the first large-scale open-source dataset for ML in VLSI physical design, built from RISC-V designs mapped to 28nm technology with 10,242 post-flow layouts from 12,960 backend design runs (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000019). It supports three prediction tasks—congestion, DRC violation, and IR drop—evaluated using NRMSE and SSIM for congestion, and ROC-AUC/PR-AUC for DRC and IR drop (pqac-00000019). CircuitNet 2.0 extends coverage to million-gate designs (CPU, GPU, AI chips) at 14nm FinFET and adds timing prediction tasks (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000018). Available at https://circuitnet.github.io/ (pqac-00000018). **Relevance:** The strongest open chip-physical-design dataset family; it informs evaluation methodology for congestion/DRC prediction that could be adapted for PCB routing quality assessment, though it does not directly address board-level constraints.

### 3.2 ISPD/ICCAD Contest Benchmark Suites

**Citation:** Various, 2005–2025. See Kahng, A.B. (2021). Advancing Placement. *ISPD 2021*, pp. 15–22; and individual contest proceedings.

The ISPD and ICCAD conferences have hosted 14+ placement and routing contests since 2005, with evolving themes including routability-driven placement (ISPD 2011, DAC 2012), detailed placement (ICCAD 2013), timing-driven placement (ICCAD 2014/2015), and large-scale global routing (ISPD 2025) (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025). Standard metrics include half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL), routing congestion/overflow, timing slack (WNS/TNS), legality, and runtime (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000025). Benchmarks are typically distributed in Bookshelf or LEF/DEF formats and are publicly available through conference archives (pqac-00000024). **Relevance:** Essential methodological precedent for chip placement/routing evaluation; the benchmarking culture and progressive metric design serve as a model for establishing equivalent PCB benchmark contests, though chip-level benchmarks do not capture board-level assembly, EMI, or thermal constraints.

### 3.3 EDALearn

**Citation:** Pan, J., Chang, C.-C., Xie, Z., Chen, Y., & Li, H.H. (2024). EDALearn: A Comprehensive RTL-to-Signoff EDA Benchmark for Democratized and Reproducible ML for EDA Research. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–8.

EDALearn is the first holistic, open-source benchmark suite spanning the complete RTL-to-signoff EDA flow, targeting FreePDK 45nm and ASAP 7nm technology nodes (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027). It aggregates practical VLSI instances from multiple public sources (IWLS, BOOM/Chipyard, OpenCores, ISCAS, ITC'99) and provides standardized evaluation for routability prediction, IR-drop prediction, and cross-stage analysis with both image-like and vector-based features. **Relevance:** A strong template for how an open PCB benchmark should connect source design intent through intermediate stages to final manufacturable artifacts, supporting reproducibility and cross-technology transfer.

### 3.4 EDA-Schema-V2

**Citation:** Shrestha, P. et al. (2025). EDA-Schema-V2: A Multimodal Schema, Open Datasets, and Benchmarks for Machine Learning in Digital Physical Design.

EDA-Schema-V2 provides approximately 7,800 physical design instances from 18 IWLS'05 benchmark circuits across four open PDKs (SkyWater 130nm, Nangate 45nm, IHP SG13G2 130nm, ASAP 7nm), encompassing over 275 million gates, 75 million nets, and 36 million extracted timing paths (pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053). It offers stage-resolved representations from synthesis through detailed routing in a multimodal entity-relationship schema covering circuit graphs, spatial layout images, and QoR metrics. The dataset and schema are released on GitHub. **Relevance:** Particularly relevant methodologically as a multimodal, stage-aware, reproducible, and open benchmark—a strong model for how an open PCB benchmark could combine schematic, layout, netlist, images, and fabrication outputs in a unified schema.

## 4. Analog Circuit Datasets and Benchmarks

### 4.1 AnalogGym

**Citation:** Li, J. et al. (2024). AnalogGym: An Open and Practical Testing Suite for Analog Circuit Synthesis. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–9.

AnalogGym provides 30 circuit topologies across five categories: amplifiers (16), low-dropout regulators (6), sensing front ends (6), voltage references (3), and phase-locked loops (1) (pqac-00000028). It supports comprehensive evaluation including DC, AC, pole-zero, and transient analyses across process-voltage-temperature (PVT) corners (SS/FF/SF/FS/TT, 1.08–1.32V, −40 to 125°C) (pqac-00000030). Compatible with Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, and the open-source Ngspice simulator with the sky130 PDK for amplifier and LDO categories (pqac-00000029). Available at https://github.com/CODA-Team/AnalogGym. **Relevance:** Directly relevant for analog portions of control PCBs (power rails, sensor front ends, voltage references), though it evaluates IC-level analog synthesis rather than discrete-component board schematics.

### 4.2 AICircuit

**Citation:** Mehradfar, A. et al. (2024). AICircuit: A Multi-Level Dataset and Benchmark for AI-Driven Analog Integrated Circuit Design. *ArXiv*, abs/2407.18272.

AICircuit comprises seven basic analog/RF circuits (common-source voltage amplifier, two-stage amplifier, cascode amplifier, LNA, power amplifier, VCO, and mixer) plus two complex heterogeneous wireless transceiver systems (pqac-00000045, pqac-00000048). Data is generated via Cadence schematic-level parameter sweeps with commercial simulation, and performance metrics include DC power, bandwidth, gain, noise figure, phase noise, and output power (pqac-00000046). The dataset and code are open-sourced on GitHub (pqac-00000048). **Relevance:** Useful for learning analog performance mappings and inverse design concepts that could inform board-level analog block selection, but it is IC-centric rather than a PCB schematic/layout benchmark.

## 5. PCB-Level Datasets and Benchmarks

### 5.1 PCBSchemaGen

**Citation:** Zou, H., Han, P., Nazerian, E., & Huang, A.Q. (2025). PCBSchemaGen: Constraint-Guided Schematic Design via LLM for Printed Circuit Boards (PCB). *ArXiv*, abs/2602.00510.

PCBSchemaGen is the most directly relevant benchmark for evaluating generative PCB design pipelines. It provides 23 unique PCB schematic-generation tasks spanning analog, digital, and power domains, with a curated KiCad-compatible component library containing 18 symbols, 21 footprints, and 14 reference datasheets (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032). Evaluation uses Pass@k (k=1,5 with n=15 trials and up to 3 self-correction retries), topology verification via subgraph isomorphism (VF2), ERC checks, and semantic constraint checking grounded in a datasheet-derived knowledge graph (pqac-00000035, pqac-00000033). The framework outputs executable SKiDL Python code that produces KiCad-ready PCB layout files. The benchmark and knowledge graph are open-sourced on GitHub. **Relevance:** The most directly applicable resource for evaluating open-hardware control-PCB generation—it is board-level, datasheet-grounded, KiCad/SKiDL-compatible, and checks topology rather than text similarity alone.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics by Domain

The following table summarizes the key metrics employed across different EDA evaluation domains:

| Domain | Key Metrics | Description | Relevance to PCB Generation |
|---|---|---|---|
| HDL/RTL Generation | pass@k; functional pass rate; synthesizability; PPA | Measures whether any of *k* samples pass simulation/testbench checks, whether generated RTL compiles and synthesizes, and optionally whether post-synthesis area/power/delay are acceptable rather than merely syntactically valid (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000011). | Relevant for open-hardware control boards that include FPGA/CPLD logic, soft peripherals, or digital glue logic; less directly useful for PCB-only pipelines because it does not assess connectors, footprints, ERC, or manufacturability. |
| Placement | HPWL; congestion; density; timing slack (WNS/TNS) | HPWL approximates interconnect cost before detailed routing; congestion compares routing demand to routing capacity; density/legalization captures overlap and spread; WNS/TNS quantify timing closure risk after placement (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000025). | Provides a strong methodological template for evaluating board component placement quality, especially compactness and routability, but chip placement metrics do not map cleanly to board-level assembly, creepage, or connector accessibility. |
| Routing | routed wirelength; DRC violations; congestion overflow; runtime | Evaluates the quality of routed solutions by total route length, whether design-rule violations remain, how much routing demand exceeds available resources, and often solve/runtime cost in contest settings (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000024). | Highly relevant to PCB generation because board autorouters also need short routes, low overflow, and DRC-clean outputs; however PCB routing additionally needs layer assignment, EMI, thermal, return-current, and manufacturability considerations not captured in chip-routing benchmarks. |
| Analog Circuit Synthesis | gain; bandwidth; power; noise figure; area; PVT compliance | Assesses whether synthesized analog circuits meet electrical specifications across process, voltage, and temperature corners using DC/AC/PZ/transient analyses and multi-objective optimization targets (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000046). | Relevant for control-PCB subcircuits such as sensor front ends, regulators, references, and filters; still mostly IC-centric, so discrete-component tolerances, package parasitics, and board parasitics remain underrepresented. |
| PCB Schematic Generation | Pass@k; topology verification; ERC; DRC; semantic constraint checks | Measures whether generated schematics are executable and correct at the graph/topology level, pass electrical-rule checks, satisfy pin-role and datasheet constraints, and can be iteratively repaired through verifier feedback (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000035). | This is the most directly relevant metric family for open-hardware control-PCB generation because it checks whether a produced schematic is actually electrically and structurally valid, not just textually plausible. |
| End-to-End Chip Design | final PPA; timing closure; DRC-clean GDSII; signoff success | Evaluates the final post-route outcome instead of surrogate intermediate metrics, asking whether the generated design closes timing, satisfies physical verification, and delivers acceptable final QoR/signoff artifacts (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000043). | Extremely important as a lesson for PCB evaluation: schematic-level or placement-level success is insufficient unless the full pipeline yields a manufacturable, rule-clean, and functionally testable board release package. |


*Table: This table summarizes the main evaluation metrics used across HDL generation, physical design, analog synthesis, PCB schematic generation, and end-to-end chip design. It is useful for mapping chip-level evaluation practice onto the still immature evaluation landscape for generative open-hardware PCB pipelines.*

## 7. Gaps and Challenges in Evaluating Generative Circuit/PCB Design

### 7.1 Lack of Standardized, Manufacturable, Open PCB Datasets

The most acute gap identified across the literature is the near-complete absence of large-scale, standardized, open PCB schematic and layout datasets suitable for training and evaluating generative models. As noted by multiple sources, "automated PCB schematic design remains unexplored due to the scarcity of open-source data and the absence of simulation-based verification" (pqac-00000039). PCB schematic capture formats are vendor-specific, making large-scale corpus construction difficult (pqac-00000034). Even at the chip level, "EDA and design data are limited and often not in the open domain," and generating ML-ready EDA data requires expensive and time-consuming flows that can take "weeks to months" per run, compounded by NDA restrictions that "prohibit the release of the original data" (pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038). PCBSchemaGen's 23-task benchmark represents a pioneering but very small-scale effort; no "ImageNet-scale" open PCB dataset yet exists (pqac-00000043).

### 7.2 Data Contamination and Benchmark Validity

Data contamination—where benchmark data inadvertently leaks into LLM pre-training corpora—is a critical concern for HDL generation benchmarks. VeriContaminated demonstrated near-100% contamination rates for some commercial LLMs on both VerilogEval and RTLLM, fundamentally undermining the validity of reported pass rates and limiting industrial adoption (pqac-00000040). The undisclosed nature of pre-training datasets, even for nominally open-source models, makes contamination detection essential but difficult. This concern extends directly to any PCB-level benchmarks that might be developed from publicly available KiCad repositories.

### 7.3 Surrogate vs. End-to-End Metrics

A recurring finding across chip-level work is that intermediate surrogate metrics frequently misalign with final design quality. ChiPBench demonstrated that even if a placement algorithm achieves dominant intermediate metrics, "the final PPA results are unsatisfactory" (from paper search results). At the HDL level, prior work critiques BLEU-like metrics noting they "do not correlate well with functional correctness" (pqac-00000002). For PCB generation, this implies that evaluating only schematic correctness is insufficient—a complete evaluation must also verify that the generated design can be routed, passes DRC, has a valid BOM, and is actually manufacturable.

### 7.4 Reproducibility Concerns

Multiple sources highlight reproducibility as a systemic challenge. Proprietary PDKs, closed-source EDA tools, and restricted benchmark data limit who can reproduce experimental results (pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042). The NSF Workshop on AI for EDA explicitly calls for "open-source initiatives for developing shared datasets, tools, and benchmarks" to "enable reproducibility and innovation" (pqac-00000037). Open toolchains such as OpenROAD, Ngspice, KiCad, and open PDKs (sky130, Nangate45, ASAP7) are enabling a more reproducible ecosystem, but coverage gaps remain—especially for advanced nodes and board-level design rules.

### 7.5 Missing Dimensions for PCB Evaluation

Board-level design introduces evaluation dimensions absent from chip-level benchmarks: component sourcing and BOM validation, mechanical constraints (connector placement, enclosure fit), thermal and EMI compliance, creepage/clearance requirements, return-current path analysis, and integration with firmware/software. Existing analog simulators like SPICE are insufficient for PCB-level correctness validation because they model idealized components rather than real IC packages with pin-level constraints (pqac-00000039). No current benchmark comprehensively addresses these board-specific dimensions, representing the largest gap for evaluating a generative open-hardware control-PCB pipeline.

## 8. Conclusions

The landscape of datasets and benchmarks for ML/generative EDA has matured substantially for HDL code generation (VerilogEval, RTLLM, CVDP) and chip physical design (CircuitNet, ISPD/ICCAD suites, EDALearn, EDA-Schema-V2), with growing resources for analog circuit synthesis (AnalogGym, AICircuit). However, the PCB domain remains severely underserved: PCBSchemaGen's 23-task benchmark is a pioneering but small-scale first step. For evaluating a generative pipeline targeting open-hardware control PCBs, the community urgently needs (a) large-scale, standardized, manufacturable PCB corpora in open formats such as KiCad, (b) multi-stage evaluation frameworks that assess schematic correctness, routability, DRC/ERC compliance, BOM validity, and downstream manufacturability, and (c) contamination-aware benchmarking practices that ensure evaluation integrity. The methodological lessons from chip-level evaluation—particularly the importance of end-to-end metrics over surrogates and the value of open, stage-aware, multimodal benchmark schemas—provide a strong foundation for building the missing PCB evaluation infrastructure.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes VerilogEval as an open-source benchmark and online judge for Verilog code generation that provides a curated evaluation dataset of 156 problems drawn from HDLBits. The framework emphasizes automated, reproducible testing: Verilog code completions are evaluated via automatic functional-correctness testing (simulation against canonical/golden solutions) and the design follows best practices from the HumanEval Python coding benchmark. The project also created a synthetic supervised fine-tuning dataset by using LLMs to generate paired problem descriptions and Verilog code to enable SFT experiments. The excerpt does not specify particular numeric metrics such as pass@k, nor does it state a software license or a repository URL/explicit availability details beyond calling the benchmark "open-source."

contexts.context: The original VerilogEval benchmark (VerilogEval v1) contains 156 problems sourced from HDLBits and includes two problem-description types: VerilogEval-machine and VerilogEval-human. Performance is reported as a pass rate using the pass@k metric: the LLM is sampled multiple times and a problem is considered passed if at least one of the k samples passes the functional check. The paper explains the pass@k formulation and notes the original results used k = 1, 5, 10. Functional correctness is determined by counting trials that "pass the functional check" (c) out of trials (n). VerilogEval v2 extends the benchmark to support specification-to-RTL tasks (v1 only supported code completion), in-context learning examples, fine-grained failure classification, and a Makefile-based evaluation environment for easier scaling and inspection. The improved benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval. The excerpt does not state the project's license, so licensing details are not provided in the provided text.
contexts.question: What are the details of VerilogEval benchmark including number of problems, evaluation metrics (pass@k, functional correctness), license, and availability?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the VerilogEval benchmark design and parts of its dataset creation and evaluation protocol. VerilogEval follows HumanEval best practices and provides a sandbox environment for simple, reproducible evaluation of LLM-generated Verilog. The benchmark draws problems from the HDLBits instructional site; the authors manually converted 156 problem descriptions in total. In an automated/machine-generation stage they considered 156 candidate problems and found 108 produced successful solutions on initial sampling. Functional correctness is the evaluated metric: canonical solutions are provided as the golden reference for testing. The authors use sampling-based validation (e.g., descriptions marked invalid if none of the generated completions succeed across 100 samples) and describe sampling iterations with 8 completions per description in some phases; sampling for a problem stops once a passing completion is found. The repository URL is provided (https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval). The excerpt does not state explicit pass@k results/details, the benchmark license, or the exact final problem count used for evaluation.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents key details of the VerilogEval benchmark: Table I lists VerilogEval-machine as a Verilog benchmark with 8,502 training problems and 143 test problems, and VerilogEval-human with 156 test problems (no train count). The authors state they "generated 143 valid problem descriptions." Evaluation focuses on functional correctness rather than match-based scores: they "follow recent work in directly measuring code functional correctness through pass@k metric," where "a problem is considered solved if any of the k samples passes the unit tests," and they reference using an unbiased estimator from prior work. The paper critiques BLEU-like metrics, noting "such metrics does not correlate well with functional correctness." For automated testing they adapted a sandbox (to safely run untrusted programs), installed the open-source ICARUS Verilog simulator in a Docker container, and handle simulation/testing under the hood so results can be produced with a single command; they also note the simulator may not include all IEEE-1364 features. The excerpt does not state the benchmark's license or where/download availability is provided.

contexts.context: RTLLM is an open benchmark for RTL generation that "collects 30 common designs with various design scales and complexities." It defines three progressive evaluation goals: the syntax goal (generated RTL must be syntactically correct and "can be verified by checking whether the design can be correctly synthesized into netlist by synthesis tools"), the functionality goal (the RTL should match designers' expectations and "can be verified by checking whether the generated design passes all test cases in a comprehensive testbench"; the benchmark samples a reasonable number of test cases and notes that passing them does not guarantee exhaustive correctness), and the quality goal (practical design qualities: "including performance, power, area (PPA)" evaluated by measuring PPA "after the synthesis and layout of generated V"). For each of the 30 designs the benchmark supplies three files for automated evaluation: a natural-language Description, a Testbench (testbench.v) with multiple test cases, and a Correct Design (designer RTL.v) reference. The excerpt states availability: "It will be open-sourced in https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM." The provided excerpt does not state the licensing terms.
contexts.question: What are the details of RTLLM benchmark including number of designs, evaluation metrics (syntax goal, functionality goal, design quality goal), license, and availability?

We name the first and the most fundamental goal as the syntax goal.
It means the syntax of generated RTL design V should at least be correct.

After ensuring syntax correctness, we name the second goal as the functionality goal.
It means the functionality of generated RTL design V should be exactly the same as the designers’ expectation.
It can be verified by checking whether the generated design passes all test cases in a comprehensive testbench.
We name this goal as quality goal.
including performance, power, area (PPA) should also be desirable.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes TURTLE's evaluation pipeline and metrics relevant to RTL generation benchmarks (including mentions of RTLLM plots). Syntax, functionality and synthesis checks are binary stages named STX, FNC, and SYN, respectively, and are aggregated using PASS@k; TURTLE sets k = 1 and reports PASS@1 for LCA, STX, FNC, and SYN. Designs that pass SYN are assessed for design quality using a post-synthesis PPA-score derived from OpenLANE reports; PPA components are power, performance, and area. The designs in TURTLE are synthesized with the open-source SKY130A PDK and constrained to a 10ns delay. The PPA-score maps per-generation PPA metrics ˆpi,j to discrete interpretations (ˆpi,j=0: fails or ≥2× worse than reference; ˆpi,j=1: equal to human reference; ˆpi,j=2: idealized zero area/time/energy) and averages these across generations and across PPA dimensions (area, power, performance). The framework uses a cascade scoring (STX ≥ FNC ≥ SYN) and aggregates results across benchmarks via a weighted average. The excerpt does not provide RTLLM-specific counts (number of designs), license, or availability details; those items must be obtained from other sections or the paper's repository/metadata.
contexts.question: What are the details of RTLLM benchmark including number of designs, evaluation metrics (syntax goal, functionality goal, design quality goal), license, and availability?

RTLLM-2.0 refines the original RTLLM taxonomy by dividing designs into four primary classes: Arithmetic Modules, Memory Modules, Control Modules, and Miscellaneous Modules. Control Modules include FSMs, sequence detectors, and counters (e.g., 12-bit counter, Johnson counter, ring counter, up/down counter). snippets: The benchmark includes 50 designs across various applications, with bold designs representing newly added designs relative to RTLLM.
The benchmark RTLLM-2.0 dataset is meticulously categorized into four primary module classes: Arithmetic Modules, Memory Modules, Control Modules, and Miscellaneous Modules.
Arithmetic Modules comprise various adders, subtractors, multipliers, dividers, comparators, accumulators, and other specialized units like fixed-point arithmetic components.
Memory Modules are designed to handle data storage and retrieval with FIFO (First-In, First-Out) and LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) buffers

Control Modules focus on state management and counting mechanisms, featuring finite state machines (FSMs), sequence detectors, and various counters such as a 12-bit counter, Johnson counter (JC_counter), ring counter, and an up/down counter.
Unlike RTLLM, which broadly classifies designs into Arithmetic and Logic types, RTLLM-2.0 takes a closer look by categorizing designs based on their specific functions and applications.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes RTLLM as an open-source benchmark for natural-language-to-RTL generation. RTLLM contains 30 designs spanning a range of complexities and scales. The benchmark defines three progressive evaluation goals: a syntax goal, a functionality goal, and a design quality goal; automation scripts are provided so the benchmark can automatically evaluate an LLM solution against all three goals. RTLLM supports evaluation of any generated HDL format (Verilog, VHDL, Chisel) provided the output can be run through logic synthesis and RTL simulation. It also provides ground-truth human-crafted RTLs as a standard baseline for assessing design quality. Design complexity and scale are quantified in the paper by number of HDL lines and number of cells in the post-synthesis netlist (as shown in Table I). contexts.context: The excerpt describes the RTLLM/AssertEval benchmark and dataset details. AssertEval (part of RTLLM-2.0) includes 18 open-source designs spanning cryptographic units, processor cores, arithmetic units, communication protocols, and memory controllers. For each design RTLLM-2.0 provides three files: design_description.txt (natural-language specification and I/O), testbench.v (multiple test cases with inputs and expected outputs), and designer_RTL.v (human reference Verilog). Evaluation uses three progressive goals: (1) Syntax goal — the generated RTL should be syntactically correct and synthesizable (verifiable by synthesis into a netlist); (2) Functionality goal — the RTL should pass the provided testbench test cases; (3) Design-quality goal — practical qualities (PPA: performance, power, area) verified by measuring PPA after synthesis and layout. For assertion-generation evaluation the metrics include Syntax checks, FPV pass/fail (formal property verification), and COI (cone of influence) coverage. The authors limited specification documents to "fewer than 60 pages and contain fewer than 60" (signal/architectural items). Prior dataset work (RTLCoder) produced 27K samples and a verified high-quality subset of 7K “mostly-correct” samples. The excerpt does not provide explicit information about the benchmark license or where/how to download the benchmark (availability).

snippets: The benchmark AssertEval consists of 18 open-source designs that cover a diverse array of applications, including cryptographic units, processor cores, arithmetic units, communication protocols, and memory controllers.
The first and basic goal is the syntax goal.
It can be verified by checking whether the design can be correctly synthesized into netlist by synthesis tools [43].
The second is functionality goal. It requires the generated RTL design to function as expected, verified by passing all test cases provided in testbench.v.
also be desirable. This is the quality goal, verified by measuring PPA values after synthesis and layout.
For each design, RTLLM-2.0 provides the following information in three separate files.
Description (design_description.txt): A natural language description of the target design’s functionality, serving as a prompt for LLMs to generate RTL code.
Testbench (testbench.v): A testbench containing multiple test cases with input and expected output values.
Correct Design (designer_RTL.v): A reference design Verilog hand-crafted by human designers.

these metrics are computed to evaluate the quality of generated assertions:  • Syntax: checks if generated assertions have syntax errors.  • FPV pass/fail: when RTL designs are bug-free, an assertion that passes the FPV check is considered semantically correct,  • COI coverage: cone of influence (COI) coverage measures the percentage of design logic that is structurally connected to the properties.
Considering the capability of existing LLM-based generation methods, we have collected specification documents that are fewer than 60 pages and contain fewer than 60

Our prior work RTLCoder [28] has proposed an automated training dataset generation flow and generated 27K training samples, with each sample being a pair of design instruction (i.e., model input) and the reference RTL code (i.e., expected model output).
we further generate and release a high-quality verified dataset with 7K ‘mostly-correct’ samples.

The dataset provides multi-modal data collected from the design flow, explicitly including routability, timing, power, and IR-drop labels to support diverse ML prediction tasks. CircuitNet 2.0 expands on the original CircuitNet by increasing design scale/type coverage, using advanced 14nm process data, and adding timing-prediction tasks. The paper provides an availability pointer (https://circuitnet.github.io/). snippets: we collect more than 10,000 samples with a variety of chip designs (e.g., CPU, GPU, and AI Chip).
All the designs are conducted through complete commercial design flows in a widely-used technology node, 14nm FinFET.
We collect comprehensive data, including routability, timing, and power, from the design flow to support versatile ML tasks in EDA.
The dataset contains data to support multi-model prediction tasks in advanced technology nodes, like timing, routability, and IR-drop, covering more design objectives compared with CircuitNet.

CircuitNet 2.0 goes beyond the original CircuitNet in three perspectives: 1) Providing data for million-gate designs like CPU, GPU, and AI chips to increase circuit types and scales; 2) Generating data with advanced 14nm FinFET technology node to increase the manufacturing and modeling complexity; 3) Adding an additional timing prediction task for circuit performance to increase task diversity and practicality.

Previous efforts like CircuitNet (Chai et al., 2022; 2023) try to alleviate such situation by releasing an open-source dataset containing over 10,000 samples and supports ML tasks in EDA, e.g., routability and IR-drop prediction (Ren & Hu, 2022).
https://circuitnet.github.io/

contexts.context: CircuitNet 2.0 is a cross-stage, multi-modal dataset generated with commercial EDA tools. It uses an advanced 14nm FinFET PDK (the original CircuitNet used 28nm planar CMOS) and includes a wider variety of design types (CPU, GPU, AI chip). The dataset targets three core objectives: routability, IR-drop, and timing. Typical evaluation metrics and labels discussed include routing congestion ("the overflow of routing demand over routing resources"), distributions of design rule violations (DRV hotspots), and IR-drop ("the deviation of supply power due to large local current demand"). CircuitNet 2.0 provides both geometric (cell locations, suitable for image-based models/image-to-image tasks) and topological data (cell/net connectivity, suitable for graph-based modeling), enabling image- and graph-based ML approaches. Table 1 lists multiple designs spanning scales (examples: RISCY, RISCY-FPU, zero-riscy, OpenC910-1, Vortex-small/large, NVDLA-small/large) with per-design cell/net/macro/pin/IO counts and sample counts. Sample generation time varies widely (from ~2 hours for zero-riscy to ~1 week for NVDLA-large). The excerpt does not state dataset license or public availability details.
contexts.question: What are the details of CircuitNet dataset for chip design ML? Include number of designs, technology nodes, features (congestion, DRC, IR drop), evaluation metrics, license, and availability.

snippets: CircuitNet 2.0 is generated by commercial design tools with advanced technology node, 14nm FinFET, and various designs, whose types and scales are widely different.

CircuitNet 2.0 includes richer design types ( e.g., CPU, GPU, and AI Chip) and advanced PDK (e.g., 14nm FinFET) and supports additional timing prediction tasks, while only CPU designs and 28nm planar CMOS PDK are employed in the original CircuitNet.
This dataset focuses on optimizing three most important design objectives, i.e., routability, IR-drop, and timing.
Congestion is defined as the overflow of routing demand over routing resources.
DRV hotspots are positions with undesirable designs that will become defects in manufacturing.
IR-drop is the deviation of supply power due to large local current demand.
our dataset provides the cross-stage multi-modal data generated by commercial EDA flow and tools with a convenient data format for a variety of ML tasks.

As a chip comprises both geometric information like the locations of cells, and topological information like the connectivity of cells (i.e., nets), we can leverage such multi-modal information to build ML models.
Image-based Modeling: In physical design, many tasks can be formulated as image-to-image translation tasks, as the geometric information on the chip can be represented as an image.

Graph-based Modeling: Modeling with graphs has been introduced into tasks that largely rely on topological information and require higher granularity, such as congestion prediction ... and timing prediction
† The smallest design zero-riscy takes about 2 hours to generate one data sample, while the largest design NVDLA-large takes nearly 1 week to generate one data sample.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CircuitNet 2.0 as a cross-stage, multi-modal dataset produced by commercial EDA tools at an advanced technology node (14nm FinFET). It includes a wider variety of design types (CPU, GPU, AI chips) compared with the original CircuitNet (which used 28nm planar CMOS). The dataset targets three primary ML prediction objectives: routability, IR-drop, and timing; routability relates to routing congestion and distributions of design rule violations (DRV/DRC), IR-drop is described as deviation of supply power due to local current demand, and timing covers delays of cells and nets. snippets: Each design has 2160 settings, and all the designs have 12,960 runs of the back-end design.
Eventually, we obtained 10,242 layouts after excluding the failed runs.

We included both graph-like features (i.e., gate-level netlists) and image-like features  (i.e., two-dimensional feature maps extracted from the physical layouts, as the design information can be naturally represented by image-like data by dividing a layout into tiles and regarding each tile as a pixel).
To evaluate the effectiveness of CircuitNet, we further conducted experiments on three prediction tasks: congestion, design rule check (DRC) violation, and IR drop.
First,  for congestion prediction, we used the normalized root-mean-square-error and structural similarity index measure as metrics to evaluate
for DRC violation, we considered the area under the curve (AUC) of the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and that of the precision-recall (PR) curve
for IR drop prediction, we evaluated the AUC of the ROC curve and that of the PR curve.

We separated the features shown in Figure 1(b) and stored them in different directories to enable custom applications. We provided scripts for preprocessing and combining different features for training and testing used in the above experiments as references.
Access methods. The user guide and the download link  for CircuitNet can be accessed from https://circuitnet.github.io.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CircuitNet 2.0: a large, commercially generated dataset for ML in physical chip design. Key facts present: CircuitNet 2.0 contains 10,791 designs produced using commercial EDA tools (Design Compiler and Innovus) at an advanced 14nm FinFET process node (original CircuitNet used 28nm). Design types include CPU, GPU, and AI chips and sample scales vary widely (e.g., NVDLA-large has 1,478,865 cells; zero-riscy 35,969). The dataset extracts multi-modal features and labels from different flow stages (synthesis, floorplan, placement, routing). Examples of available features/labels listed include RUDY, congestion, DRV, eCongestion, instance placement, cell density, overall/temporal power, IR-drop, netlist/topology, pin positions, and net delay; routing data are produced after global routing (GR) and detailed routing (DR). contexts.context: The excerpt describes CircuitNet as the "first open-source dataset called CircuitNet" for ML in VLSI CAD. The dataset uses RISC-V designs: "RISC-V designs were mapped from register transfer level (RTL) designs to gate-level netlists in the 28 nm technology node with Synopsys Design Compiler." Physical design used Cadence Innovus to produce layouts. The corpus includes multiple design variants: "Each design has 2160 settings, and all the designs have 12,960 runs of the back-end design flow." Available prediction targets and features mentioned include congestion, DRC violations, and IR drop, along with other layout and power/timing related features (e.g., cell density, macro regions, pin configuration, instance power, utilizations, #Macro, #Power Mesh, filler insertion, clock tree, signal arrival). Feature extraction spans synthesis through placement and routing stages. Evaluation metrics cited include pixel-level accuracy for some spatial tasks (an FCN baseline produced "0.040 and 0.80, respectively"), and for DRC violation prediction they used "the area under the curve (AUC) of the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and that of the precision-recall (PR) curve." The paper/ dataset entry is available via the provided DOI/URL. The excerpt does not specify a license string beyond calling the dataset "open-source."

contexts.context: The excerpt critiques existing placement/routing benchmark suites and contest testcases as episodic, obfuscated, incomplete, and often outdated (e.g., MCNC-era suites). It calls for updated, long-lived core formulations, solution criteria, and benchmark generators to better represent modern designs (Challenge 7). Recent open-hardware initiatives (RISC-V, POSH, FOSSi, CHIPS Alliance, OpenHW) are noted as promising sources for more complete and modern benchmarks. The text emphasizes that placement optimizers co-optimize many metrics — explicitly listing wirelength, timing, density, congestion, non-overlap, and pin accessibility — and that importance of these objectives can shift across iterations/stages. It highlights routability as especially hard to capture because a placement’s routability is only known after routing and depends on a specific router’s heuristics and fixed-die context. The excerpt also calls for improved congestion formulations for analytic placers (Challenge 11) and implies that contest suites and benchmarking culture should be refreshed to drive research and properly measure tradeoffs (quality vs runtime).
contexts.question: What are the details of ISPD and ICCAD placement/routing benchmarks and contest suites? What metrics do they use (wirelength, congestion, timing)?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the 2023 MLCAD FPGA macro-placement contest benchmark format and evaluation methodology. Benchmarks were provided in an extended Bookshelf format (similar to ISPD contest formats), with files such as design.nodes and design.nets; the public suite has 140 designs and a hidden suite of 198 designs. Contestants used a Vivado place-and-route (P&R) flow that ingested placements in the extended Bookshelf format, validated legality, and performed standard-cell placement and routing. The P&R flow was explicitly non-timing-driven: the contest emphasized routability and runtime rather than timing optimization. Solutions were evaluated on column/SITE legality, total routing congestion, macro placement runtime, and total Vivado P&R runtime (5-hour limit). The scoring function combined runtime and routability: scorej = (timplj + timeP&Rj) * routabilityj, with timemplj = 1 + max(0, timemplj − 10) (no penalty under 10 minutes) and routability as the sum of initial and final routing scores. Routing congestion metrics were derived from Vivado’s router and, for AMD FPGAs, assessed on the interconnect tile grid, categorized by wire-length (short/long) and direction. The excerpt references ISPD-style Bookshelf formats but does not detail ICCAD suites or explicit wirelength/timing metrics beyond noting that timing was not used for this contest.
contexts.question: What are the details of ISPD and ICCAD placement/routing benchmarks and contest suites? What metrics do they use (wirelength, congestion, timing)?

contexts.context: The excerpt documents the long history of ISPD/ICCAD placement contests and their shifting emphases. From ISPD05/06 “Placement” through a series of specialized contests, 14 placement contests have been run at major conferences. Several contest themes are explicit in the table: routability-driven placement (ISPD11, DAC12, ICCAD12), detailed placement (ICCAD13), detailed routing-driven placement (ISPD14), timing-driven placement (ICCAD14/ICCAD15), and blockage-aware placement (ISPD15). The contest suites have also been used as part of broader flow contexts that include stages such as synthesis, placement, CTS, post-place sizing/optimization, and routing; contest frameworks expose tool interfaces like Timer API and Parasitics API. The excerpt therefore indicates that contest metrics and objectives have focused on routability, timing, detailed-routing objectives and placement robustness (e.g., blockage-awareness), and that contests often evaluate flow-level outcomes across multiple stages. The excerpt does not list explicit numeric metrics (e.g., wirelength or congestion scores) or their exact scoring formulas.
contexts.question: What are the details of ISPD and ICCAD placement/routing benchmarks and contest suites? What metrics do they use (wirelength, congestion, timing)?

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses historical ISPD and ICCAD benchmark datasets and their limitations for end-to-end placement/routing evaluation. Early contest datasets (e.g., ISPD2005 and ICCAD2004) used simplified Bookshelf formats that are abstracted from real design kits, meaning they cannot be used to evaluate final PPA (power, performance, area). Later benchmarks (ISPD2015 and ICCAD2015) improved realism by providing netlists, library files, and design exchange files, but the paper notes these still lack essential design kits (e.g., timing constraints, LEF, complete implementation kits) required to run open-source physical flows like OpenROAD. The authors’ Table 1 contrasts existing datasets with their ChiPBench, indicating that earlier ISPD entries do not provide a complete design suite or logic synthesis/full EDA flow support, while newer ICCAD/ISPD 2015 datasets are closer to real-world but still insufficient for full end-to-end PPA evaluation. The excerpt does not enumerate the specific contest metrics (wirelength, congestion, timing) used by ISPD/ICCAD contests, only highlighting that their data formats limit the ability to measure final PPA.
contexts.question: What are the details of ISPD and ICCAD placement/routing benchmarks and contest suites? What metrics do they use (wirelength, congestion, timing)?

contexts.context: The excerpt introduces EDALearn as "the first holistic, open-source benchmark suite specifically for ML tasks in EDA," providing an end-to-end flow from synthesis to signoff. EDALearn targets multiple back-end EDA tasks including placement, routing, power analysis, timing analysis, and IR drop prediction, and it "includes a diverse set of real-world VLSI design instances, along with a standardized evaluation framework that facilitates the fair assessment of different ML models and techniques." The paper emphasizes reproducibility and transferability across technology nodes and provides in-depth data analysis to help model development. The excerpt also contrasts prior efforts: one prior dataset "only adopted 6 RISC-V designs in total," noting that is limited, while TITAN focuses on FPGA placement/routing and produced a Titan23 set of 23 circuits. A referenced work discusses parameterized synthetic combinational-circuit generation to produce many varied instances. TITAN [3] is a benchmark that focuses on FPGA placement and routing. It uses Altera’s Quartus II FPGA CAD software to perform HDL synthesis and creates the Titan23 benchmark set, which consists of 23 benchmark circuits.
[4] discussed parameterized generation of synthetic combinational circuits, providing a convenient way to generate large numbers of circuits with a wide range of characteristics.
