snippets: AICircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design.
We present AICircuit, a com- prehensive multi-level dataset and benchmark for developing and evaluating ML algorithms in analog and radio-frequency circuit design.

AICircuit comprises seven commonly used basic circuits and two complex wireless transceiver systems composed of multiple circuit blocks, encompassing a wide array of design scenar- ios encountered in real-world applications.
We extensively evaluate various ML algorithms on the dataset, revealing the potential of ML algorithms in learning the mapping from the design specifications to the desired circuit parameters.

The data and codebase are open-sourced to advance the development of machine learning in the analog and radio-frequency circuit design domain in the following link: https://github.com/AvestimehrResearchGroup/AICircuit.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGym: an open testing suite for ML-driven analog circuit synthesis. AnalogGym claims to include 30 circuit topologies across five categories (sensing front ends, voltage references, AMPs, LDOs, PLLs). Table 1 lists per-category counts and simulators: Amplifiers (16) use Spectre/Ngspice with open PDK; LDOs (6) use Spectre/Ngspice with open PDK; Sensing front ends (6) use Spectre (no open PDK); Voltage references (3) use Hspice (no open PDK); PLLs (1) use Hspice (no open PDK). LDOs and AMPs specifically support open-source Ngspice and PDKs for accessibility and reproducibility. The suite requires comprehensive simulations (DC, AC, pole-zero, transient) and provides an encapsulated netlist and a textual testbench (TB) format where lines select netlist, PVT corners (P, V, T), load capacitance, and optimization parameters (MOSFET size, caps, resistors, input bias). The sky130 PDK uses micrometer units for MOS dimensions. MOSFET naming follows a structured underscore-separated convention (example: MOSFET_23_1_L_gm3_NMOS). No information about EDALearn, AICircuit, licenses, exact availability links, or other datasets is present in the excerpt.

snippets: Therefore, we propose AnalogGym, an open and practical testing suite that provides a comprehensive and standardized framework for evaluating ML algorithms in analog circuit synthesis.
AnalogGym encompasses 30 circuit topologies in five key categories: sensing front ends, voltage references, AMPs, low dropout regulators (LDOs), and phase-locked loops (PLLs).
Table 1. Details of AnalogGym Components.
Amplifier                 16            Spectre/Ngspice      ✓
Low dropout regulator           6            Spectre/Ngspice      ✓
Sensing front end              6                Spectre          ×
Voltage reference              3                Hspice          ×
Phase-locked loop             1                Hspice          ×
Among these, the LDOs and AMPs support the open-source Ngspice simulator and PDKs, allowing for greater accessibility and reproducibility.
To fully evaluate an amplifier’s performance, simulations must include direct current (DC), alternating current (AC), pole-zero (PZ), and transient analyses.
To streamline complexity and establish an open-source standard within the academic community, we have encapsulated the netlist, allowing for straightforward replacements within the TB.
The TB is provided in an easily interpretable textual format.
The second, third, and fourth lines correspond to P, V, and T combinations.
The sixth line defines the parameters that require optimization within the circuit, including MOSFET size, capacitors, resistors, and input bias current.
For MOSFETs, the naming convention follows a structured format using underscores to separate different attributes.
MOSFET_23_1_L_gm3_NMOS:

contexts.context: The provided excerpt describes AnalogGym as an open-source testing suite for analog circuit synthesis intended to enable fair, comprehensive evaluations of ML-based synthesis methods. AnalogGym contains 30 circuit topologies organized into five categories: sensing front ends, voltage references, low dropout regulators, amplifiers, and phase-locked loops. It targets both academic and commercial use by supporting several technology nodes and is compatible with Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, and the open-source Ngspice simulator. The suite emphasizes reproducibility by publishing open datasets and detailed benchmark specifications, standardizing the assessment of ML algorithms in analog circuit synthesis. AnalogGym is publicly available on a GitHub repository and has online documentation. The excerpt does not include specifics about other datasets named in the question (EDALearn benchmark, AICircuit, or additional analog circuit synthesis datasets), such as exact numbers of circuits per dataset, metric lists, licenses, or precise availability details for those datasets.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the EDALearn benchmark as a comprehensive RTL-to-signoff EDA benchmark suite focused on enabling ML research across EDA tasks. EDALearn targets two technology nodes: FreePDK 45nm and ASAP 7nm, and supports tasks including routability prediction, IR drop prediction, and cross-stage data analysis. The suite aggregates practical VLSI instances from multiple public sources (IWLS, BOOM/Chipyard, OpenCore, ISCAS, Gaisler, Faraday, ITC’99) and applies a range of synthesis, placement, and CTS-stage variations (referenced in Table II, IV–VI). It provides both image-like and vector-based feature representations and refers to Table III for design-instance statistics. The excerpt documents the benchmark scope, node choices, sources of designs, and that per-stage/option variations and multiple feature modalities are included. The excerpt does not provide explicit counts of circuits, concrete PPA/ML metrics, software license text, or dataset availability/URLs beyond the paper citation; those specific details (number of circuits, metrics reported, license, and download/access information) are not present in the provided pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AICircuit: a multi-level analog/RF dataset and benchmark built to train ML models for circuit design. AICircuit contains seven core homogeneous circuit types (common-source voltage amplifier, two-stage voltage amplifier, cascode voltage amplifier, low-noise amplifier, power amplifier, voltage-controlled oscillator, and mixer) and also includes heterogeneous systems composed of multiple cascaded blocks. Data were generated by designing schematics in Cadence, identifying key parameters, sweeping each parameter over a specified range with fine steps, running Cadence simulations, and storing each parameter set and its simulated performance as one dataset row. Performance metrics include DC power consumption, bandwidth, voltage gain, and other (including “advanced”) metrics reported from a commercial simulator. The dataset is split into train and test sets for ML evaluation. The paper also benchmarks a range of ML models (from random forests to neural networks) on these data. The excerpt does not specify dataset size, per-circuit sample counts, exact metric definitions/formats, license, or public download URL for the dataset; those details are not present and would need to be obtained from the paper’s appendix, repository, or authors.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGym as an open-source testing suite for analog circuit synthesis that provides standardized, reproducible benchmarks. It includes 30 circuit topologies grouped into five categories: sensing front ends, voltage references, low dropout regulators, amplifiers, and phase-locked loops. The suite supports several technology nodes for academic and commercial applications and is compatible with Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, and Ngspice. AnalogGym emphasizes comprehensive evaluation: benchmark specifications cover large-signal and small-signal analyses, PVT (process, voltage, temperature) variations, and require simulations including DC, AC, pole-zero (PZ), and transient analyses. The project provides open datasets and benchmark specifications to promote fair comparisons, and the code/data are available on GitHub (https://github.com/CODA-Team/AnalogGym) with documentation hosted by the project. The excerpt does not state the licensing terms, nor does it enumerate an exact total number of circuit instances beyond the 30 topologies; detailed lists of evaluation metrics and explicit license information are not included in the shown pages.
contexts.question: What are the details of AnalogGym analog circuit synthesis benchmark including circuit topologies, evaluation metrics, license, availability, and number of circuits?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGym’s open testbench: simulations use the open-source Ngspice simulator with the sky130 PDK for AMP and LDO circuits. Some analog blocks (voltage references, temperature sensing front ends) are not supported with open PDKs because precise subthreshold modeling is needed; PLLs likewise require detailed analog/noise models typically absent from open PDKs. The benchmark frames analog circuit synthesis as a multi-objective optimization problem F(x,P,V,T) over design parameters x (transistor dims, resistances, capacitances, bias currents, ...), optimizing metrics such as gain, bandwidth, power consumption, noise, and area while satisfying constraints across PVT corners. The paper gives an active-area computation that sums transistor W×L×multiplicity×parallel plus capacitor and resistor contributions (capacitors scaled by 1089 per pF, resistors by 5 per kΩ) and then takes the square root. Process corners (SS, FF, SF, FS, TT), voltage points (max 1.32V, min 1.08V, nominal 1.2V), and temperatures (125°C, -40°C, 25°C) are explicitly considered. The excerpt does not state the benchmark license, availability location, or the total number of circuits included.

The formula for calculating the active area is as follows:

In this formula, 𝑊𝑖is the width of the 𝑖-th transistor, 𝐿𝑖is the length of the 𝑖-th transistor, 𝑀𝑖is the multiplicity of the 𝑖-th transistor, and 𝑃𝑖is the number of parallel transistors for the 𝑖-th transistor.

This formula accounts for active area contributions from all transistors by multiplying their width, length, multiplicity, and parallel count, and includes contributions from capacitors and resistors, scaled by constants derived from real-world technology (1089 for pF and 5 for kΩ) before taking the square root of the total sum.
Process corner modeling captures the variability in the parameters of N-MOSFET (NMOS) and P-MOSFET (PMOS)
The five commonly used process cor- ners are slow NMOS+slow PMOS (SS), fast NMOS+fast PMOS (FF), slow NMOS+fast PMOS (SF), fast NMOS+slow PMOS (FS), and typical NMOS+typical PMOS (TT).
typical voltage conditions include maximum voltage (1.32V), minimum voltage (1.08V), and nominal voltage (1.2V).

contexts.context: The excerpt states that AnalogGym is an open and practical testing suite comprising "30 circuit topologies in five key categories: sensing front ends, voltage references, AMPs, low dropout regulators (LDOs), and phase-locked loops (PLLs)." contexts.context: The PCBSchemaGen benchmark comprises an open-sourced KG and benchmark (available at the project's GitHub) and evaluates LLM-driven schematic generation across 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains. The framework is training-free: an LLM proposes SKiDL-style Python code and a datasheet-driven knowledge graph plus a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) verifier enforces pin-role and topological constraints. Reported evaluation focuses on design accuracy and computational efficiency, with results claiming significant improvements in those metrics. The paper stresses gaps in PCB datasets and verification: PCB schematic designs are scarce for large scale training, schematic capture is vendor-specific, and there is no established verification tool for PCB schematics. Prior analog verification via SPICE is noted as inadequate because "SPICE is designed for idealized functional simulation, not for validating PCB schematic correctness under real IC package and pin-level constraints." These limitations motivate the KG+SI verifier and the choice to release the benchmark and KG as open-source to help address dataset scarcity and evaluation reproducibility.
contexts.question: What are the details of PCBSchemaGen benchmark for PCB schematic generation by LLMs? What datasets are used, what metrics, and what gaps exist in PCB dataset availability?

contexts.context: PCBSchemaGen introduces a first benchmark for evaluating LLM-driven PCB schematic generation consisting of 23 unique circuits covering digital, analog, and power domains; each task is tied to real-world IC components and packages. The system demonstrates end-to-end capability: from natural-language specification to KiCAD PCB-layout–ready output, and the benchmark is presented as reproducible and practical for future work. The authors also build a domain knowledge graph from open-source IC datasheets encoding pin-role semantics, constraint types, and topological constraints to drive verification and provide interpretable feedback. The excerpt does not enumerate explicit numeric metrics or detailed evaluation protocols; it implies evaluation focuses on end-to-end generation success (e.g., KiCAD-ready outputs) and verifier-driven code-synthesis success but gives no formal metric list. Important dataset gaps are highlighted: PCB schematic data are scarce, schematic capture formats are vendor-specific (hindering large-scale training), and prior work largely omits board-level packaging and pin-constraint modeling—limiting opportunities for fine-tuning LLMs on PCB schematic tasks.
contexts.question: What are the details of PCBSchemaGen benchmark for PCB schematic generation by LLMs? What datasets are used, what metrics, and what gaps exist in PCB dataset availability?

contexts.context: The PCBSchemaGen benchmark is a 23-task PCB schematic-generation benchmark (Table 1 lists tasks ID 1–23) covering sensing, auxiliary power, power stages, drivers, DC-DC and DC-AC designs (examples include resistor dividers for HV-to-LV ADC sensing, LDO-based low-voltage supplies, and synchronous buck converters). The evaluation focuses on LLM capabilities for pin-function understanding, knowledge-graph and topology constraints, topology generation, and mixed analog/digital/power synthesis. Evaluation metrics: Pass@k (from Chen et al. 2021) is used with k = 1 and 5, n = 15 trials, and each trial allows up to three self-correction retry attempts; the paper gives the Pass@k formula and sets model temperature = 0.5. Operational cost/runtime reported: ~23k tokens per task, ~$0.07, ~2.4 minutes; human expert ~1.5 hours. Models evaluated include multiple open- and closed-source LLMs and fine-tuned variants. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss broader PCB dataset sources or availability; it does note that open-source models underperform on complex designs (e.g., >20 devices and >70 pins), implying challenges on large/complex PCB tasks but not detailing dataset gaps or public dataset availability.
contexts.question: What are the details of PCBSchemaGen benchmark for PCB schematic generation by LLMs? What datasets are used, what metrics, and what gaps exist in PCB dataset availability?

We use most common Pass@k metrics for code generation evaluation (Chen et al., 2021).
The Pass@k measures the probability of achieving at least one success within k independent trials, where each trial allows for a maximum of three self-correction retry attempts.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes methodological elements of PCBSchemaGen relevant to benchmarking LLM-generated PCB schematics, though it does not list specific dataset names or standard numeric metrics. The authors build a domain-specific Knowledge Graph (KG) from real-world IC datasheets to encode pin roles, electrical properties, topological constraints, and isolation boundaries, explicitly to avoid uploading full datasheets to LLMs. This KG reduces per-component token footprint from ~16k tokens (full datasheet) to ~300 tokens, a 30–70× reduction. The KG is formally defined as KG = (C, R, A, Φ, I) and is used both for constrained generation and verification. For evaluation, they employ a multi-stage verification pipeline: Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in Electrical Rule Checking (ERC), inter- and intra-connection checks, and subgraph isomorphism/topology verification (Algorithm 1: BuildBipartiteGraph, BuildTopologyGraph, rule evaluation). Prompt engineering techniques (ICL, Chain-of-Thought) and SKiDL as the code medium are emphasized to improve LLM understanding and executability. The excerpt implies gaps in raw PCB dataset availability (motivating the KG and token-reduction approach) but does not enumerate benchmark datasets, quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, BLEU, or F1), or dataset scale—those details are absent from this excerpt.
contexts.question: What are the details of PCBSchemaGen benchmark for PCB schematic generation by LLMs? What datasets are used, what metrics, and what gaps exist in PCB dataset availability?

contexts.context: The authors built a custom benchmark and component library for PCB schematic generation using real-world IC components and datasheets. The benchmark contains 23 unique tasks spanning analog, digital, power, and mixed circuits; task difficulty is measured by circuit nodes and net counts, ranging from 4 component pins to over 100. They injected lookalike components (same package and similar pin counts but different functionality, e.g., voltage regulator vs. op-amp) to test LLMs' ability to choose correct parts. Verification and metrics emphasize topology- and rule-based checks: subgraph isomorphism (VF2) for topology verification, semantic constraint checks, and error reporting. They provide three feedback/error-reporting modalities—detailed and specific error messages (syntax, ERC, subgraph verification with precise pin/net/value information), concise error-only messages, and binary pass/fail—to evaluate LLM performance and guide refinement. The framework's correctness was validated via human expert evaluation, and ablation studies show subcircuit retrieval improves success rates. Full benchmark details and component-library construction are deferred to the Appendix.
contexts.question: What are the details of PCBSchemaGen benchmark for PCB schematic generation by LLMs? What datasets are used, what metrics, and what gaps exist in PCB dataset availability?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen’s authored KiCad component library and evaluation approach. The provided library includes a small, curated dataset of components: 18 symbols, 21 footprints, and 14 reference datasheets (organized for KiCad 9 / SKiDL). PCBSchemaGen emits executable SKiDL (Python) artifacts (netlists, SVGs, initialized PCB files) enabling downstream EDA integration. In benchmarking, the paper performs a qualitative comparison across dimensions such as core task, output executability, scale, error correction, and constraints — claiming end-to-end system generation capability for system-scale PCBs (>100 pins) with closed-loop verification and knowledge-graph-driven physical constraints and subgraph-isomorphism checks. It contrasts against CircuitLM (which outputs CircuitJSON, is prone to open-loop hallucination and lacks datasheet-grounded verification) and GNN baselines (which degrade beyond ~50 nodes and lack pin-role semantics). The excerpt references Pass@k metrics but the detailed metric definitions and dataset split information are truncated. Major dataset gaps implied: lack of large, standardized, datasheet-linked PCB schematic corpora for system-scale (>100-pin) designs, inconsistent intermediate formats (CircuitJSON vs executable SKiDL), and missing ground-truth pin-to-datasheet mappings needed for reliable physical verification.
contexts.question: What are the details of PCBSchemaGen benchmark for PCB schematic generation by LLMs? What datasets are used, what metrics, and what gaps exist in PCB dataset availability?

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights several gaps relevant to evaluating generative circuit (and by extension PCB) design. Key issues include a limited availability of open datasets and an absence of standardized data representations and schemas, which impede interoperability, comparability, and reproducibility across ML-based EDA research. Closed-source workflows and proprietary PDKs further restrict reproducibility and public accessibility. Existing efforts are limited in scope: METRICS2.1 provides only aggregate RTL-to-GDSII metrics rather than stage-wise or structural data, CircuitNet relies on closed tools, and CircuitOps—though open—omits pre-route representations and image modalities. Additional challenges cited are variations across technology nodes, time-consuming data preparation, inconsistent preprocessing, and the lack of a unified benchmarking framework. The paper argues for a standardized, stage-aware, multimodal schema and openly shared datasets to enable reproducible, comparable ML benchmarks across stages (from synthesis through routing) and modalities (graphs and images). The excerpt does not explicitly discuss data contamination or benchmark saturation, focusing instead on dataset availability, standardization, and reproducibility challenges.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights several gaps and challenges directly relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. Data availability and openness are limited: "EDA and design data are limited and often not in the open domain," which constrains creation of standardized PCB datasets. Data quality issues are called out explicitly: "Garbage-in, garbage-out issues in AI training data often limit the effectiveness of predictive models," implying risks of contaminated or low-quality corpora undermining evaluation. Model generalization is a showstopper: "AI models often fail to adapt to new designs or nodes," complicating benchmark validity across domains. The text also notes practical barriers to reproducible evaluation: lack of standardized datasets/benchmarks and tool integration, urging "Open-source initiatives for developing shared datasets, tools, and benchmarks" and to "Develop public benchmarks and datasets ... to enable reproducibility and innovation." Additional constraints impacting evaluation include "DRC complexity" (many negotiable rules), "Computational resource demands" limiting who can run evaluations, and "Integration challenges" that prevent end-to-end, comparable pipelines. Together these points identify dataset scarcity/openness, data quality/contamination risks, reproducibility and benchmarking needs, and resource/integration limits as core evaluation gaps.

Integration challenges: Lack of seamless integration across EDA tools hinders the realization of end-to-end AI-driven workflows.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights key gaps and challenges relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. Public datasets for EDA are scarce because producing them requires many full EDA flow runs that can take weeks to months and deep domain knowledge, making data generation extremely expensive. NDAs in manufacturing technology and EDA tools further prevent releasing original data, forcing most studies to rely on small internal datasets and hindering benchmarking and reproducibility. Existing benchmarks are often isolated and task-specific (e.g., placement or routing) and lack intermediate EDA-flow data needed for cross-stage ML tasks. Current public datasets fall short in representing realistic variation in design types, scales, manufacturing technology nodes, and ML task diversity, which can bias model evaluation and limit generalization. The paper positions CircuitNet 2.0 as an attempt to mitigate these issues by providing a larger, multi-modal dataset covering million-gate designs, an advanced 14nm FinFET node, and additional tasks (e.g., timing) to support broader, more realistic evaluation and transfer learning across PDKs and designs.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

snippets: Although ML has been introduced into EDA, there are very few public datasets available to implement and evaluate ML algorithms, compared with other fields like computer vision, natural language processing, speech recognition, and recommendation systems.

an ML for EDA dataset needs to be generated by numerous runs of EDA flows with domain knowledge in chip design and each run can take weeks to months, making it extremely expensive for data generation.
Furthermore, the Non-disclosure Agreement (NDA) in manufacturing technology and EDA tools also prohibits the release of the original data.
As a result, most studies can only generate small internal datasets for validation of ML techniques, making benchmarking and reproducing results extremely challenging.
most of these benchmarks only contain isolated data used for specific tasks, usually placement or routing, which cannot be directly used for ML tasks and constraints further cross-stage innovation.
However, the design types, design scales, manufacturing technology, and supported ML tasks are still far from representing the realistic chip design environment.
The EDA community has been expecting a large-scale and effective dataset like ImageNet

CircuitNet 2.0 goes beyond the original CircuitNet in three perspectives: 1) Providing data for million-gate designs like CPU, GPU, and AI chips to increase circuit types and scales; 2) Generating data with advanced 14nm FinFET technology node to increase the manufacturing and modeling complexity; 3) Adding an additional timing prediction task for circuit performance to increase task diversity and practicality.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights several gaps relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. Data scarcity and access barriers are primary issues: high-quality labeled datasets are scarce, proprietary, sensitive, and costly to produce, limiting training and evaluation. Existing datasets and efforts (HLsyn: 80,000 HLS programs; Chrysalis: ~1,500 annotated designs; ICCAD 2024 benchmark contest) help but are still far from "ImageNet-scale", so standardized, large open PCB/circuit datasets remain lacking. Toolchain drift and domain shift are critical evaluation challenges: models can fail when the target toolchain or design domain changes, requiring transfer-learning or adaptation methods. Trust, reliability, and verification are also major gaps — establishing comprehensive validation/verification processes is resource-intensive but necessary for industry adoption. Together these factors impede reproducibility and robust benchmarking: proprietary data and varying toolchains make experiments hard to reproduce, and limited benchmark scale risks overfitting or saturation of metrics. The report implies more coordinated dataset collection, standardized benchmarks, and methods for toolchain- and domain-robust evaluation are needed.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

Legal and commercial restrictions further impede sharing: "the Non-disclosure Agreement (NDA) in manufacturing technology and EDA tools also prohibits the release of the original data." As a result, "most studies can only generate small internal datasets," which makes "benchmarking and reproducing results extremely challenging." Existing benchmarks and suites often provide only isolated data for narrow tasks (e.g., placement or routing) and "cannot be directly used for ML tasks" or cross-stage innovation; they also lack intermediate flow dumps. Even efforts to expand public resources (e.g., CircuitNet) still leave gaps in representing realistic design diversity: "the design types, design scales, manufacturing technology, and supported ML tasks are still far from representing the realistic chip design environment."
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights several evaluation-relevant gaps for applying LLMs to EDA that relate to generative circuit/PCB design. Key concerns include data privacy and proprietary data leakage: models trained on large datasets may inadvertently expose confidential designs, and the authors explicitly note the need for techniques that let models learn from proprietary data without risk of leakage. There is a lack of EDA-specific research addressing these issues (“no public work directly relating such concerns to using LLMs for EDA”), indicating a gap in domain-tailored evaluation protocols. Interpretability is another major gap: the “black box” nature of LLMs hampers understanding of design rationales, which is critical for debugging, verification, validation, and regulatory compliance. Practical evaluation is also affected by computational and energy costs; iterative design workflows with LLMs incur nontrivial inference energy, motivating hardware and model-efficiency solutions that impact reproducibility and the feasibility of large-scale benchmarks. The authors suggest work to demonstrate/exploit extraction risks and adapt defenses. The excerpt does not explicitly mention standardized open PCB datasets, benchmark saturation, or granular reproducibility protocols, so those topics are not directly supported here.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights major evaluation challenges for ML-driven circuit design centered on data and benchmarking. A key gap is the lack of a generic, diverse, high-quality dataset and robust metrics needed to thoroughly evaluate and improve ML algorithms in analog and RF circuit design. Existing ML methods are predominantly validated on simple, homogeneous circuits (e.g., two-stage amplifiers), leaving it unclear how to scale neural-network-based approaches to heterogeneous, real-world systems with different components. The schematic-level design process remains highly time-consuming and dependent on human experts, complicating reproducible large-scale evaluation. The authors argue that datasets encompassing complex circuits and multi-block systems are indispensable but currently undeveloped and inaccessible, motivating the creation of multi-level datasets and benchmarks (such as the proposed AICircuit) to advance evaluation, generalization testing, and more systematic comparison of ML methods.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several key gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design. First, there is a scarcity of open-source PCB schematic data and a near absence of suitable simulation-based verification, which limits training and evaluation: "Automated PCB schematic design remains unexplored due to the scarcity of open-source data and the absence of simulation-based verification." Second, PCB schematic work remains largely manual and iterative, demanding long validation cycles and expert knowledge: "the traditional PCB schematic design workflow is heavily dependent on manual effort, often requiring months or even years of iteration to validate a usable design." Third, common analog simulators like SPICE are insufficient for PCB-level correctness because they model idealized components and do not validate real IC package and pin-level constraints: "SPICE is designed for idealized functional simulation, not for vali- dating PCB schematic correctness under real IC package and pin-level constraints." Additional hurdles include vendor-specific schematic capture formats that impede large-scale dataset creation and generalization: "Since PCB schematic capture is vendor specific, this makes large scale training difficult." The paper also emphasizes the need for interpretable, rule-based verification to improve reproducibility and reliability in evaluation workflows.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

contexts.context: The excerpt analyzes evaluation challenges for LLM-driven hardware (Verilog) code generation, focusing on data contamination where benchmark examples leak into pre-training/fine-tuning corpora. It highlights the undisclosed nature of pre-training datasets (even for open-source models) and the relative sparsity of domain-specific hardware data, which raises the likelihood that benchmarks overlap with training data and thereby skew results. The authors detect near-100% contamination rates for some models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o) across VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks and use detection methods (e.g., CCD, Min-K% Prob, Dolos) to assess similarity. They frame contamination as undermining fair ranking, benchmarking validity, and industrial adoption, and they explore mitigation trade-offs between code quality and fairness, aiming for contamination-free evaluation. The work is specific to Verilog/hardware LLMs and does not address PCB datasets explicitly, but the problems identified—undisclosed pretraining data, dataset sparsity, benchmark overlap/contamination, need for standardized/clean benchmarks, and trade-offs when mitigating contamination—are directly relevant to evaluating generative circuit/PCB design systems.
contexts.question: What are the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design? Lack of standardized open PCB datasets, reproducibility concerns, data contamination, benchmark saturation issues.

contexts.context: The AICircuit dataset (Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design) is presented as a multi-level benchmark for AI-driven analog IC design. The excerpt describes evaluations on homogeneous circuit blocks including two-stage voltage amplifier (TSVA), Mixer, voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO), and a power amplifier (PA); it also references noise figure in a low-noise amplifier (LNA) and phase noise in a VCO as advanced metrics included. Models trained include transformer and MLP (also SVR and RF compared); neural nets were trained for 100 epochs with the Adam optimizer at learning rate 0.001. The excerpt does not state the total number of circuits/topologies included in the full dataset nor the dataset license; it notes additional circuit results are deferred to Appendix D.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AICircuit, a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ML-assisted analog and RF circuit design. The dataset includes seven core homogeneous circuit blocks: common-source voltage amplifier (CSVA), two-stage voltage amplifier (TSVA), cascode voltage amplifier (CVA), low-noise amplifier (LNA), power amplifier (PA), voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO), and mixer. In addition, AICircuit contains complex heterogeneous systems composed of multiple cascaded circuit components. Data were generated by creating Cadence schematics, identifying key parameters, setting [beg, end] ranges, sweeping each parameter with small step sizes, running an accurate commercial simulator to obtain performance metrics, and saving each parameter set and its performance as a row. The dataset is split into train and test sets. The collection contains circuit parameters and simulated performance metrics (including advanced metrics) and lists typical performance targets such as DC power consumption, bandwidth, and voltage gain. The work evaluates a range of ML models—from conventional methods like random forest and decision trees to neural networks such as MLPs and transformers—on the benchmark. The excerpt does not state the dataset license or exact metric definitions/formats.
contexts.question: What are the details of AICircuit dataset for analog circuit design? How many circuits, what topologies, what evaluation metrics, what license?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AICircuit as a multi-level dataset and benchmark for AI-driven analog and radio-frequency circuit design. The dataset “comprises seven commonly used basic circuits and two complex wireless transceiver systems composed of multiple circuit blocks,” covering both simple building blocks and heterogeneous system-level topologies. It is intended to support ML workflows that learn the mapping from design specifications (examples given: power consumption, bandwidth) to circuit parameters and to facilitate circuit sizing and topology decisions. The authors report that they “extensively evaluate various ML algorithms on the dataset,” showing ML’s potential for predicting parameters from specifications. The data and codebase are open-sourced with a GitHub link provided. The excerpt does not list the specific basic-circuit types/topology names, the concrete evaluation metrics or protocols used for benchmarking, nor does it state the dataset license — those details are not present in the provided pages and would need to be retrieved from the paper’s later sections or the linked GitHub repository.

we extensively evaluate various ML algorithms on the dataset, revealing the potential of ML algorithms in learning the mapping from the design specifications to the desired circuit parameters.

contexts.context: AICircuit is presented as a multi-level benchmark dataset for analog and radio-frequency (RF) circuit design that includes both homogeneous circuits (one or multiple circuits with identical functions) and heterogeneous circuits (circuits with different functions). The paper evaluates ML models (MLPs, Transformers, SVRs) on example heterogeneous systems: a transmitter (noted components: VCO and PA) and a receiver (LNA, mixer, cascode voltage amplifier). Evaluations report both system-level and component-level metrics; example metrics shown include Power Consumption, Voltage Gain, Noise Figure for the receiver and Large Signal Power Gain, Drain Efficiency, PAE for the PA in the transmitter. The authors note that receiver circuits are less complex and easier to predict, while transmitter-level relationships become more non-linear and harder to model. They also show that models trained on an isolated circuit (e.g., PA alone) generalize poorly inside a larger heterogeneous system due to load effects. The excerpt does not state how many circuits are in the dataset, the full list of topologies covered, dataset size, evaluation protocol details, or the dataset license.

snippets: Aicircuit: a multi-level dataset and benchmark for ai-driven analog integrated circuit design.
In this work, we propose a multi-level benchmark dataset for analog and radio-frequency circuit design.
The proposed dataset, AICircuit, covers homogeneous and heterogeneous circuits.
Homogeneous circuits comprise one or multiple circuits with identical functions, while heterogeneous circuits comprise circuits with different functions.
Receiver. We further evaluate ML models on a receiver system comprising an LNA, a mixer, and a cascode voltage amplifier.
Transmitter (VCO and PA).
Power Consumption                Voltage Gain                  Noise Figure
Large Signal Power Gain             Drain Efficiency                 PAE
We evaluate various machine learning algorithms on the benchmark datasets, including multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), transformers, and support vector regression (SVRs).
From a receiver to a transmitter, the relationship between circuit parameters and the performance becomes more non-linear.
Heterogeneous circuits investigated in the paper only cover typical radio-frequency transmitter and receiver systems.

contexts.context: The excerpt surveys several public datasets and benchmark suites relevant to ML for physical design. It notes a scarcity of public data due to NDA and generation cost, and highlights prior open resources: CircuitNet (over 10,000 samples) supporting routability and IR-drop prediction, and the new CircuitNet 2.0 which extends CircuitNet by including million-gate designs (CPU/GPU/AI chips), using a 14nm FinFET PDK, and adding timing prediction alongside routability and IR-drop to support multi-model tasks. Standard EDA contest benchmarks from ISPD and ICCAD are mentioned as providers of pre-processed task-specific data. Other open benchmarks discussed include OPDB (a scalable, extensible design benchmark), the EPFL combinational benchmark suite (logic optimization benchmarks), and BeGAN (GAN-based synthetic PDN benchmark generation focused on IR-drop). The excerpt also references ML research directions enabled by datasets (e.g., transfer learning across PDKs, handling imbalanced samples) and emphasizes that many existing benchmarks are task-specific and often lack intermediate EDA flow dumps needed for cross-stage ML innovations.

snippets: Although ML has been introduced into EDA, there are very few public datasets available to implement and evaluate ML algorithms, compared with other fields like computer vision, natural language processing, speech recognition, and recommendation systems.

Previous efforts like CircuitNet (Chai et al., 2022; 2023) try to alleviate such situation by releasing an open-source dataset containing over 10,000 samples and supports ML tasks in EDA, e.g., routability and IR-drop prediction (Ren & Hu, 2022).
CircuitNet 2.0 ... contains data to support multi-model prediction tasks in advanced technology nodes, like timing, routability, and IR-drop, covering more design objectives compared with CircuitNet.

CircuitNet 2.0 goes beyond the original CircuitNet in three perspectives: 1) Providing data for million-gate designs like CPU, GPU, and AI chips to increase circuit types and scales; 2) Generating data with advanced 14nm FinFET technology node to increase the manufacturing and modeling complexity; 3) Adding an additional timing prediction task for circuit performance to increase task diversity and practicality.
the well-known conferences, e.g. ISPD and ICCAD, host contests about EDA problems, which provide benchmarks with pre-processed data for the researchers.
OPDB (Tziantzioulis et al., 2021) is a scalable, modular, heterogeneous, and extensible design benchmark, which allows users to generate hardware designs conveniently.
The EPFL combinational benchmark suite (Amarú et al., 2015) provides data to challenge modern logic optimization tools.
BeGAN (Chhabria et al., 2021a) proposed GAN and transfer learning-based methodology for synthetic Power Delivery Network (PDN) benchmark generation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes EDA-Schema-V2, an open, multimodal schema and dataset for ML in digital physical design. Datasets are generated with the OpenROAD tool flow and open PDKs (SkyWater 130 nm, Nangate 45 nm, IHP SG13G2 130 nm, ASAP 7 nm) and are based on the IWLS’05 benchmark suite. The release contains approximately 7,800 design instances across 18 benchmark circuits with stage-resolved representations from synthesis through detailed routing, providing multimodal data (circuit graphs, spatial layout images, and QoR metrics). The dataset scale is large: over 275 million gates, 75 million nets, and more than 36 million extracted timing paths. The work identifies twelve representative prediction tasks spanning timing, power, area, and routing, and provides baseline analyses and standardized metrics; the formatted dataset and schema code are released on GitHub. Related prior efforts noted include METRICS2.1, CircuitNet, and CircuitOps, with discussion of their scope and limitations (e.g., closed-source PDKs or limited stage/modal coverage). The excerpt does not, however, include a survey or detailed information specifically on "circuit foundation models" or "large circuit models."

contexts.context: The excerpt documents the EDA-Schema-V2 multimodal schema and the accompanying large-scale dataset and benchmarks for ML in digital physical design. The dataset was generated with the OpenROAD tool chain using four open PDKs (SkyWater 130 nm, Nangate 45 nm, ASAP7 7 nm, IHP SG13G2 130 nm) and is derived from IWLS’05 benchmark circuits via systematic parameter sweeps. It comprises roughly 7,800 physical design instances from 18 benchmark circuits, covering >275 million gates, 75 million nets, and >36 million extracted timing paths. The schema captures stage-resolved RTL-to-GDSII artifacts in an entity–relationship structure; representations include graph models (logical/physical connectivity), spatial layout images, and heatmaps of analyzed properties. The paper provides baseline analyses and metrics (referenced in Table 8) to quantify early-stage QoR prediction accuracy across stages and PDKs, positioning Table 8 as a reference leaderboard. The specification, dataset, and tooling are publicly available at the cited GitHub repository. snippets: This paper presents EDA-Schema-V2, a standardized multimodal schema for machine-learning applications in digital physical design.
The schema organizes artifacts generated throughout the RTL-to-GDSII design flow within a unified entity–relationship structure that captures both structural and spatial circuit characteristics.

Graph representations model logical and physical connectivity among design elements, while image-based representations capture spatial layout characteristics and analyzed circuit properties are provided as heatmaps.
A large-scale dataset is generated using the OpenROAD tool chain together with the four open process design kits (SkyWater 130 nm, Nangate 45 nm, ASAP7 7 nm, and IHP SG13G2 130 nm).
The dataset is generated from IWLS’05 benchmark circuits through systematic sweeps of key physical design parameters.

The resulting dataset contains approximately 7,800 physical design instances derived from 18 benchmark circuits and spans more than 275 million gates, 75 million nets, and over 36 million extracted timing paths.

Stage-resolved information across the physical design flow from synthesis through detailed routing is captured together with multimodal representations that include circuit graphs, spatial layout images, and quality-of-results (QoR) metrics.

A baseline analysis is provided to quantify the accuracy of predicted final post-routing QoR metrics during execution of intermediate stages of the physical design flow and across different process design kits (PDKs).
Table 8 serves as a reference benchmark leaderboard for early-stage QoR prediction problems in physical design

To support reproducibility and broader community adoption, the EDA-Schema-V2 specification, dataset, and supporting tooling are publicly released through an open repository available at https://github.com/drexel-ice/EDA-schema.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes datasets, formats, APIs, and benchmark suites created to enable ML research in physical design. Key infrastructure includes CircuitOps (an AI-friendly data format) and numerous OpenROAD Python APIs that speed dataset generation and enable back-annotation and ML-in-the-loop workflows. DATC has produced IR tables (CircuitOps IR Table format) from post-route DEF for multiple open PDK/design sets: eight designs in NanGate45, five in SKY130HD, and four in ASAP7, published in the OpenROAD-flow-scripts and CircuitOps repositories. The 2024 ICCAD Contest Problem C (gate sizing) benchmarks include eight MacroPlacement GitHub designs and two OpenCores designs implemented in ASAP7; these benchmarks are distributed both as standard EDA files (SDC, LIB, LEF, DEF, gate-level Verilog) and as NVIDIA CircuitOps IR Tables. For LLM-based EDA work, the EDA Corpus is introduced as the first open-source LLM-supporting dataset: 943 OpenROAD prompt-script pairs (including 373 prompt→script pairs for flow stages and 570 prompt→script database-query pairs) plus question-answer pairs; OpenROAD-Assistant is an open-source LLM built on this corpus. Repositories and tutorial materials (ASP-DAC24, CircuitOps GitHub, OpenROAD-flow-scripts) are provided to seed community research, though challenges such as fragmented repos and reproducibility remain.

snippets: CircuitOps [23], a standard data format to storedesign data in an AI-friendly way, was developed.
Numerous Python APIs in OpenROAD have been developed, offering faster dataset generation when compared to TCL interfaces [9].

Using these Python APIs, as a part of DATC activities, we have created IR tables using data from post-route DEF for eight designs in NanGate45 [40], five designs in SKY130HD [55], and four designs in ASAP7[10];allofthesedesignsareavailableintheOpenROAD-flow-scripts repository.
The benchmarks are provided in two formats: (1) standard EDA files, which consist of SDC, LIB, LEF, DEF and gate-level netlist Verilog files, and (2) NVIDIA’s CircuitOps [23] IR Table format.
The contest benchmarks [33] include eight designs fromtheMacroPlacementGitHubrepository[57],alongwithtwode-signs from OpenCores [50], all implemented in ASAP7.
The EDA Corpus dataset [32] [39] is the first open-source dataset designed to support large language model (LLM) based research (e.g., chatbot development and script generation) in physical design.

EDA Corpus includes 943 OpenROAD prompt-script data pairs, comprising 373 pairs of prompts and OpenROAD Python scripts corresponding to various stages of the physical design flow, along with 570 pairs of prompts and OpenROAD Python scripts to query information from the OpenROAD database.
The OpenROAD-Assistant project [30] [52] is an open-source LLM built on the EDA Corpus dataset.
The IR tables are made available to boost research on ML EDA applications and seed further data generation by the community.
A detailed overview of all newly developed APIs is available at the ASP-DAC24-Tutorial GitHub repository [34].

contexts.context: The excerpt introduces a survey of foundation AI models for VLSI circuit design and EDA, motivating the trend by noting that circuit-focused AI lags behind NLP/CV and that recent foundation models for EDA show strong generalization, few-shot learning, and generative abilities. It states that these models commonly follow a two-stage paradigm: pre-training on large-scale datasets followed by fine-tuning for specific tasks. The authors highlight a lack of systematic definitions and analyses in the community and present an evolutionary tree (Fig. 2) that categorizes recent works into encoder-based (covered in Section 4) and decoder-based (covered in Section 5) circuit foundation models. Numerous representative model names are shown across 2021–2025 (examples visible in the figure include Design2Vec, FGNN, NetTAG, DeepGate3, LLM-HD, DeepRTL, RTLSquad, ChipGPTV, VerilogEval). The excerpt, however, does not provide specific details about "large circuit models" definitions, EDA-Schema datasets, or explicit open datasets and benchmark suites for ML in physical design — those details are not present on these pages.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt is the introduction to a comprehensive survey proposing “circuit foundation models (CFMs)” — AI models developed via a two-stage paradigm of self-supervised pre-training on large unlabeled circuit data followed by fine-tuning for downstream EDA tasks. The authors categorize CFMs into two primary types: encoder-based models for general circuit representation learning (predictive tasks) and decoder-based models that leverage LLMs for generative tasks. The survey claims to cover over 130 works (with over 90% published in or after 2022) and examines input modalities, model architectures, pre-training strategies, domain adaptation techniques, and downstream applications across nearly all VLSI design stages and objectives (from architecture and HLS/RTL to placement/routing and metrics such as timing, power, area, congestion, IR drop, and functional verification). The excerpt highlights advantages of the paradigm (better generalization, less labeled-data dependence, few-shot/fine-tuning capabilities, and generative power). The excerpt does not list or describe specific open datasets or benchmark suites (such as EDA-Schema or large-circuit dataset names) for ML in physical design; those details likely appear later in the full paper.
contexts.question: What are the details of the survey on circuit foundation models, large circuit models, and EDA-Schema datasets for ML in physical design? What specific open datasets and benchmark suites are discussed?

snippets: This new paradigm brings many advantages: model generalization, less reliance on labeled circuit data, efficient adaptation to new[cs.AR]  tasks, and unprecedented generative capability.
we propose referring to AI models developed with this new paradigm as circuit foundation models (CFMs).
Over 90% of our introduced works were published in or after 2022, indicating that this emerging research trend has attracted wide attention in a short period.
unprecedentedly covering over 130 relevant works.

we propose to categorize all existing circuit foundation models into two primary types: 1) encoder-based methods performing general circuit representation learning for predictive tasks; and 2) decoder-based methods leveraging large language models (LLMs) for generative tasks.
Both paradigms develop the foundation AI model through two stages: self-supervised pre-training and fine-tuning.
Various ML models can be trained to provide early predictions or optimizations for circuits, bypassing time-consuming downstream design and simulation steps.

almost all standard VLSI design stages (e.g., architecture stage, high-level synthesis (HLS) code, register-transfer level (RTL) code, gate-level netlist, post-placement layout, clock tree, and post-routing layout) and all primary circuit design objectives (e.g., timing, power, area, congestion, IR drop, signal integrity, and functionality).
This type of work includes two paradigms, named encoder-based and decoder-based circuit models.

contexts.context: The excerpt from the Fang et al. survey catalogs many recent open datasets and benchmark suites used for ML and large-model work in RTL/Verilog/VHDL code generation and evaluation. Notable open resources called out include RTL-Repo ("A benchmark for evaluating llms on large-scale rtl design projects"), OpenLLM-RTL ("Open dataset and benchmark for llm-aided design rtl generation"), and MG-Verilog ("Multi-grained dataset towards enhanced llm-assisted verilog generation"). The survey also references evaluation frameworks and benchmark efforts such as VHDL-Eval (a framework for evaluating LLMs in VHDL), Revisiting VerilogEval (updated VerilogEval evaluations), and a multimodal Verilog benchmark ("Natural language is not enough: Benchmarking multi-modal generative ai for verilog generation"). In addition, works focused on synthetic data and representation — e.g., CraftRTL (high-quality synthetic data generation for Verilog models) and DeepRTL (a unified representation model bridging Verilog understanding and generation) — are listed. The survey thus aggregates datasets, benchmarks, and tooling efforts that support LLM-assisted hardware code generation, evaluation, and data augmentation for RTL/Verilog/VHDL tasks.

Revisiting verilogeval: Newer llms, in-context learning, and specification-to-rtl tasks.
Craftrtl: High-quality synthetic data generation for verilog code models with correct-by-construction non-textual representations and targeted code repair.
Deeprtl: Bridging verilog understanding and generation with a unified representation model.
Rtlsquad: Multi-agent based interpretable rtl design.
